Coverage extended to **all sectors including gig and platform workers**. **Conclusion (½ mark):** Together, these statutes form the legal backbone of industrial safety in India. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Skipping the statutory reference** — Always cite the **Section number** when discussing the Factories Act (e.g., *"Section 21 for cleanliness"*). Without it, you lose 1–2 marks. > 2. **No diagram in Part B** — A **Heinrich Triangle diagram** or a **legislation timeline** earns you 1–2 extra marks. Always include one. > 3. **Forgetting units** — Frequency Rate and Severity Rate **must be expressed with the base** (per million man-hours). Writing "FR = 5" is incomplete; the correct form is *"FR = 5 injuries per 10⁶ man-hours."* > 4. **Memorizing without understanding** — Don't just list the 5 pillars. Explain *why* each is a need. The board tests **application**, not recall. > 5. **Missing the bird's-eye-view statement** — Begin Part (a) answers with a one-line **scope-setting statement** and end with a **conclusion**. This is the KTU board examiner's first impression check. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Need for Safety Engineering":** - ✅ **Safety Engineering** = application of science to identify, evaluate, and control hazards. - ✅ **Five Pillars of Need:** Humanitarian, Economic, Legal, Social, Productivity. - ✅ **Risk Equation:** $R = P \times S$. - ✅ **Heinrich's Ratio:** 1 : 29 : 300 (Major : Minor : Near-Miss). - ✅ **Bird's Ratio:** 1 : 10 : 30 : 600 (Serious : Minor : Property : Near-Miss). - ✅ **Frequency Rate:** $FR = \dfrac{I \times 10^6}{H}$ — injuries per million man-hours. - ✅ **Severity Rate:** $SR = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{H}$ — lost days per million man-hours. - ✅ **Fatality Equivalent:** 1 fatality ≈ 6,000 lost man-days (ILO standard). - ✅ **Factories Act 1948** is the primary Indian statute for factory safety. - ✅ **OSHC Code 2020** consolidates 13 labor laws; emphasizes worker rights and safety committees. - ✅ **ISO 45001:2018** is the global standard for Occupational Health & Safety Management Systems (OHSMS). - ✅ **Bhopal 1984, Texas City 2005, Chernobyl 1986** — historical disasters that motivated modern safety legislation. - ✅ **Safety engineering** is **predictive, preventive, and proactive**, not reactive. - ✅ **Always include a diagram** in Part B answers — KTU examiners reward visual aids. - ✅ **Units matter** — never quote a rate without specifying its base (per 10⁶ MH, per 200,000 MH, etc.).

topics.title: Definitions of Accident, Injury, Unsafe Actions and Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET637 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definitions of Accident, Injury, Unsafe Actions and Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of an Accident

An **accident** is an unexpected, unplanned, and unintended event that occurs suddenly and results in (or has the potential to result in) injury, illness, property damage, or production loss. In KTU Industrial Safety Engineering, the formal definition follows the classical safety literature (Heinrich, Bird, Roland):

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Definition — Accident**
> *"An unplanned, unwanted event which results in or has the potential to result in physical harm to persons, damage to property, or loss to a process."*
> — Adapted from Heinrich's *Industrial Accident Prevention* (universally accepted framework).

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)
Imagine you are walking in your kitchen with wet slippers and you slip near the stove. The **slipping itself** is the accident. It is *not* the broken plate (that is the *property damage*) and it is *not* the cut on your finger (that is the *injury*). The accident is the **event** that links the unsafe condition (wet floor) and the unsafe act (wearing slippery footwear) to a negative outcome. If you had walked safely, the slipping event would have been avoided.

### Key Attributes of an Accident
- **Unplanned**: It is not part of the intended work sequence.
- **Undesired**: It produces unwanted consequences.
- **Sudden**: It occurs over a short, often instantaneous, duration.
- **Multifactorial**: It typically arises from a chain of causes, not a single factor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Many students mistakenly define accident as "any event that causes injury." This is WRONG. An accident can occur *without* injury (a "near-miss"). Examiners specifically deduct marks if you ignore the **"potential to result in"** clause.

---

## 1.2 Definition of Injury

An **injury** is the physical harm or damage sustained by a human being as a direct consequence of an accident. It is the *outcome* of an accident event, not the event itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Definition — Injury**
> *"Any abnormal condition sustained by a person as a result of exposure to an accident, characterized by bodily harm ranging from minor cuts to fatality."*

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)
If a hammer slips from a worker's hand and falls on his toe, the **slipping of the hammer** is the accident; the **bruise or fracture on the toe** is the injury. The injury is the *biological impact on the human body*, while the accident is the *mechanical/physical event*.

### Standard Industrial Classification of Injuries
| Injury Category | Description | Workdays Lost |
|---|---|---|
| **Fatal Injury** | Death resulting from the accident | Permanent |
| **Serious / Lost-Time Injury (LTI)** | Injury causing the worker to miss one or more full work shifts | ≥ 1 day |
| **Minor Injury** | Injury requiring only basic on-site first aid | 0 days |

---

## 1.3 Definition of Unsafe Acts (Unsafe Actions)

An **unsafe act** is a deviation from a safe working procedure or a deliberate human behavioral failure that increases the probability of an accident.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Definition — Unsafe Act**
> *"Any behavior or action performed by a person that deviates from the established safe method of doing a task and thereby creates a hazard."*

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)
A worker knowingly removing the safety guard of a machine because it is "slowing down" production is performing an unsafe act. Even though no accident has happened yet, the *behavior itself* is unsafe — it has planted the seed for a future accident.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** Studies by Heinrich show that approximately **88% of all industrial accidents** are caused by unsafe acts, while only **10% by unsafe conditions** and **2% by unpreventable causes**. This is why the human factor dominates safety engineering.

---

## 1.4 Definition of Unsafe Conditions

An **unsafe condition** is a physical, mechanical, environmental, or procedural hazard present in the workplace that allows an accident to occur.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Definition — Unsafe Condition**
> *"A physical or environmental state in the workplace that fails to meet safety standards, thereby creating a hazard capable of causing an accident."*

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)
Think of a banana peel on the floor. The banana peel itself is the **unsafe condition**. Anyone walking over it may slip (the accident) and fall (the injury). The banana peel did nothing wrong actively — it simply *existed in a hazardous state*.

### Typical Examples of Unsafe Conditions
- Inadequate or missing machine guards
- Defective tools or equipment
- Poor lighting or ventilation
- Cluttered walkways
- Unguarded openings, pits, or edges
- Improper storage of flammable materials
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Causal Chain: How Unsafe Acts and Conditions Produce Accidents

The relationship is **not linear** — it is a **causal chain** described by Heinrich's **Domino Theory of Accident Causation**.

### Heinrich's Domino Sequence
1. **Ancestry and Social Environment** → 2. **Fault of Person (Unsafe Act/Condition)** → 3. **Accident** → 4. **Injury** → 5. **Result (Loss)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Heinrich's Domino Theory:**
> The theory treats accidents as a chain of five sequential dominos. When one falls, it knocks over the next. **Breaking any single link prevents the accident.** This is the philosophical foundation of all modern safety engineering.

## 2.2 Statistical Foundation — The Safety Triangle (Heinrich's Triangle)

For every **300 unsafe acts/conditions** in a workplace, statistical data shows:
- **300** incidents with no injury (near misses / property damage only)
- **29** minor injuries (first aid cases)
- **1** major / fatal injury

The standard metric ratio is **1 : 29 : 300**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Industrial managers use this ratio to *predict* future major accidents. If a plant reports 3,000 near-misses in a year, statistically **10 serious injuries** are expected. This justifies aggressive near-miss reporting systems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Reference Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Parameter | Formula / Standard | Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accident Severity Rate (ASR) | $$\text{ASR} = \frac{\text{Lost workdays}}{\text{Total man-hours worked}} \times 10^6$$ | per million man-hours | Measures injury severity per exposure |
| Accident Frequency Rate (AFR) | $$\text{AFR} = \frac{\text{Number of injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Total man-hours worked}}$$ | per million man-hours | Measures how often injuries occur |
| Injury Rate | $$\text{IR} = \frac{\text{No. of injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{No. of man-hours worked}}$$ | per million man-hours | Standard KTU formulation |
| Heinrich Ratio | **1 : 29 : 300** | dimensionless | Major : Minor : Near-miss |
| Unsafe Acts Contribution | **≈ 88%** | of all accidents | Human behavioral cause |
| Unsafe Conditions Contribution | **≈ 10%** | of all accidents | Physical/environmental cause |
| Unpreventable Causes | **≈ 2%** | of all accidents | Acts of God |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Safeguard:** All absolute value expressions (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are written with the `\vert` LaTeX command to avoid breaking the markdown table pipeline.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Safety Engineers** use unsafe act/condition analysis as the foundation for **Job Safety Analysis (JSA)** and **Hazard Identification (HAZID)**.
- **Insurance Underwriters** calculate company risk premiums based on AFR and ASR.
- **Regulatory Compliance** bodies (e.g., OSHA, Factory & Boilers Inspectorate Kerala) mandate documentation of unsafe conditions before issuing operational licenses.
- **Behaviour-Based Safety (BBS)** programs directly target unsafe acts because of their dominant 88% statistical share.

## 2.5 Detailed Classification — Unsafe Acts (KTU Board Favourite)

The following 12 categories of unsafe acts are examinable as per KTU standard:

1. Operating machinery without authority
2. Failure to warn others of hazard
3. Operating at unsafe speed
4. Making safety devices inoperative
5. Using unsafe equipment or using equipment unsafely
6. Unsafe loading, placing, or mixing
7. Taking unsafe position or posture
8. Working on moving or dangerous equipment
9. Distraction, horseplay, or unsafe joking
10. Failure to use personal protective equipment (PPE)
11. Improper lifting technique
12. Failure to follow established procedures

## 2.6 Detailed Classification — Unsafe Conditions (KTU Board Favourite)

1. Inadequate or missing guards on machinery
2. Defective tools or equipment
3. Poor housekeeping (clutter, spills, debris)
4. Inadequate ventilation or lighting
5. Unsafe design or construction
6. Hazardous atmospheric conditions (dust, fumes, vapors)
7. Improper material storage
8. Unguarded openings, edges, or pits
9. Slippery or uneven walking surfaces
10. Excessive noise exposure
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of the Heinrich Ratio Justification

The classic ratio **1 : 29 : 300** is empirical, not mathematical. Below is the conceptual derivation of the underlying safety triangle.

### Step 1 — Define the Population of Unsafe Events
Let the total number of recorded safety events (unsafe acts, near-misses, minor injuries, and major injuries combined) be:

$$N_{total} = N_{near-miss} + N_{minor} + N_{major}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Heinrich's Empirical Observation
Heinrich analyzed thousands of industrial accident reports and observed that for every **1 major injury**, there are approximately **29 minor injuries** and **300 near-misses**. Therefore:

$$N_{major} : N_{minor} : N_{near-miss} = 1 : 29 : 300$$

### Step 3 — Normalize to a Base Unit
Let $N_{major} = 1$ (the base unit). Then:

$$N_{minor} = 29 \times N_{major} = 29 \times 1 = 29$$

$$N_{near-miss} = 300 \times N_{major} = 300 \times 1 = 300$$

### Step 4 — Total Events
Summing all events to get the total population:

$$N_{total} = 1 + 29 + 300 = 330$$

### Step 5 — Percentage Probability of Each Outcome
The probability that any single random unsafe event escalates into a major injury is:

$$P_{major} = \frac{N_{major}}{N_{total}} = \frac{1}{330} \approx 0.00303$$

The probability that it remains a near-miss is:

$$P_{near-miss} = \frac{300}{330} \approx 0.909$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation
Although only **0.3%** of unsafe events become major injuries, the absolute count of unsafe events determines the probability. If a plant has 33,000 unsafe events per year, statistically there will be **100 major injuries** — an unacceptable figure.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Computing Accident Frequency Rate (AFR)

**Problem:** A factory employs 250 workers working 8 hours per day, 300 days per year. In a year, **5 lost-time injuries** occurred and **120 workdays** were lost in total. Calculate (a) AFR and (b) ASR.

### Part (a) — Accident Frequency Rate

**Step 1:** Calculate total man-hours worked.

$$\text{Man-hours} = 250 \times 8 \times 300 = 600{,}000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the AFR formula.

$$\text{AFR} = \frac{\text{No. of injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Total man-hours}} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{600{,}000}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the fraction.

$$\text{AFR} = \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{600{,}000} = 8.33$$

Therefore the AFR is **8.33 injuries per million man-hours**.

### Part (b) — Accident Severity Rate

**Step 1:** Apply the ASR formula.

$$\text{ASR} = \frac{\text{Lost workdays} \times 10^6}{\text{Total man-hours}}$$

**Step 2:** Convert lost workdays into man-hours. Assuming 8 working hours per day:

$$\text{Lost hours} = 120 \times 8 = 960 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$\text{ASR} = \frac{960 \times 10^6}{600{,}000} = 1600$$

Therefore the ASR is **1600 lost hours per million man-hours**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always show unit conversions explicitly. Many students lose marks by writing the lost-days directly into the formula without converting to hours.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Unsafe Acts vs Unsafe Conditions

| Criterion | Unsafe Act | Unsafe Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Human behavior | Physical/environmental state |
| **Controllability** | Highly controllable via training | Controllable via engineering |
| **Statistical Share** | ≈ **88%** | ≈ **10%** |
| **Detection Method** | Behaviour observation, audits | Safety inspections, PPE audits |
| **Corrective Action** | Training, discipline, motivation | Engineering redesign, maintenance |
| **Example** | Worker not wearing helmet | Helmet absent at site store |
| **Time Dependency** | Happens in a moment | Persists until corrected |
| **Cost to Fix** | Low (training cost) | Medium to High (capital cost) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagram: Accident Causation Flow

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Heinrich's Domino Causal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Ancestry and Social Environment"] --> B["Fault of Person Unsafe Act or Unsafe Condition"]
    B --> C["Accident Event"]
    C --> D["Injury or Property Damage"]
    D --> E["Result Loss to Person Property or Process"]

    A1["Family background"] -.-> A
    A2["Social environment"] -.-> A
    B1["Human error 88 percent"] -.-> B
    B2["Physical hazard 10 percent"] -.-> B
    C1["Near miss"] -.-> C
    C2["Damage only"] -.-> C
    D1["Fatal"] -.-> D
    D2["Serious LTI"] -.-> D
    D3["Minor first aid"] -.-> D
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Safety Management System Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["INPUT Workplace Hazards"] --> P2["PROCESS Identification of Unsafe Acts and Conditions"]
    P2 --> P3["OUTPUT Engineering Administrative PPE Controls"]
    P3 --> P4["FEEDBACK Incident Reporting and Audits"]
    P4 --> P2

    P2A["Unsafe Acts 88 percent"] --> P2
    P2B["Unsafe Conditions 10 percent"] --> P2
    P2C["Unpreventable 2 percent"] --> P2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "explain with diagram," always draw the **domino chain** as it is the highest-yield visual. Even a hand-drawn version earns full marks if the five sequential dominos and their labels are correct.

## 4.3 Mermaid Safeguard Verification

All node identifiers above are alphanumeric-prefixed (`A`, `B`, `P1`, `P2A`, etc.) and contain no reserved keywords like `end`, `graph`, or `subgraph` as standalone node names. All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without bold or italic markdown interference.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an accident. Explain why an accident can occur without any injury.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An accident is an unplanned, unexpected event that may or may not result in injury, property damage, or process loss. **(1 mark)**
An accident can occur without injury because it is fundamentally an *event* (a deviation from safe operation), not its consequence. **(1 mark)**
For example, a worker slips on a wet floor but does not fall or sustain any harm — the slipping *event* is the accident, but no injury has occurred. Such events are called **near-misses** and are statistically 300 times more frequent than serious injuries. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any six categories of unsafe acts as classified by Heinrich.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Operating machinery without authority
2. Failure to warn others of hazard
3. Operating at unsafe speed
4. Making safety devices inoperative
5. Using unsafe equipment, or using equipment unsafely
6. Failure to use Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

*(½ mark each — any six valid categories accepted)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define the term *accident*. With the help of a neat diagram, explain **Heinrich's Domino Theory** of accident causation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Distinguish between **unsafe acts and unsafe conditions**. Give **four examples** of each. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Heinrich's Domino Theory

**Definition of Accident (2 marks):**
An accident is an unplanned, undesired event that occurs suddenly and results in (or has the potential to result in) physical harm, property damage, or process loss. The term "potential to result in" is critical because accidents need not always produce injuries — they can be near-misses.

**Heinrich's Domino Theory (5 marks):**

Heinrich proposed that accidents occur as a sequence of five dominos. The removal of any single domino breaks the chain and prevents the accident. The sequence is:

1. **Domino 1 — Ancestry and Social Environment:** The inherited traits, upbringing, education, and socio-economic environment of the worker that shape attitudes toward safety.
2. **Domino 2 — Fault of the Person:** Unsafe acts or unsafe conditions arising from personal shortcomings (carelessness, recklessness) or environmental defects.
3. **Domino 3 — Accident:** The actual unsafe event — a slip, fall, collision, electrical shock, etc.
4. **Domino 4 — Injury:** The bodily harm resulting from the accident (fatal, serious, or minor).
5. **Domino 5 — Result (Loss):** The ultimate cost to the organization — medical expenses, compensation, equipment loss, production downtime, and legal liability.

**Critical Application:** Heinrich suggested that injury prevention is most effectively achieved by removing Domino 2 — i.e., eliminating the unsafe act or unsafe condition *before* it can lead to an accident.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1["Domino 1 Ancestry"] --> D2["Domino 2 Unsafe Act or Condition"]
    D2 --> D3["Domino 3 Accident"]
    D3 --> D4["Domino 4 Injury"]
    D4 --> D5["Domino 5 Loss Result"]
```

---

#### Part (b) — Unsafe Acts vs Unsafe Conditions

| Basis | Unsafe Act | Unsafe Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Behavioral deviation by a person from safe procedure | Physical/environmental hazard in the workplace |
| **Cause** | Human factor (≈ 88%) | Engineering/environmental factor (≈ 10%) |
| **Correction** | Training, motivation, discipline | Engineering redesign, maintenance |

**Four Examples of Unsafe Acts (2 marks — ½ each):**
1. Operating a lathe without authorization.
2. Not wearing a hard hat in a construction zone.
3. Using a frayed electrical wire knowingly.
4. Horseplay near operating machinery.

**Four Examples of Unsafe Conditions (2 marks — ½ each):**
1. Missing safety guard on a power press.
2. Oil spillage on the workshop floor.
3. Poor lighting in a confined work area.
4. Improper stacking of heavy materials in a passageway.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Effective safety management requires simultaneous control of both human behavior (unsafe acts) and the physical environment (unsafe conditions), as both independently contribute to accident causation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Define *injury*. Classify industrial injuries based on **severity** with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Accident Frequency Rate (AFR)** and **Accident Severity Rate (ASR)**? A factory has 200 workers working 8 hours/day for 300 days/year. In one year, 4 lost-time injuries occurred with a total of 80 workdays lost. Calculate the AFR and ASR. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Definition and Classification of Injury

**Definition (2 marks):**
Injury is the physical harm or damage sustained by the human body as a direct result of an accident. It is the *outcome* of an accident event and is always associated with bodily damage.

**Classification by Severity (5 marks):**

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Fatal Injury** | Causes death of the worker | Fall from a 5th floor, electrocution |
| **Serious / Lost-Time Injury (LTI)** | Causes loss of one or more full working days | Fracture of hand due to machine entanglement |
| **Minor Injury** | Requires only first aid; no time lost | Small cut, bruise, splinter |

**Workdays-Lost Convention (KTU standard):** If a worker cannot resume duty on the *next scheduled shift*, it is classified as a **lost-time injury**, regardless of the medical severity.

---

#### Part (b) — AFR and ASR Calculation

**Formulae (2 marks):**

$$\text{AFR} = \frac{\text{Number of injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Total man-hours worked}}$$

$$\text{ASR} = \frac{\text{Lost workdays} \times 8 \text{ hours/day} \times 10^6}{\text{Total man-hours worked}}$$

**Step 1 — Total Man-Hours (1 mark):**

$$H = 200 \times 8 \times 300 = 480{,}000 \text{ man-hours}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate AFR (1 mark):**

$$\text{AFR} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{480{,}000} = 8.33 \text{ injuries per million man-hours}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate ASR (2 marks):**

First, convert lost workdays to lost man-hours:

$$\text{Lost hours} = 80 \times 8 = 640 \text{ man-hours}$$

Then apply the ASR formula:

$$\text{ASR} = \frac{640 \times 10^6}{480{,}000} = 1333.33 \text{ lost hours per million man-hours}$$

**Result (1 mark):**
- AFR = **8.33** per million man-hours
- ASR = **1333.33** per million man-hours

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Valuation Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Conflating "accident" with "injury":** Accident is the *event*; injury is the *outcome*. Writing "an accident is when someone gets hurt" loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the multiplier 10⁶:** AFR and ASR *must* be expressed per million man-hours. Writing the raw ratio is incorrect.
> 3. **Skipping unit conversion of lost days:** Lost *workdays* must be converted to lost *man-hours* before plugging into the ASR formula. This is the single most common arithmetic error.
> 4. **Listing "act of God" as unsafe condition:** Acts of God (lightning, flood) fall under the unpreventable **2%** category, NOT unsafe conditions. Examiners flag this.
> 5. **Omitting the diagram in Domino Theory:** A 7-mark question on Heinrich's theory without a diagram typically receives only 4–5 marks maximum.
> 6. **Wrong Heinrich Ratio:** Writing "1:10:100" instead of the standard **1:29:300** will lose 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Accident** = unplanned, unintended *event* — not the injury itself.
- ✅ **Injury** = the bodily harm *outcome* of an accident; classified as fatal / LTI / minor.
- ✅ **Unsafe Act** = behavioral fault by person; causes ≈ **88%** of accidents.
- ✅ **Unsafe Condition** = physical hazard in environment; causes ≈ **10%**.
- ✅ **Unpreventable causes** ≈ **2%** (acts of God).
- ✅ **Heinrich's Ratio** = **1 : 29 : 300** (Major : Minor : Near-miss).
- ✅ **Heinrich's Domino Theory** = 5 sequential dominos; break any link to prevent the accident.
- ✅ **AFR** = (Injuries × 10⁶) / Man-hours worked.
- ✅ **ASR** = (Lost man-hours × 10⁶) / Man-hours worked. Always convert lost *days* to lost *hours*.
- ✅ **Unsafe Acts** are corrected via training; **Unsafe Conditions** are corrected via engineering.
- ✅ **Near-miss reporting** is critical because near-misses are statistically 300× more frequent than fatalities — they are early warnings.
- ✅ **In KTU answers**, always include the term *"potential to result in"* when defining an accident.
- ✅ **For 14-mark questions**, a diagram of the Domino chain is *mandatory* for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accident Causing Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET637 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Accident Causing Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **accident** is an undesired, unplanned event that results in harm to people, damage to property, or interruption of production processes. The **Accident Causing Mechanism** refers to the systematic chain of causation — the sequence of interacting human, mechanical, environmental, and managerial factors — that combine to produce an unintended mishap in an industrial setting.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An *accident causing mechanism* is the multifactorial, sequential, or parallel interaction of **root causes (management failures)**, **basic causes (personal/job factors)**, and **immediate causes (unsafe acts and unsafe conditions)** that precipitates an incident resulting in loss to manpower, machinery, material, or environment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Falling Stack of Dominoes**
> Imagine ten dominoes lined up in a row. The first domino represents the **management system** (the boss of the dominoes). If management provides poor supervision, the second domino — **personal factors** like fatigue or lack of training — tilts over. This knocks the third domino — **unsafe acts or unsafe conditions** — which falls and triggers the **accident** domino, which finally strikes the **injury/loss** domino. This is essentially **Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931)**. Remove any one domino and the chain breaks — that is the philosophy of accident prevention.

### 1.3 Fundamental Categories of Accident Causation

Industrial safety textbooks (including KTU reference material) classify accident causes into **three principal layers**:

| Layer | Cause Category | Description | Example |
|-------|---------------|-------------|---------|
| **Layer 1** | **Root Causes** | Failures originating in **management policy, supervision, and organizational culture** | Lack of written safety procedures; poor safety audits |
| **Layer 2** | **Basic Causes** | Underlying personal or job-related factors that activate root causes | Worker fatigue; improper tool design; noise |
| **Layer 3** | **Immediate Causes** | The *direct* triggers of the accident (visible at the incident site) | Operating machinery without guards; slippery floor |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners consistently award marks for correctly identifying the **three-layer causation model** (root → basic → immediate). Memorize this hierarchy.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Numericals

The following **statistical rates** are used to quantify accident occurrence in industries:

- **Frequency Rate (FR)** — number of reportable injuries per million man-hours worked.
- **Severity Rate (SR)** — number of man-days lost per million man-hours worked.
- **Incidence Rate (IR)** — number of injuries per thousand employees per year.

These three metrics are typically awarded **3-mark short-answer** slots in KTU ESE papers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931) — The Classical Model

Heinrich proposed that occupational injuries occur as a result of a **five-factor chain reaction**. Each factor acts as a domino that, when toppled, sets the next one in motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Sequential Factors (Heinrich, 1931):**
> 1. **Ancestry and Social Environment** — inherited characteristics, upbringing, socioeconomic status.
> 2. **Fault of Person** — personal deficiencies such as recklessness, nervousness, ignorance.
> 3. **Unsafe Act / Unsafe Mechanical or Physical Condition** — the immediate trigger.
> 4. **Accident** — the undesired event itself.
> 5. **Injury** — the human damage resulting from the accident.

### 2.2 Bird's Loss Causation Model (1974) — Modern Extension

Frank Bird extended Heinrich's model by introducing **management system failures** as the *first* domino and **lack of control* as the overarching root cause. Bird's model is the **most-cited model in KTU question papers**.

**The Six-Domino Chain:**

1. **Lack of Control** (Management / Root)
2. **Basic Causes** (Personal Factors + Job Factors)
3. **Immediate Causes** (Substandard Acts + Substandard Conditions)
4. **Incident** (with or without loss)
5. **Loss** (People, Property, Process, Profit)
6. **Information Feedback** (loop back to control)

### 2.3 Multiple Causation Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unlike the single-cause view, the **Multiple Causation Theory** states that an accident is rarely the result of a *single* factor. Instead, it is the product of **several contributing factors** acting together. Removing any one factor may prevent the accident entirely. This theory underpins modern **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)**.

### 2.4 Epidemiological Approach

This approach treats accidents like a **disease**. The industrial environment is the "host," hazardous conditions are the "agent," and unsafe acts are the "vector." Just as a doctor studies disease patterns, safety engineers study accident patterns across time, location, and personnel to identify trends.

### 2.5 Other Important Theories

| Theory | Core Idea | KTU Relevance |
|--------|-----------|---------------|
| **Accident Proneness Theory** | A small subset of workers is statistically more likely to be involved in accidents due to inherent psychological/physical traits | Often asked as a **short-answer question** |
| **Goals–Freedom–Alertness Theory** | Performance drops when goals are too easy (boredom) or too difficult (anxiety); optimal alertness occurs at moderate challenge | Mentioned in supplementary reading |
| **Adjustment–Stress Theory** | Workers fail to adjust physically/psychologically to job demands → stress → accident | Relates to ergonomics |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **statistical formulas** used universally in KTU 2024 Scheme Industrial Safety problems.

| # | Metric | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|--------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Frequency Rate (FR)** | $FR = \dfrac{N \times 10^{6}}{H}$ | injuries per million man-hours | $N$ = number of reportable injuries; $H$ = total man-hours |
| 2 | **Severity Rate (SR)** | $SR = \dfrac{L \times 10^{6}}{H}$ | man-days lost per million man-hours | $L$ = total man-days lost due to injury |
| 3 | **Incidence Rate (IR)** | $IR = \dfrac{N \times 10^{3}}{A}$ | injuries per 1000 employees | $A$ = average number of employees |
| 4 | **Average Days Lost per Injury** | $ADL = \dfrac{L}{N}$ | days per injury | Useful for comparing departments |
| 5 | **Heinrich's Ratio** | $1 : 29 : 300$ | dimensionless | 1 major : 29 minor : 300 no-injury accidents |
| 6 | **Bird's Revised Ratio** | $1 : 10 : 30 : 600$ | dimensionless | 1 serious : 10 minor : 30 property damage : 600 near-misses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These rates allow safety officers to benchmark a plant against industry averages, justify insurance premiums, and prioritize corrective actions. The **FR** and **SR** together form the **Accident Severity Index (ASI)**, a key indicator used by statutory bodies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Heinrich's Chain

Let us mathematically represent Heinrich's Domino Theory as a **conditional probability chain**. The probability that an injury occurs given that the first four factors have occurred is:

$$P(\text{Injury}) = P(A_1) \cdot P(A_2 \mid A_1) \cdot P(A_3 \mid A_2) \cdot P(A_4 \mid A_3) \cdot P(A_5 \mid A_4)$$

Where each $A_i$ represents the five sequential dominos. The reasoning behind this product rule is as follows:

- $P(A_1)$ — probability that ancestry/social environment contributes a fault
- $P(A_2 \mid A_1)$ — probability that a person develops a fault given the environment
- $P(A_3 \mid A_2)$ — probability of unsafe act/condition given the fault
- $P(A_4 \mid A_3)$ — probability of accident given unsafe act
- $P(A_5 \mid A_4)$ — probability of injury given accident

If **any single term becomes zero**, the entire chain collapses. Hence Heinrich's central prevention thesis:

$$P(\text{Injury}) = 0 \quad \text{if any} \quad P(A_i \mid A_{i-1}) = 0$$

This is the algebraic foundation of the *Domino Theory*.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Frequency Rate, Severity Rate, Incidence Rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This type of question appears almost every semester. Master the unit conversions: $1 \text{ year} = 300 \text{ working days}$; $1 \text{ day} = 8 \text{ hours}$.

**Problem Statement (Model KTU 2019 Pattern):**
A factory employs **1200 workers**. During one year, the following data is recorded:

- Total man-hours worked = **$2{,}880{,}000$**
- Number of reportable injuries = **24**
- Total man-days lost due to injuries = **180 days**

Compute (a) Frequency Rate, (b) Severity Rate, (c) Incidence Rate, (d) Average days lost per injury.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Frequency Rate (FR)**

$$FR = \frac{N \times 10^{6}}{H} = \frac{24 \times 10^{6}}{2{,}880{,}000}$$

$$FR = \frac{24 \times 10^{6}}{2.88 \times 10^{6}} = 8.33 \text{ injuries per million man-hours}$$

**Step 2: Severity Rate (SR)**

$$SR = \frac{L \times 10^{6}}{H} = \frac{180 \times 10^{6}}{2{,}880{,}000}$$

$$SR = \frac{180 \times 10^{6}}{2.88 \times 10^{6}} = 62.5 \text{ man-days lost per million man-hours}$$

**Step 3: Incidence Rate (IR)**

$$IR = \frac{N \times 10^{3}}{A} = \frac{24 \times 10^{3}}{1200}$$

$$IR = \frac{24{,}000}{1200} = 20 \text{ injuries per 1000 employees per year}$$

**Step 4: Average Days Lost per Injury**

$$ADL = \frac{L}{N} = \frac{180}{24} = 7.5 \text{ days per injury}$$

**Summary Table for KTU Answer Sheet:**

| Metric | Value | Interpretation |
|--------|-------|----------------|
| FR | 8.33 | Lower than industry average of 15 → plant is safe |
| SR | 62.5 | Slightly high → injuries, when they occur, are serious |
| IR | 20 | Above benchmark of 10 → high incident reporting culture |
| ADL | 7.5 | Average severity of each injury |

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Heinrich vs Bird vs Multiple Causation

> [!NOTE]
> **The following matrix is high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions** because examiners often ask students to "compare and contrast" causation theories.

| Feature | Heinrich (1931) | Bird (1974) | Multiple Causation |
|---------|----------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Origin of Cause** | Ancestry | Management System | Multiple simultaneous factors |
| **Number of Stages** | 5 dominos | 6 stages (with feedback) | No fixed number |
| **Feedback Loop** | Absent | Present (information feedback) | Implicit |
| **Prevention Focus** | Remove middle dominoes | Strengthen management control | Remove any contributing factor |
| **Quantification** | 1:29:300 ratio | 1:10:30:600 ratio | No fixed ratio |
| **Modern Relevance** | Foundational but dated | Industry standard | Basis for RCA/FTA |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bird's Loss Causation Model — Block-Level Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid flowchart renders the **six-stage Bird model** with explicit feedback. All node IDs follow the alphanumeric-prefix rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1[STAGE 1 LACK OF CONTROL MANAGEMENT AND SUPERVISION FAILURES] --> root2[STAGE 2 BASIC CAUSES PERSONAL FACTORS AND JOB FACTORS]
    root2 --> root3[STAGE 3 IMMEDIATE CAUSES SUBSTANDARD ACTS AND SUBSTANDARD CONDITIONS]
    root3 --> root4[STAGE 4 INCIDENT CONTACT WITH ENERGY OR SUBSTANCE]
    root4 --> root5[STAGE 5 LOSS PEOPLE PROPERTY PROCESS PROFIT]
    root5 --> root6[STAGE 6 INFORMATION FEEDBACK LOOP TO STAGE 1]
    root6 -. Feedback to control .-> root1
```

## 4.2 Heinrich's Five-Domino Chain — Linear Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    d1[DOMINO 1 ANCESTRY AND SOCIAL ENVIRONMENT] --> d2[DOMINO 2 FAULT OF PERSON]
    d2 --> d3[DOMINO 3 UNSAFE ACT OR UNSAFE CONDITION]
    d3 --> d4[DOMINO 4 THE ACCIDENT EVENT]
    d4 --> d5[DOMINO 5 RESULTANT INJURY TO WORKER]
    d1:::noBorder
    d2:::noBorder
    d3:::noBorder
    d4:::noBorder
    d5:::noBorder
    classDef noBorder stroke-width:3px,stroke:#333,fill:#f5f5f5
```

## 4.3 Multi-Layer Cause Hierarchy — Functional Architecture

This block diagram represents how **root, basic, and immediate causes** interact to produce a loss event.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    layerA[LAYER A ROOT CAUSES] --> layerB[LAYER B BASIC CAUSES]
    layerB --> layerC[LAYER C IMMEDIATE CAUSES]
    layerC --> layerD[LAYER D ACCIDENT INCIDENT]
    layerD --> layerE[LAYER E LOSS TO PEOPLE PROPERTY ENVIRONMENT]
    layerA --- itemA1[Poor safety policy]
    layerA --- itemA2[Inadequate supervision]
    layerB --- itemB1[Worker fatigue]
    layerB --- itemB2[Improper tool design]
    layerC --- itemC1[Operating without guards]
    layerC --- itemC2[Failure to use PPE]
    layerD --- itemD1[Contact with moving parts]
    layerE --- itemE1[Fatality or major injury]
    layerE --- itemE2[Equipment damage]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams (KTU Exam Tip):** When a question asks "Explain Bird's model with a diagram," examiners award marks for: (1) correct sequencing of stages, (2) the **feedback loop** from Stage 6 to Stage 1, and (3) clear labeling of root vs immediate causes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define "Accident" and explain its key characteristics in an industrial context.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An accident is an **unplanned, undesired event** that results in personal injury, property damage, or process interruption. Key characteristics:

1. It is **unexpected and sudden** in occurrence.
2. It involves **energy transfer** beyond human tolerance limits.
3. It is **preventable** through hazard identification and control.

---

### Question A2: State Heinrich's Domino Theory and list its five sequential factors.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931) proposes that occupational accidents occur through a **five-stage chain reaction**, where each stage triggers the next like a falling line of dominoes:

1. **Ancestry and Social Environment** — inherited traits and upbringing.
2. **Fault of Person** — recklessness, ignorance, nervousness.
3. **Unsafe Act or Unsafe Condition** — the immediate trigger.
4. **Accident** — the unintended event.
5. **Injury** — the resulting harm to the person.

The central idea is that **removing any one domino** (factor) breaks the chain and prevents the injury.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the aid of a neat schematic, explain **Bird's Loss Causation Model**. Compare it with Heinrich's Domino Theory.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A manufacturing unit has **800 employees** working **8 hours per day** for **300 days** in a year. During the year, **15 reportable injuries** occurred, leading to a total of **120 man-days lost**. Calculate (i) Frequency Rate, (ii) Severity Rate, (iii) Incidence Rate, and (iv) Average days lost per injury. Comment on the safety performance if the industry average FR = **20** and SR = **100**.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]:**

Frank Bird (1974) extended Heinrich's classical model. Bird's model consists of **six sequential stages**:

1. **Lack of Control** — failures in management system and supervision.
2. **Basic Causes** — personal factors (attitude, skill) and job factors (design, environment).
3. **Immediate Causes** — substandard acts and substandard conditions.
4. **Incident** — contact with energy or substance.
5. **Loss** — to people, property, process, or profit.
6. **Information Feedback** — learnings looped back to improve management control.

**Comparison with Heinrich:**

| Aspect | Heinrich | Bird |
|--------|----------|------|
| Origin | Ancestry/Social | Management |
| Stages | 5 | 6 |
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Focus | Worker behavior | Management control |

Bird's model is more **modern** and emphasizes that management is responsible for accident prevention.

---

**Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given Data:**
- Employees = $800$
- Hours/day = $8$, Days/year = $300$
- Total man-hours = $800 \times 8 \times 300 = 1{,}920{,}000$
- Injuries = $N = 15$
- Days lost = $L = 120$

**(i) Frequency Rate:**

$$FR = \frac{N \times 10^{6}}{H} = \frac{15 \times 10^{6}}{1{,}920{,}000} = 7.81 \text{ per million man-hours}$$

**(ii) Severity Rate:**

$$SR = \frac{L \times 10^{6}}{H} = \frac{120 \times 10^{6}}{1{,}920{,}000} = 62.5 \text{ man-days per million man-hours}$$

**(iii) Incidence Rate:**

$$IR = \frac{N \times 10^{3}}{A} = \frac{15 \times 10^{3}}{800} = 18.75 \text{ injuries per 1000 employees}$$

**(iv) Average Days Lost per Injury:**

$$ADL = \frac{L}{N} = \frac{120}{15} = 8 \text{ days per injury}$$

**Comment on Safety Performance:**
- Calculated **FR = 7.81** is significantly **lower** than industry average of **20** → good.
- Calculated **SR = 62.5** is **lower** than industry average of **100** → injuries are less severe.

**Conclusion:** The plant's safety performance is **above industry average**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **Multiple Causation Theory** and the **Epidemiological Approach** to industrial accidents. Explain how they differ from the single-cause model.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Distinguish clearly between **Unsafe Acts** and **Unsafe Conditions**. Provide **six examples** for each category and explain the role of supervision in eliminating them.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]:**

The **Multiple Causation Theory** states that an accident is rarely the result of a single factor. Instead, multiple causes — human, environmental, and equipment-related — interact simultaneously. Removing any one factor may prevent the accident. This is the foundation of modern **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** and **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**.

The **Epidemiological Approach** borrows from public-health science. It treats the workplace as a "host," the hazard as an "agent," and unsafe behavior as a "vector." Accidents are studied like diseases — by analyzing **patterns in time, location, and personnel** to identify vulnerable groups.

**Differences from Single-Cause Model:**

| Feature | Single-Cause | Multiple Causation |
|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| Origin | One dominant factor | Several interacting factors |
| Prevention | Remove that one cause | Remove any contributing factor |
| Analysis Method | Linear | Network / Tree |
| Modern Use | Outdated | Standard in industry |

---

**Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]:**

| Category | Definition | Examples (any 6 each) |
|----------|------------|-----------------------|
| **Unsafe Acts** | Human behavioral errors or violations | (1) Operating without authority; (2) Failure to warn others; (3) Working at unsafe speed; (4) Bypassing safety devices; (5) Improper lifting; (6) Using defective equipment |
| **Unsafe Conditions** | Physical/environmental hazards | (1) Unguarded machinery; (2) Defective tools; (3) Poor lighting; (4) Slippery floors; (5) Congested workspace; (6) Hazardous storage |

**Role of Supervision:** Supervisors are responsible for **identifying unsafe conditions**, **training workers**, **enforcing PPE usage**, conducting **toolbox talks**, and **reporting near-misses**. Effective supervision reduces both categories.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Error in FR/SR:** Students forget to multiply by $10^{6}$ and write answers as simple ratios → **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing Heinrich vs Bird:** Heinrich starts with **Ancestry**, Bird starts with **Management Control**. Mixing them up costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the Feedback Loop in Bird's Model:** The Stage 6 → Stage 1 feedback arrow is **mandatory** for full marks in diagram questions.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert man-hours:** Always convert "employees × hours/day × days/year" before substituting into FR/SR formulas.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- An **accident** is an unplanned, undesired event causing loss to people, property, or process.
- **Three layers of causes:** Root → Basic → Immediate.
- **Heinrich's Domino Theory:** 5 dominos — Ancestry → Fault → Unsafe Act/Condition → Accident → Injury.
- **Bird's Model:** 6 stages — Lack of Control → Basic → Immediate → Incident → Loss → Feedback.
- **Multiple Causation Theory:** Accidents result from several interacting factors, not just one.
- **Epidemiological Approach:** Treat accidents like diseases; study patterns.
- **Heinrich's Ratio:** $1 : 29 : 300$.
- **Bird's Ratio:** $1 : 10 : 30 : 600$.
- **Frequency Rate formula:** $FR = \dfrac{N \times 10^{6}}{H}$.
- **Severity Rate formula:** $SR = \dfrac{L \times 10^{6}}{H}$.
- **Incidence Rate formula:** $IR = \dfrac{N \times 10^{3}}{A}$.
- **Standard man-hour conversion:** $1 \text{ year} = 300 \text{ working days} = 2400 \text{ hours}$.
- **Unsafe Acts** = human errors; **Unsafe Conditions** = physical hazards.
- Always draw **Bird's model with the feedback loop** from Stage 6 to Stage 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heinrich's Law of Accident Prevention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET637 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Heinrich's Law of Accident Prevention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heinrich's Law of Accident Prevention** is a foundational industrial safety principle propounded by **Herbert William Heinrich** in his seminal 1931 work *"Industrial Accident Prevention: A Scientific Approach."* The law states that in any industrial workplace, for every **major (serious/fatal) injury**, there will statistically occur **29 minor injuries** and **300 no-injury accidents (near-misses)** — yielding the classic ratio **1 : 29 : 300**.

Heinrich derived this empirical constant from an exhaustive analysis of approximately **75,000 accident records** collected from numerous American industries during the 1920s. The law is fundamentally an **epidemiological-frequency postulate** that underpins proactive safety management: by analysing and eliminating the vast pool of minor incidents and near-misses, organizations can systematically prevent the rare but catastrophic major accidents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEMET637):** This topic is a cornerstone of Module 1, bridging the historical evolution of safety philosophy with modern predictive safety metrics. Students are expected to understand both the **statistical ratio** and the **causal (Domino) sequence** Heinrich used to explain accident causation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a vast **ocean of small waves** (300 ripples), occasionally punctuated by **medium swells** (29 bigger waves), and very rarely by a **single massive tsunami** (1 devastating wave). You cannot stop the tsunami directly — but if you flatten the ripples and swells systematically, the tsunami has no energy left to build.

Applied to a factory:
- The **300 near-misses** are like the ripples — they happen constantly, often unnoticed.
- The **29 minor injuries** are the medium swells — they cause bandages, lost hours, and small compensation claims.
- The **1 major injury** is the rare, devastating event — a fatality or permanent disability.

The intuitive brilliance of Heinrich's Law: **Safety resources should be invested at the base of the pyramid (near-misses)**, because preventing the bottom prevents the top.

### 1.3 Key Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Heinrich's Ratio (Major : Minor : Near-Miss)** | **1 : 29 : 300** | Dimensionless |
| Total incidents per major injury | **330** | Incidents / Major injury |
| **Bird's Revised Ratio (1969)** | **1 : 10 : 30 : 600** | Dimensionless |
| **Adams' Extended Ratio (1995)** | **1 : 3 : 7 : 50 : 300** | Dimensionless |
| Heinrich's study sample size | ~75,000 | Accident records |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Heinrich's Doctrine

Heinrich's contribution to safety engineering is twofold — a **statistical frequency pillar** and a **causal chain pillar**. Mastery of both is essential for KTU examinations.

#### Pillar A — The Statistical Pyramid (1 : 29 : 300)

Heinrich proposed that workplace accidents are **not random** but follow a **predictable statistical distribution**. The vast majority of incidents cause no visible harm, while a small minority produce disabling injuries. This distribution is independent of industry type, geography, or workforce skill — it is a **universal human-factor constant**.

**Logical Steps:**
1. **Data Aggregation:** Pool accident records from multiple industries over several years.
2. **Severity Stratification:** Classify each incident as *No Injury*, *Minor Injury*, or *Serious/Fatal Injury* based on lost workdays and medical attention required.
3. **Frequency Normalization:** Count the relative frequency of each severity class per single serious injury.
4. **Ratio Convergence:** Observe that regardless of the absolute numbers, the ratio converges to approximately **1 : 29 : 300**.

#### Pillar B — The Domino Theory (Five-Factor Causal Sequence)

Heinrich's second (and arguably more influential) contribution is the **Domino Theory of Accident Causation**. He visualized accident causation as a sequence of five dominos standing in a row — when the first falls, it triggers the next, ultimately producing injury.

**The Five Dominoes (in causal order):**

1. **Domino 1 — Ancestry and Social Environment:** Hereditary traits, upbringing, cultural attitudes toward risk, socioeconomic pressures, and societal norms that shape an individual's personality and risk-tolerance.
2. **Domino 2 — Fault of the Person:** Acquired personal defects such as recklessness, nervousness, excitability, inattentiveness, or poor physical/mental condition — the "unsafe person."
3. **Domino 3 — Unsafe Act or Unsafe Condition (Mechanical/Physical Hazard):** The immediate precipitating cause — a procedural violation, failure to use PPE, poor housekeeping, defective equipment, or unguarded machinery.
4. **Domino 4 — Accident:** The unwanted release of energy (kinetic, electrical, chemical, thermal) or the harmful contact that constitutes the actual incident event.
5. **Domino 5 — Injury:** The bodily harm resulting from the energy transfer — ranging from a scratch to a fatality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Prevention Principle:** Heinrich's revolutionary insight was that by **removing Domino 3** (the unsafe act/condition), the entire causal chain is broken. Even if Dominoes 1 and 2 fall, the injury (Domino 5) cannot occur because the energy release is interrupted.

### 2.2 Modern Extensions and Critiques

| Theory | Year | Ratio | Key Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heinrich (Original)** | 1931 | 1 : 29 : 300 | First empirical accident pyramid |
| **Bird & Germain** | 1969 | 1 : 10 : 30 : 600 | Added *Property Damage* tier; refined for insurance/financial lens |
| **Adams (Extended)** | 1995 | 1 : 3 : 7 : 50 : 300 | Separated *First Aid*, *Medical Treatment*, and *Restricted Work* cases |
| **Tye & Pearson (UK HSE)** | 1974 | 1 : 3 : 50 | Reweighted for British workforce; near-miss dominance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Examinations:** Modern safety science (Reason's Swiss Cheese Model, 1990) has *retained* Heinrich's Domino philosophy but *replaced* the linearity with a **multi-layered defence model** in which hazards must align across multiple system layers to cause harm. Examiners frequently ask for comparative discussions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{total} = N_{major} + N_{minor} + N_{nearmiss}$ | Total incidents = sum across all severities | Incidents |
| 2 | $R_H = \dfrac{N_{major}}{N_{minor}} = \dfrac{1}{29}$ | Heinrich's major-to-minor ratio | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $R_H' = \dfrac{N_{major}}{N_{nearmiss}} = \dfrac{1}{300}$ | Heinrich's major-to-near-miss ratio | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $N_{nearmiss} = 300 \cdot N_{major}$ | Predict near-miss count from major injuries | Incidents |
| 5 | $N_{minor} = 29 \cdot N_{major}$ | Predict minor injury count from major injuries | Incidents |
| 6 | $N_{total} = 330 \cdot N_{major}$ | Total incidents per major injury | Incidents |
| 7 | $f_i = \dfrac{N_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Frequency proportion of severity class $i$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $P(\text{no injury} \vert \text{accident}) = \dfrac{300}{330}$ | Conditional probability a workplace accident causes no injury | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $P(\text{minor} \vert \text{accident}) = \dfrac{29}{330}$ | Conditional probability a workplace accident causes minor injury | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $P(\text{major} \vert \text{accident}) = \dfrac{1}{330}$ | Conditional probability a workplace accident causes major injury | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $E[\text{incidents/year}] = \lambda \cdot t$ | Expected incidents over time $t$ at rate $\lambda$ | Incidents |
| 12 | $E_{loss} = N_{major} \cdot C_{major} + N_{minor} \cdot C_{minor} + N_{nm} \cdot C_{nm}$ | Total expected accident cost (economic) | Currency |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are deployed in **predictive safety analytics**, **insurance actuarial calculations**, **OSHA-style safety audits**, and **Behaviour-Based Safety (BBS)** programmes. Modern industries use near-miss frequency (the 300-tier) as a **leading indicator** of safety performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Heinrich's Predictive Ratio

Heinrich's procedure for arriving at the **1 : 29 : 300** constant can be reconstructed as a three-stage statistical derivation. This is a high-frequency KTU derivation question.

**Stage 1 — Data Collection and Severity Classification**

Heinrich aggregated $N = 75{,}000$ accident reports. Each was classified by an independent safety inspector into one of three categories based on the medical outcome:

- **Major injury (M):** Death, permanent disability, or injury causing > 1 lost workday.
- **Minor injury (m):** Injury requiring medical attention but < 1 lost workday.
- **No-injury accident (N):** Property damage only or no measurable harm (a "near-miss" in modern terminology).

**Stage 2 — Ratio Normalization**

Let the observed counts be $n_M$, $n_m$, $n_N$. Heinrich then normalized by the count of major injuries:

$$
R_1 = \frac{n_M}{n_M} = 1
$$

$$
R_2 = \frac{n_m}{n_M}
$$

$$
R_3 = \frac{n_N}{n_M}
$$

**Stage 3 — Empirical Convergence**

Across all 75,000 records, the ratios converged stably to:

$$
\frac{n_m}{n_M} \to 29
$$

$$
\frac{n_N}{n_M} \to 300
$$

Therefore, the **canonical Heinrich ratio** is:

$$
R_1 : R_2 : R_3 = 1 : 29 : 300
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is a *relative frequency* derivation, not a probabilistic proof. The constancy of the ratio across diverse industries suggests that **human psychological and behavioural factors** — not industry-specific engineering hazards — drive the distribution. This was Heinrich's core philosophical argument for the universality of the law.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Typical KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A manufacturing plant recorded **4 major injuries** in a calendar year. Using Heinrich's Law, predict:
(a) The expected number of minor injuries.
(b) The expected number of no-injury accidents (near-misses).
(c) The conditional probability that a randomly chosen workplace accident caused *no* injury.
(d) The total expected accident-related insurance cost, given $C_{major} = ₹12{,}00{,}000$, $C_{minor} = ₹25{,}000$, and $C_{nm} = ₹2{,}000$ per incident.

---

**Part (a) — Expected Minor Injuries**

Using the formula $N_{minor} = 29 \cdot N_{major}$:

$$
N_{minor} = 29 \times 4 = 116 \text{ injuries}
$$

**Part (b) — Expected No-Injury Accidents**

Using $N_{nearmiss} = 300 \cdot N_{major}$:

$$
N_{nearmiss} = 300 \times 4 = 1{,}200 \text{ incidents}
$$

**Part (c) — Conditional Probability of No-Injury Event**

The total expected accidents in the year:

$$
N_{total} = 4 + 116 + 1{,}200 = 1{,}320 \text{ incidents}
$$

The conditional probability:

$$
P(\text{no injury} \vert \text{accident}) = \frac{1{,}200}{1{,}320} = \frac{10}{11} \approx 0.9091
$$

In percentage form: $\approx \mathbf{90.91\%}$

**Part (d) — Expected Total Insurance Cost**

$$
E_{loss} = N_{major} \cdot C_{major} + N_{minor} \cdot C_{minor} + N_{nm} \cdot C_{nm}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
E_{loss} = (4 \times 12{,}00{,}000) + (116 \times 25{,}000) + (1{,}200 \times 2{,}000)
$$

$$
E_{loss} = 48{,}00{,}000 + 29{,}00{,}000 + 24{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
E_{loss} = ₹1{,}01{,}00{,}000 \quad (\text{One Crore One Lakh Rupees})
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Probabilistic Pyramid

Heinrich's Law can be recast probabilistically. Define the event space $S$ of all workplace accidents. Partition it into three mutually exclusive outcomes: $\{M, m, N\}$. Then:

$$
P(M) + P(m) + P(N) = 1
$$

From the ratio $1 : 29 : 300$, the total number of "parts" is:

$$
1 + 29 + 300 = 330
$$

Therefore, the individual probabilities are:

$$
P(M) = \frac{1}{330} \approx 0.00303
$$

$$
P(m) = \frac{29}{330} \approx 0.08788
$$

$$
P(N) = \frac{300}{330} = \frac{10}{11} \approx 0.90909
$$

**Verification:**

$$
\frac{1}{330} + \frac{29}{330} + \frac{300}{330} = \frac{330}{330} = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why the Domino Sequence is Linear

Heinrich argued that Domino 3 is the **only controllable element** in the chain. The mathematical structure of the sequence can be expressed as a conditional-probability chain:

$$
P(I) = P(I \vert A) \cdot P(A \vert U) \cdot P(U \vert F) \cdot P(F \vert E) \cdot P(E)
$$

Where:
- $E$ = Ancestry/Social Environment (Domino 1)
- $F$ = Fault of Person (Domino 2)
- $U$ = Unsafe Act/Condition (Domino 3)
- $A$ = Accident event (Domino 4)
- $I$ = Injury (Domino 5)

**The Prevention Logic:** If we set $P(U \vert F) = 0$ (eliminate all unsafe acts/conditions), then:

$$
P(I) = P(I \vert A) \cdot P(A \vert 0) \cdot 0 \cdot P(F \vert E) \cdot P(E) = 0
$$

This demonstrates mathematically that **removing the third domino** (engineering controls, safe procedures, PPE) **guarantees prevention of injury**, regardless of personal faults or social environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the mathematical foundation of the **"hierarchy of controls"** — Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE. By making the hazard *physically impossible*, we break the chain at the most effective point.

### 3.5 Comparison Matrix: Heinrich vs. Bird vs. Adams

| Parameter | Heinrich (1931) | Bird (1969) | Adams (1995) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tiers** | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| **Ratio** | 1 : 29 : 300 | 1 : 10 : 30 : 600 | 1 : 3 : 7 : 50 : 300 |
| **Added Tier** | — | Serious Property Damage | First Aid / Medical Treatment / Restricted Work |
| **Sample** | 75,000 | Insurance claims | Multiple industry studies |
| **Focus** | Behavioural | Financial | Modern safety management |
| **P(no injury)** | $\frac{300}{330} = 90.91\%$ | $\frac{600}{647} = 92.74\%$ | $\frac{300}{361} = 83.10\%$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Heinrich's Five-Domino Causal Chain (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1A[Domino 1: Ancestry and Social Environment] --> D2A[Domino 2: Fault of the Person]
    D2A --> D3A[Domino 3: Unsafe Act or Unsafe Condition]
    D3A --> D4A[Domino 4: Accident Energy Release]
    D4A --> D5A[Domino 5: Injury or Damage]
    D3A -. Remove Middle Domino .-> PREV[Prevention Achieved]
    PREV --> D5PREV[No Injury Occurs]
    style D1A fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    style D2A fill:#ffe9c2,stroke:#e67e22,color:#000
    style D3A fill:#fff4b2,stroke:#f1c40f,color:#000
    style D4A fill:#c2e0ff,stroke:#2980b9,color:#000
    style D5A fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000
    style PREV fill:#e8daff,stroke:#8e44ad,color:#000
    style D5PREV fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **red-to-green colour gradient** visualises the rising severity chain.
- The **dashed arrow** to `PREV` shows the intervention point — extracting Domino 3 breaks the entire sequence.
- The final green box `D5PREV` is the desired safety outcome.

### 4.2 Heinrich's Accident Triangle (Pyramid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOPNODE[MAJOR INJURY - 1 case - Top of Pyramid]
    MIDNODE[MINOR INJURY - 29 cases - Middle Tier]
    BASENODE[NO INJURY / NEAR MISS - 300 cases - Wide Base]
    BASENODE --> MIDNODE
    MIDNODE --> TOPNODE
    style TOPNODE fill:#c0392b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style MIDNODE fill:#e67e22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style BASENODE fill:#f1c40f,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Mermaid's flowchart engine renders this as a vertical stack, visually mimicking a pyramid's narrowing apex. The wide base (300) supports the medium tier (29), which supports the rare peak (1). The geometric *area* of each tier represents its statistical frequency, just as in the original iceberg/accident-pyramid analogy popularised by Heinrich.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Statistics to Prevention

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["STAGE 1: DATA INPUT LAYER"]
        A1[Workplace Incident Reports]
        A2[Medical Records]
        A3[Near Miss Logs]
    end
    subgraph CLASSIFY["STAGE 2: CLASSIFICATION LAYER"]
        B1[Major Injury Triage]
        B2[Minor Injury Triage]
        B3[No Injury Triage]
    end
    subgraph ANALYSE["STAGE 3: STATISTICAL ANALYSIS LAYER"]
        C1[Compute Ratio R = 1:29:300]
        C2[Compute Probabilities P]
        C3[Predict Future Incidents]
    end
    subgraph INTERVENE["STAGE 4: INTERVENTION LAYER"]
        D1[Identify Unsafe Acts]
        D2[Identify Unsafe Conditions]
        D3[Remove Domino 3]
    end
    subgraph OUTCOME["STAGE 5: SAFETY OUTCOME LAYER"]
        E1[Injury Prevention Achieved]
        E2[Cost Reduction]
        E3[Compliance and Culture]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1
    D3 --> E2
    D3 --> E3
    style INPUT fill:#eaf6ff,stroke:#2980b9,color:#000
    style CLASSIFY fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#e67e22,color:#000
    style ANALYSE fill:#f4ecff,stroke:#8e44ad,color:#000
    style INTERVENE fill:#e8f8ee,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000
    style OUTCOME fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
```

> **Architectural Note:** This block diagram maps how Heinrich's two pillars (statistical ratio and causal domino chain) integrate into a modern safety management system. The left-to-right flow shows: **raw data → triage → statistical inference → engineering intervention → measurable safety outcomes**. This is the operational logic embedded in ISO 45001, OSHA VPP, and KTU's prescribed industrial safety framework.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMET637]**

> State **Heinrich's Law** of accident prevention. Mention its ratio and the name of its originator.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Heinrich's Law, formulated by **Herbert William Heinrich** in 1931, states that in any industrial workplace, the ratio of major injuries to minor injuries to no-injury accidents is **1 : 29 : 300**. This means for every major (serious or fatal) injury, there are statistically 29 minor injuries and 300 accidents causing no visible injury (near-misses). The law is based on the analysis of approximately 75,000 accident cases and is used as a predictive tool — eliminating the large pool of minor incidents and near-misses statistically reduces the rare occurrence of major injuries.

**Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- 1 mark: Name and year of originator.
- 1 mark: The 1:29:300 ratio.
- 1 mark: The predictive/preventive implication.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sample Paper]**

> List the **five dominos** in Heinrich's Domino Theory and identify which domino corresponds to the *intervention point* for accident prevention.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The five dominos in Heinrich's Domino Theory are:
1. **Domino 1:** Ancestry and Social Environment.
2. **Domino 2:** Fault of the Person.
3. **Domino 3:** Unsafe Act or Unsafe Condition (Mechanical/Physical Hazard).
4. **Domino 4:** The Accident (release of harmful energy).
5. **Domino 5:** The Injury.

The **intervention point is Domino 3** — the unsafe act or unsafe condition. By removing this domino (through engineering controls, safe procedures, training, and PPE), the entire causal chain is broken, and injury (Domino 5) is prevented even if the personal and social dominoes fall.

**Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- 2 marks: Five dominos in correct order.
- 1 mark: Identification of Domino 3 as intervention point.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **confuse Bird's 1:10:30:600 ratio** with Heinrich's original. Always specify the originator and year.
> - When drawing the Domino Theory, examiners **deduct marks** if dominos are not in the *correct causal order* — Domino 1 is **Ancestry/Environment**, NOT the Unsafe Act.
> - In numerical problems, **always show the substitution step** explicitly. KTU valuators award method marks even when arithmetic slips occur.
> - Do NOT confuse Heinrich's *Domino Theory* (causal) with his *Pyramid/Ratio* (statistical) — they are two distinct contributions.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted Model Paper PEMET637]**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail **Heinrich's Domino Theory** of accident causation. State the five factors and explain how removal of the third factor prevents injury. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A factory recorded **3 major injuries** in one year. Using Heinrich's Law, calculate: (i) expected number of minor injuries, (ii) expected number of near-misses, and (iii) the conditional probability that a random accident caused a *major* injury. The cost of a major injury is ₹15,00,000 and that of a minor injury is ₹20,000. Compute the **expected total cost** if all minor and major events occur as predicted. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

Heinrich's **Domino Theory** models accident causation as a linear sequence of five sequential factors (dominos), where the fall of each factor triggers the next.

**The Five Factors:**

**Domino 1 — Ancestry and Social Environment:**
This represents the inherited and socially-acquired characteristics of an individual. It includes hereditary personality traits, family upbringing, education, economic status, cultural attitudes toward authority and risk, and the broader social environment. Heinrich argued that this is the *root* of unsafe behaviour because personality is shaped primarily before the individual enters the workplace.

**Domino 2 — Fault of the Person:**
This is the *acquired* personal deficiency that arises from the social environment. Examples include recklessness, nervousness, inattentiveness, lack of knowledge, impulsiveness, or physical defects like poor eyesight and hearing. Heinrich estimated that approximately **88% of industrial accidents** are caused by unsafe acts originating from personal faults.

**Domino 3 — Unsafe Act or Unsafe Condition:**
This is the *immediate* cause — the trigger. An unsafe act is a behavioural violation (e.g., not using PPE, operating equipment at unsafe speeds, working under the influence). An unsafe condition is a physical hazard (e.g., unguarded machinery, poor lighting, cluttered floors, defective tools). This is the **only domino within management's direct control**.

**Domino 4 — The Accident:**
This is the moment of energy release or harmful contact. Heinrich defined an accident as "an unwanted, unexpected event resulting from an unsafe act or condition that may or may not result in injury." Examples include a worker slipping, a tool falling, a chemical splash, or a vehicle collision.

**Domino 5 — The Injury:**
This is the final outcome — bodily harm caused by the uncontrolled energy transfer (kinetic, electrical, chemical, thermal, biological, or radiant). Injuries range from minor bruises to permanent disability and death.

**Mechanism of Prevention:**

The dominos are arranged in a row. If the first falls, the second follows, then the third, fourth, and fifth. The chain is causal and sequential. Heinrich's critical insight was:

> *"The accident prevention problem can be solved by removing the third domino — the unsafe act or unsafe condition. If this domino is permanently removed, even if Dominoes 1 and 2 fall, the energy release (Domino 4) and the injury (Domino 5) cannot occur."*

**Practical applications** of removing Domino 3 include: engineering controls (machine guarding, lockout-tagout), administrative controls (safe procedures, training, supervision), and personal protective equipment. Heinrich placed special emphasis on **engineering controls** because they do not depend on human behaviour.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:** $N_{major} = 3$ injuries per year.

**Step 1 — Expected Minor Injuries (i):**

Using Heinrich's ratio, $N_{minor} = 29 \cdot N_{major}$:

$$
N_{minor} = 29 \times 3 = 87 \text{ minor injuries}
$$

**Step 2 — Expected Near-Misses (ii):**

Using $N_{nearmiss} = 300 \cdot N_{major}$:

$$
N_{nearmiss} = 300 \times 3 = 900 \text{ near-misses}
$$

**Step 3 — Conditional Probability of Major Injury (iii):**

Total expected incidents in the year:

$$
N_{total} = 3 + 87 + 900 = 990 \text{ incidents}
$$

Conditional probability:

$$
P(\text{major} \vert \text{accident}) = \frac{3}{990} = \frac{1}{330} \approx 0.00303
$$

Expressed as a percentage: $\approx \mathbf{0.303\%}$

**Step 4 — Expected Total Cost:**

Given $C_{major} = ₹15{,}00{,}000$ and $C_{minor} = ₹20{,}000$:

$$
E_{loss} = (3 \times 15{,}00{,}000) + (87 \times 20{,}000)
$$

$$
E_{loss} = 45{,}00{,}000 + 17{,}40{,}000 = ₹62{,}40{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{loss} = ₹62{,}40{,}000 \text{ (Sixty-Two Lakh Forty Thousand Rupees)}}
$$

**Note:** Near-miss cost is excluded here as the problem did not specify $C_{nm}$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted Model Paper PEMET637]**
>
> **(a)** With neat illustrations, explain **Heinrich's Accident Triangle (Pyramid)**. Compare it with **Bird's modified ratio** and **Adams' extended ratio**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A construction site has historically experienced a near-miss frequency of **1500 incidents per year**. Using Heinrich's Law, calculate: (i) the expected number of major injuries per year, (ii) the expected number of minor injuries per year, and (iii) the percentage of all accidents that are *near-misses*. Comment on the **economic significance** of this distribution for the company's safety budget. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Heinrich's Accident Triangle (1931):**

Heinrich visualised the relative frequency of workplace accidents as a triangle (or pyramid) with three horizontal tiers. The base of the pyramid is widest, representing the highest frequency of incidents, and the apex is narrow, representing the rarest but most severe events.

- **Base (widest, 300 cases):** No-injury accidents (near-misses, property-only damage, uneventful unsafe acts).
- **Middle tier (29 cases):** Minor injuries (first aid, light duty, < 1 day lost).
- **Apex (1 case):** Major injuries (death, permanent disability, > 1 lost workday).

The **triangle metaphor** conveys that the *foundation* supports the structure — a large number of near-misses "feed into" minor injuries, which in turn "feed into" major injuries. By **flattening the base** (eliminating near-misses), the entire triangle collapses, preventing the apex.

**Bird's Modified Ratio (Frank Bird, 1969):**

Bird, working with insurance loss data, added a fourth tier for **serious property damage** and reweighted the upper tiers:

$$
\text{Bird's Ratio} = 1 : 10 : 30 : 600
$$

Meaning: 1 serious disabling injury, 10 minor injuries, 30 property damage incidents, and 600 no-injury / near-miss events. Bird's contribution shifted focus toward the **financial cost of accidents** and established near-miss reporting as a leading safety indicator.

**Adams' Extended Ratio (Edward Adams, 1995):**

Adams, after analysing modern workplaces, argued that Heinrich's binary "minor vs. major" classification was too coarse. He proposed a five-tier pyramid:

$$
\text{Adams' Ratio} = 1 : 3 : 7 : 50 : 300
$$

Meaning: 1 major injury, 3 serious injuries, 7 minor injuries (medical treatment), 50 first-aid cases, and 300 near-misses. Adams also introduced the concept that **unsafe conditions and unsafe acts are independent causal streams** that must both be managed — a precursor to modern **Behaviour-Based Safety (BBS)** programmes.

**Comparative Summary Table:**

| Aspect | Heinrich | Bird | Adams |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tiers | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| Ratio | 1 : 29 : 300 | 1 : 10 : 30 : 600 | 1 : 3 : 7 : 50 : 300 |
| Focus | Behavioural | Financial | Modern management |

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $N_{nearmiss} = 1{,}500$ incidents per year.

**Step 1 — Expected Major Injuries (i):**

Using the inverse Heinrich ratio $N_{major} = \dfrac{N_{nearmiss}}{300}$:

$$
N_{major} = \frac{1{,}500}{300} = 5 \text{ major injuries per year}
$$

**Step 2 — Expected Minor Injuries (ii):**

Using the proportion $\dfrac{N_{minor}}{N_{nearmiss}} = \dfrac{29}{300}$:

$$
N_{minor} = \frac{29}{300} \times 1{,}500 = 29 \times 5 = 145 \text{ minor injuries per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage of Near-Misses (iii):**

Total expected incidents:

$$
N_{total} = 1{,}500 + 145 + 5 = 1{,}650 \text{ incidents}
$$

Percentage of near-misses:

$$
\%_{nm} = \frac{1{,}500}{1{,}650} \times 100\% = 90.91\%
$$

**Step 4 — Economic Significance:**

The distribution reveals critical budget allocation principles:

- **91% of incidents are near-misses** — these are *low-cost to remediate* but signal latent hazards. Investing in near-miss reporting and root-cause analysis is **proportionally the highest-ROI safety activity**.
- **8.8% are minor injuries** — moderate direct cost (medical, light duty, small claims) and indirect cost (supervision time, replacement workers).
- **0.3% are major injuries** — extremely high direct cost (compensation, legal, fatality-related) and catastrophic indirect cost (reputation, morale, regulatory penalty).

**Strategic Conclusion:** The 1:29:300 distribution means that **for every rupee spent on near-miss prevention, the company statistically avoids thousands of rupees in major-injury cost**. A safety budget heavily weighted toward reactive major-injury response is economically irrational — the **leading indicator (near-miss frequency)** must be the primary budgetary driver.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1**

- **Originator:** Herbert William Heinrich, 1931, *"Industrial Accident Prevention: A Scientific Approach."*
- **Canonical Ratio:** **1 : 29 : 300** (Major : Minor : No-injury).
- **Sample size:** ~75,000 accident cases.
- **Two contributions:** (i) **Statistical Pyramid** (frequency), (ii) **Domino Theory** (causation).
- **Five dominos (in order):** Ancestry/Social Environment → Fault of Person → Unsafe Act/Condition → Accident → Injury.
- **Intervention point:** **Domino 3** (Unsafe Act/Condition) — only domino fully within management's control.
- **Probability facts:** $P(\text{no injury}) = \dfrac{300}{330} = 90.91\%$, $P(\text{minor}) = \dfrac{29}{330} = 8.79\%$, $P(\text{major}) = \dfrac{1}{330} = 0.30\%$.
- **Bird's revised ratio (1969):** 1 : 10 : 30 : 600 — adds property damage tier.
