| **JHA / JSA (Job Hazard / Safety Analysis)** | Task-specific | Breaks jobs into steps |
| **FMEA (Failure Mode & Effects Analysis)** | Equipment/component level | Hardware reliability focus |
| **FTA (Fault Tree Analysis)** | Top-down logical analysis | Identifies root combinations |
| **ETA (Event Tree Analysis)** | Forward consequence analysis | Post-initiator outcomes |
| **HAZOP (Hazard & Operability Study)** | Process plants | Deviation from design intent |
| **What-If / Checklist Analysis** | Quick screening | Simple, low-resource |
| **Bow-Tie Analysis** | Cause + Consequence integration | Visual risk barrier model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Focus:** HIRA, JSA, and FMEA are the most frequently tested. HAZOP is also emphasized as it appears in chemical and process plant safety contexts.

## 2.3 Risk Assessment Matrix (The Classic 5×5 Grid)

The most widely used risk assessment tool, taught in Module 2, is the **5×5 Likelihood × Consequence Matrix**:

**Step 1: Define Likelihood Categories (Probability)**

| Score | Likelihood | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Rare | May occur in exceptional circumstances |
| **2** | Unlikely | Could occur but not expected |
| **3** | Possible | Could occur during project lifetime |
| **4** | Likely | Will probably occur |
| **5** | Almost Certain | Expected to occur, possibly multiple times |

**Step 2: Define Consequence Categories (Severity)**

| Score | Consequence | Description (Persons) | Description (Assets) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Negligible | No injury | < ₹ 10,000 loss |
| **2** | Minor | First aid injury | ₹ 10,000 – ₹ 1 Lakh |
| **3** | Moderate | Medical treatment, lost time | ₹ 1 Lakh – ₹ 10 Lakh |
| **4** | Major | Serious injury, disability | ₹ 10 Lakh – ₹ 1 Crore |
| **5** | Catastrophic | Fatality, multiple fatalities | > ₹ 1 Crore |

**Step 3: Risk Score = Likelihood × Consequence**

| Risk Score | Risk Level | Action Required |
|---|---|---|
| **1 – 4** | **Low (Acceptable)** | Monitor, no immediate action |
| **5 – 9** | **Medium (Caution)** | Reduce risk where practicable, plan controls |
| **10 – 15** | **High (Substantial)** | Senior management attention, implement controls ASAP |
| **16 – 25** | **Very High (Intolerable)** | Stop activity, immediate corrective action mandatory |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tolerable Risk Principle:** No industrial activity is "zero risk." Society accepts a small, residual level of risk in exchange for industrial benefits. This is the concept of **"Tolerable Risk"** — the level of risk that is accepted in a given context.

## 2.4 The ALARP Principle (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)

Originating from UK Health & Safety at Work Act and the Edwards v. National Coal Board (1949) case:

> *"The computation of risk is a balancing exercise between the strength of the measures to be taken against the gravity of the risk if those measures are not taken."*

**ALARP Triangle (Gross Risk → Net Risk)**:

$$
\text{Gross Risk} \xrightarrow{\text{apply cost-effective controls}} \text{Net (Residual) Risk}
$$

Controls follow the **Hierarchy of Controls** (most effective → least effective):

1. **Elimination** (remove hazard entirely)
2. **Substitution** (replace with less hazardous)
3. **Engineering Controls** (guards, ventilation, interlocks)
4. **Administrative Controls** (training, procedures, signage)
5. **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** (last resort)

> The cost of further risk reduction must be **"Grossly Disproportionate"** to the benefit gained. If implementing a control costs ₹ 10 Crores to prevent a ₹ 1 Lakh loss, ALARP is satisfied even though risk isn't zero.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Risk Equation | $R = P \times C$ | Dimensionless (qualitative) |
| 2 | Extended Risk Equation | $R = P \times C \times E$ | Includes exposure factor |
| 3 | Fatal Accident Rate (FAR) | $\text{FAR} = \frac{N_{\text{fatal}} \times 10^8}{\text{Exposure hours}}$ | Per $10^8$ person-hours |
| 4 | Frequency Rate (FR) | $\text{FR} = \frac{N_{\text{injuries}} \times 10^6}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$ | Per $10^6$ person-hours |
| 5 | Severity Rate (SR) | $\text{SR} = \frac{\text{Days lost} \times 10^6}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$ | Per $10^6$ person-hours |
| 6 | Incidence Rate (IR) | $\text{IR} = \frac{N_{\text{injuries}} \times 10^3}{\text{Average no. of employees}}$ | Per 1000 employees |
| 7 | Heinrich Ratio | $1 : 29 : 300$ | Major : Minor : Near-miss |
| 8 | Bird Ratio | $1 : 10 : 30 : 600$ | Major : Minor : Damage : Near-miss |
| 9 | Risk Matrix Score | $R = L \times S$ | L (1–5) × S (1–5) → 1–25 |
| 10 | ALARP Cost Test | $\text{Cost of control} \leq \text{Benefit} \times \text{Disproportion Factor}$ | DF typically 2–10 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize the **risk matrix (1–25 scoring)** and the **ALARP principle** — these appear in nearly every Part B (14-mark) question on Module 2.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Hazard–Risk framework is applied in:
- **Process Safety** (Oil & Gas, Refineries, Petrochemicals) → HAZOP, QRA
- **Construction Safety** → JSA, Permit-to-Work systems
- **Manufacturing** → Machine guarding, Lockout/Tagout
- **Mining** → Bow-Tie analysis, Major Hazard Management
- **Aviation / Nuclear** → Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA)
- **General Industry** → HIRA, PPE selection, safety audits
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Risk Equation from First Principles

Let us derive the **Risk Score** formula rigorously from the underlying probability and consequence definitions used in safety science.

### Step 1: Define the Probability of an Event

The probability $P$ of a hazardous event occurring during a specific time interval is a function of the **failure rate** $\lambda$ (events per unit time) and the **exposure duration** $t$.

For small time intervals, we can use the first-order approximation:

$$
P(t) \approx \lambda \cdot t \quad \text{(for } \lambda t \ll 1\text{)}
$$

For larger intervals, using the Poisson model:

$$
P(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
$$

### Step 2: Define the Consequence Severity

Consequence $C$ is a measure of harm severity, expressed in categorical units (1 to 5) or continuous units (fatalities, monetary loss, etc.). For analytical purposes, let us use a **normalized severity scale** $S$:

$$
S = \frac{C - C_{\min}}{C_{\max} - C_{\min}} \quad \text{(normalized 0 to 1)}
$$

### Step 3: Combine Probability and Consequence

The **Risk Magnitude** is the product of the likelihood of occurrence and the severity of harm:

$$
R = P \cdot C
$$

Substituting the Poisson form:

$$
R = (1 - e^{-\lambda t}) \cdot C
$$

> **Interpretation:** As $\lambda \to 0$ (rare events), $R \to 0$ (low risk). As $C \to \infty$ (extreme severity), $R$ grows proportionally. The exponential term captures the **diminishing marginal risk** for very high failure rates (the event is nearly certain, so additional exposure adds little new risk).

### Step 4: Incorporate Exposure Factor

The basic $R = P \times C$ equation does not account for **how many people** or **how much asset value** is exposed. This is included via the exposure factor $E$:

$$
R = P \cdot C \cdot E
$$

Where:
- $E = N_{\text{people exposed}} \times f_{\text{occupancy}} \times f_{\text{duration}}$

This is the **individual risk** concept used in QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment).

### Step 5: Derive Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)

FAR is a standard safety performance metric. Starting from:

$$
\text{FAR} = \frac{\text{Number of Fatalities}}{\text{Total Exposure Hours}} \times 10^8
$$

**Worked numerical derivation:**

Given:
- 2 fatalities in a factory over 1 year.
- Workforce = 500 employees.
- Average working hours per employee per year = 2,000 hours.
- Total exposure hours = $500 \times 2000 = 1{,}000{,}000$ hours = $10^6$ hours.

$$
\text{FAR} = \frac{2}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 10^8 = 2 \times 10^2 = 200
$$

> **Interpretation:** The factory has 200 fatalities per $10^8$ person-hours. This is a **very high-risk** industry (typical FAR ranges: Offices ≈ 1; Construction ≈ 5; Mining ≈ 40; Offshore Oil ≈ 100+).

### Step 6: Derive the ALARP Cost Test

ALARP requires that the cost of implementing further controls be weighed against the statistical benefit (risk reduction). The mathematical form is:

$$
\text{Cost of Risk Reduction (CoRR)} \leq \text{Gross Disproportion Factor (GDF)} \times \text{Benefit (B)}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\frac{\text{Benefit (B)}}{\text{Cost of Control (C)}} \geq \frac{1}{\text{GDF}}
$$

Where:
- $B = \Delta R \times N_{\text{affected}} \times V_{\text{statistical life}}$
- $\Delta R$ = Risk reduction achieved by the control
- $N_{\text{affected}}$ = Number of persons/operations impacted
- $V_{\text{statistical life}}$ = Statistical Value of Life (VSL) — varies by country (India ≈ ₹ 1–2 Crore)
- GDF = 2 to 10 (typically 3 for industry)

**Example numerical derivation:**

Given:
- Risk reduction from a control: $\Delta R = 0.5$ fatalities/year
- Workers affected: $N = 100$
- VSL in India: ₹ 1 Crore
- GDF = 3
- Cost of control: ₹ 5 Crore

Benefit:
$$
B = \Delta R \times N \times V_{\text{SL}} = 0.5 \times 100 \times 1\,\text{Crore} = 50\,\text{Crores}
$$

Wait — this overestimates. Correcting the formula interpretation: per-worker reduction:

$$
B = 0.5 \times 1\,\text{Crore} = 0.5\,\text{Crore/year}
$$

ALARP threshold:
$$
\text{Max justifiable cost} = \text{GDF} \times B = 3 \times 0.5 = 1.5\,\text{Crore/year}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Since the cost (₹ 5 Crore) > max justifiable cost (₹ 1.5 Crore), the control is **NOT ALARP-required** at that cost. The control is disproportionate to the risk reduction.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Board Pattern)

### Example 1: Risk Matrix Calculation

A workshop task involves working with a **hydraulic press** (Mechanical Hazard). The safety officer classifies:
- **Likelihood (L) = 4** (Likely — occurs during the project lifetime)
- **Severity (S) = 5** (Catastrophic — potential crush injury, fatality)

Compute the risk score and recommended action.

**Step 1: Calculate Risk Score**
$$
R = L \times S = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

**Step 2: Classify Risk Level**
$$
R = 20 \in [16, 25] \Rightarrow \text{Very High (Intolerable)}
$$

**Step 3: Action Required**
> Stop activity immediately. Implement multiple layers of control: fixed guards, light curtains, two-hand controls, emergency stops, and operator training. Re-assess before resumption.

---

### Example 2: Heinrich-Bird Ratio Application

A factory reported **1 fatal accident, 8 lost-time injuries, 25 minor injuries, and 450 near-misses** in a year. Check if the ratios are consistent with Heinrich/Bird's models.

**Heinrich's predicted ratio (1 : 29 : 300):**
- Expected minor injuries for 1 fatal: **29**
- Expected near-misses: **300**
- Observed: minor = 8, near-miss = 450
- Minor injuries are **lower than predicted** → better safety performance.
- Near-misses are **higher than predicted** → better reporting culture.

**Bird's predicted ratio (1 : 10 : 30 : 600):**
- Expected lost-time: 10; Observed: 8 → close.
- Expected property damage: 30 (not reported in data).
- Expected near-miss: 600; Observed: 450 → close.

> **Conclusion:** The factory's reporting culture is strong (high near-miss reporting), and serious injury rate is below predicted. The safety management system is performing well.

---

### Example 3: Incident Rate Calculation

A factory with **800 employees** reported **12 lost-time injuries** and **120 days lost** during 2024. Total man-hours worked = $800 \times 2000 = 1{,}600{,}000$ hours.

**Frequency Rate (FR):**
$$
\text{FR} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{1{,}600{,}000} = 7.5 \,\text{per } 10^6 \text{ person-hours}
$$

**Severity Rate (SR):**
$$
\text{SR} = \frac{120 \times 10^6}{1{,}600{,}000} = 75 \,\text{per } 10^6 \text{ person-hours}
$$

**Incidence Rate (IR):**
$$
\text{IR} = \frac{12 \times 1000}{800} = 15 \,\text{per } 1000 \text{ employees}
$$

> **Interpretation:** FR = 7.5 indicates moderate injury frequency. Benchmark: Indian manufacturing average is ~5–10; construction ~10–20. The factory is at the higher end and needs improvement.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hazard vs. Risk Decision Framework

| Decision Criterion | Pure Hazard Focus | Pure Risk Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Asked** | "What can harm?" | "How likely and how bad?" |
| **Output Type** | List of hazards | Risk-ranked list with scores |
| **Resource Allocation** | Equal to all | Prioritized by risk score |
| **Engineering Goal** | Elimination | Reduction to ALARP |
| **Regulatory Basis** | OHSAS 18001, ISO 45001 | ISO 31000, ALARP, QRA |
| **Use Case** | Initial brainstorming | Detailed assessment & design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** A complete safety analysis requires **both** hazard identification (qualitative) **and** risk assessment (semi-quantitative/quantitative). The board expects students to present both layers in their answers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Hazard–Risk Relationship Model (Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Source] --> B[Initiating Event]
    C[Exposure Conditions] --> B
    D[System Vulnerabilities] --> B
    B --> E{Controls Effective?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Incident Contained Near-Miss]
    E -->|No| G[Incident Occurs]
    G --> H[Consequence Realized]
    H --> I[Injury]
    H --> J[Property Damage]
    H --> K[Environmental Harm]
    H --> L[Production Loss]
    A1[Energy] --> A
    A2[Substance] --> A
    A3[Machinery] --> A
    A4[Process] --> A
    A5[Human Factor] --> A
    F --> M[Learn and Improve]
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Updated Controls]
    N --> B
```

> The diagram shows the **complete hazard-to-outcome chain** and the **feedback loop** of learning from incidents. This is the foundational mental model KTU expects students to draw in 14-mark questions.

## 4.2 The Bow-Tie Risk Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Threats / Causes] --> B[Hazardous Event]
    B --> C[Consequences / Outcomes]
    P1[Preventive Barrier 1 Elimination] --> A
    P2[Preventive Barrier 2 Engineering] --> A
    P3[Preventive Barrier 3 Administrative] --> A
    M1[Mitigative Barrier 1 Detection] --> C
    M2[Mitigative Barrier 2 Emergency Response] --> C
    M3[Mitigative Barrier 3 Recovery] --> C
    A --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> B
    B --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> C
```

> The **Bow-Tie** is the standard visual model linking **cause-side barriers** (prevention) and **consequence-side barriers** (mitigation). It is widely used in oil & gas and process safety, and KTU expects students to draw this for 7–14 mark questions.

## 4.3 Risk Assessment Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: System Definition] --> S2[Step 2: Hazard Identification]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Consequence Analysis]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Probability Estimation]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Risk Calculation R = P x C x E]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Risk Evaluation vs Criteria]
    S6 --> S7{Acceptable?}
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Document and Monitor]
    S7 -->|No| S9[Identify Risk Reduction Measures]
    S9 --> S10[Apply Hierarchy of Controls]
    S10 --> S11[Re-assess Residual Risk]
    S11 --> S6
    S8 --> S12[Periodic Review]
    S12 --> S2
```

> This is the **iterative risk management cycle** as per ISO 31000. KTU Module 2 questions often test this full flow.

## 4.4 Hierarchy of Controls (Inverted Pyramid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Most Effective Elimination] --> L2[Substitution]
    L2 --> L3[Engineering Controls]
    L3 --> L4[Administrative Controls]
    L4 --> L5[Least Effective PPE]
    N1[Best Practice] -.-> L1
    N2[Last Resort] -.-> L5
```

> **Note:** The hierarchy is presented in a top-down flow but visually represents an **inverted pyramid** — the most effective (elimination) is at the top with the widest base, the least effective (PPE) is at the bottom narrow tip.

## 4.5 Risk Matrix Block Architecture (5x5 Grid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Rare 1] --> M1
    L2[Unlikely 2] --> M2
    L3[Possible 3] --> M3
    L4[Likely 4] --> M4
    L5[Almost Certain 5] --> M5
    M1[C1: 1=Low / 2=Low / 3=Low / 4=Med / 5=Med]
    M2[C2: 2=Low / 3=Med / 4=Med / 5=High]
    M3[C3: 3=Med / 4=Med / 5=High / 6=High]
    M4[C4: 4=Med / 5=High / 6=High / 7=High]
    M5[C5: 5=Med / 6=High / 7=Very High / 8=Very High]
```
> [!NOTE]
> The above is a **functional block representation** of how Likelihood (rows) and Consequence (columns) intersect to produce the Risk Score. For a printed matrix table, students should reproduce the classic 5x5 grid in their answer sheets.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the term "Hazard" and "Risk" with one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Hazard:** A hazard is any source, situation, or act with the potential to cause harm in terms of human injury or ill-health, damage to property, damage to the environment, or a combination of these. **(1.5 marks)**
  - *Industrial Example:* A rotating unguarded gear in a gearbox is a mechanical hazard. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Risk:** Risk is the combination of the likelihood of an occurrence of a hazardous event or exposure and the severity of injury or ill-health that can be caused by that event or exposure. **(1 mark)**
  - *Example:* The risk of a worker getting entangled in the gear is HIGH if it is unguarded and operated daily; LOW if a guard is fitted and interlocks are functional. *(Self-explanatory, 0.5 mark credit included in the definition)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List the five categories of industrial hazards and give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Physical Hazard** – e.g., excessive noise from a forging hammer. **(0.6 mark)**
2. **Chemical Hazard** – e.g., exposure to sulfuric acid fumes. **(0.6 mark)**
3. **Biological Hazard** – e.g., exposure to Legionella bacteria in cooling towers. **(0.6 mark)**
4. **Ergonomic Hazard** – e.g., repetitive wrist motion leading to Carpal Tunnel Syndrome. **(0.6 mark)**
5. **Mechanical Hazard** – e.g., exposed rotating shaft. **(0.6 mark)**

*(Psychosocial and Electrical hazards may also be listed as alternatives. 0.6 marks each, total = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **HIRA (Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment)**. List the steps involved in conducting a HIRA study in a manufacturing industry, with a focus on a **lathe machine operation**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A maintenance task involves working at height (8 m) on a structural steel platform. The safety officer evaluates:
- Likelihood = 3 (Possible)
- Severity = 5 (Catastrophic — fall from height, likely fatal)

Compute the risk score, classify the risk, and recommend appropriate controls using the **Hierarchy of Controls**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — HIRA for Lathe Operation [7 marks]

**Definition of HIRA [1 mark]:**
HIRA is a systematic, structured process used to identify hazards, assess the associated risks, and determine the necessary control measures to reduce risks to an acceptable (ALARP) level. It is the foundation of any Occupational Health and Safety Management System (OHSMS) per ISO 45001.

**Steps in HIRA for a Lathe Machine Operation [6 marks]:**

1. **Step 1: Define the Scope and Task Breakdown (1 mark)**
   - Identify the lathe operation: turning, facing, threading of metal workpieces.
   - List the workpieces, tools (HSS, carbide inserts), operating speeds, and personnel involved.

2. **Step 2: Hazard Identification (1.5 marks)**
   - Mechanical Hazards: Rotating chuck, exposed moving parts, sharp cutting tool, flying chips/swarf.
   - Physical Hazards: Noise (≥ 85 dB), vibration, dust exposure.
   - Chemical Hazards: Cutting fluid mist (lubricants, coolants).
   - Ergonomic Hazards: Awkward posture, repetitive motion, manual handling of heavy workpieces.
   - Electrical Hazards: Faulty wiring, improper earthing.

3. **Step 3: Risk Assessment (1.5 marks)**
   - For each hazard, assign likelihood (1–5) and severity (1–5) scores.
   - Example: Rotating chuck entanglement → L = 4, S = 5, R = 20 (Very High).
   - Calculate risk score = Likelihood × Severity.

4. **Step 4: Identify Existing Controls (0.5 mark)**
   - Document current controls: chuck guard, coolant system, PPE (goggles, gloves), training.

5. **Step 5: Recommend Additional Controls (1 mark)**
   - Apply hierarchy: engineering (interlock guard, emergency stop, chip shield), administrative (SOP, training), PPE.

6. **Step 6: Documentation, Communication, and Review (0.5 mark)**
   - Maintain HIRA register, communicate to operators, schedule periodic review (annually or after incidents).

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Risk Matrix Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Likelihood (L) = 3
- Severity (S) = 5

**Step 1: Calculate Risk Score (1 mark)**
$$
R = L \times S = 3 \times 5 = 15
$$

**Step 2: Risk Classification (1 mark)**
$$
R = 15 \in [10, 15] \Rightarrow \textbf{High Risk (Substantial)}
$$

**Step 3: Action Required (1 mark)**
> Senior management attention required; implement controls **ASAP** before resuming work.

**Step 4: Hierarchy of Controls Recommendation (4 marks)**

| Hierarchy Level | Control Recommended for Height Work |
|---|---|
| **1. Elimination** | Can the work be done from the ground level? (e.g., pre-fabrication at ground level, then lifting as a unit) |
| **2. Substitution** | Use mechanical lifts / scissor lifts instead of scaffolding for short-duration work. |
| **3. Engineering Controls** | Install guardrails (top rail at 1.0 m, mid-rail at 0.5 m, toe-board) on the platform. Use safety nets below. Anchor points for fall arrest systems. |
| **4. Administrative Controls** | Permit-to-Work system for height work. Toolbox talk before work begins. Two-person buddy rule. Restricted access. |
| **5. PPE** | Full-body harness with shock-absorbing lanyard attached to a certified anchor point above shoulder height. Safety helmet with chinstrap. Non-slip safety boots. |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The work cannot proceed at this risk level without the controls above. The fall from 8 m has a high probability of fatality; controls must be implemented in order of the hierarchy, with PPE as a last line of defense.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | Module 2]**

**(a)** What is the **ALARP Principle**? Explain with a suitable industrial example how the cost-benefit balance is evaluated under ALARP. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical plant has the following incident data for the year 2024:
- Fatalities: 1
- Lost-time injuries: 8
- Minor injuries: 25
- Near-misses reported: 450
- Total man-hours worked = $2 \times 10^6$ hours
- Average number of employees = 1000

Compute the **Frequency Rate, Severity Rate, Incidence Rate, and FAR**. Comment on the plant's safety performance compared to industry benchmarks. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — ALARP Principle [7 marks]

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
ALARP stands for **"As Low As Reasonably Practicable."** It is the principle that the residual risk of a hazardous activity should be reduced to a level where the cost of further risk reduction is **grossly disproportionate** to the additional safety benefit obtained. It originates from UK case law (Edwards v. National Coal Board, 1949) and is enshrined in safety regulations worldwide.

**Industrial Example: Use of Benzene in a Solvent Plant (5.5 marks)**

Benzene is a known **human carcinogen** (Leukemia risk). The plant wishes to reduce worker exposure.

**Step 1: Identify the Hazard and Initial Risk (0.5 mark)**
- Hazard: Benzene vapor inhalation.
- Current exposure: 5 ppm (TLV-TWA = 1 ppm).
- Gross Risk: HIGH (4.5× above TLV).

**Step 2: Identify Risk Reduction Options (1.5 marks)**

| Option | Control | Cost (₹) | Risk Reduction (Δ fatalities/yr) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Replace benzene with toluene (substitution) | 50 Lakhs | 0.3 |
| B | Closed-loop system with vapor recovery (engineering) | 2 Crore | 0.2 |
| C | Local exhaust ventilation + PPE | 20 Lakhs | 0.05 |
| D | Administrative controls only | 5 Lakhs | 0.01 |

**Step 3: Apply the ALARP Cost Test (2 marks)**

Using VSL = ₹ 1 Crore per statistical life saved, and Gross Disproportion Factor (GDF) = 3:

For Option A (substitution):
- Benefit = $0.3 \times 1\,\text{Cr} = 30\,\text{Lakhs}$
- Max Justifiable Cost = $3 \times 30\,\text{Lakhs} = 90\,\text{Lakhs}$
- Actual Cost = ₹ 50 Lakhs < 90 Lakhs → **ALARP-required** ✓

For Option B (engineering):
- Benefit = $0.2 \times 1\,\text{Cr} = 20\,\text{Lakhs}$
- Max Justifiable Cost = $3 \times 20 = 60\,\text{Lakhs}$
- Actual Cost = ₹ 2 Crore >> 60 Lakhs → **NOT ALARP-required** (disproportionate)

**Step 4: Conclusion (1.5 marks)**
The plant should implement **Option A (substitution)** and **Option C (LEV + PPE)** to bring the risk to ALARP. Option B is too costly relative to the marginal benefit. This demonstrates how ALARP balances safety, cost, and practicality rather than demanding zero risk.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Safety Performance Metrics [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- Fatalities = 1
- Lost-time injuries (LTI) = 8
- Minor injuries = 25
- Near-misses = 450
- Total man-hours = $2 \times 10^6$ hours
- Employees = 1000

**Step 1: Frequency Rate (FR) — Using LTI count (1.5 marks)**
$$
\text{FR} = \frac{\text{Number of LTI} \times 10^6}{\text{Man-hours worked}} = \frac{8 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^6} = 4
$$

**Step 2: Severity Rate (SR) — Assume 30 days lost per LTI (1.5 marks)**
Assuming average 30 days lost per LTI: Total days lost = $8 \times 30 = 240$ days
$$
\text{SR} = \frac{\text{Days lost} \times 10^6}{\text{Man-hours worked}} = \frac{240 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^6} = 120
$$

**Step 3: Incidence Rate (IR) (1 mark)**
$$
\text{IR} = \frac{\text{LTI} \times 10^3}{\text{Number of employees}} = \frac{8 \times 1000}{1000} = 8 \,\text{per 1000 employees}
$$

**Step 4: Fatal Accident Rate (FAR) (1 mark)**
$$
\text{FAR} = \frac{\text{Fatalities} \times 10^8}{\text{Man-hours worked}} = \frac{1 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^6} = 50
$$

**Step 5: Industry Benchmark Comparison and Comments (2 marks)**

| Metric | Calculated | Industry Benchmark (Chemical) | Inference |
|---|---|---|---|
| FR | 4 | 1 – 5 | At higher end of acceptable range |
| SR | 120 | 50 – 100 | Above average; serious injury severity high |
| IR | 8 | 2 – 5 | Above benchmark; need improvement |
| FAR | 50 | 5 – 20 | **High fatality risk** (Mining-level equivalent) |

> **Conclusion:** The plant's fatality rate (FAR = 50) is alarmingly high, comparable to mining industry. The plant must urgently review its process safety management, conduct a detailed HAZOP study, and improve engineering controls. Heinrich's Ratio check: 1 fatal : 8 LTI → ratio 1:8 (close to Bird's 1:10), suggesting reporting integrity is reasonable. Near-miss reporting (450) is strong, indicating a positive safety culture that should be leveraged for preventive actions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing Hazard and Risk** (–1 to 2 marks): Writing "Hazard is the risk of injury" is incorrect. Hazard = *source of potential harm*; Risk = *likelihood × consequence*. Examiners are strict on this.
> 2. **Missing the formula units in FAR/FR/SR**: Always include "per $10^6$ (or $10^8$) person-hours" — the numerical value without units is incomplete and loses marks.
> 3. **Skipping Hierarchy of Controls order**: Writing "PPE, engineering, elimination" instead of the correct top-down order. Mark loss of 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not citing Heinrich's or Bird's ratios by name**: Always name the theorist when stating the 1:29:300 or 1:10:30:600 ratios.
> 5. **No conclusion in numerical problems**: KTU expects a concluding sentence like "the risk is unacceptable and requires immediate control" or "the FAR indicates high-risk performance."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module 2 Revision — Hazard and Risk**

- **Hazard** = Potential source of harm (intrinsic property, qualitative).
- **Risk** = Likelihood × Consequence (quantitative/qualitative measure).
- **Core Equation:** $R = P \times C \times E$ (with exposure) or $R = P \times C$ (basic).
- **Risk Matrix:** 5×5 grid → Score 1–25. Categories: Low (1–4), Medium (5–9), High (10–15), Very High (16–25).
- **Hierarchy of Controls (Top → Bottom):** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- **ALARP:** As Low As Reasonably Practicable — cost of further reduction must be grossly disproportionate to the benefit.
- **Tolerable Risk:** Acceptable level of risk for a given industrial context; never zero.
- **Heinrich's Ratio:** 1 : 29 : 300 (Major : Minor : Near-miss).
- **Bird's Ratio:** 1 : 10 : 30 : 600 (Major : Minor : Damage : Near-miss).
- **FAR Formula:** $\text{FAR} = \frac{\text{Fatalities} \times 10^8}{\text{Exposure hours}}$.
- **FR Formula:** $\text{FR} = \frac{\text{Injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$.
- **SR Formula:** $\text{SR} = \frac{\text{Days lost} \times 10^6}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$.
- **IR Formula:** $\text{IR} = \frac{\text{Injuries} \times 1000}{\text{Number of employees}}$.
- **HIRA Steps:** Define scope → Identify hazards → Assess risk → Existing controls → Recommend controls → Document & review.
- **Bow-Tie Model:** Preventive barriers (cause side) + Mitigative barriers (consequence side) around a central hazardous event.
- **Hazard Categories:** Physical, Chemical, Biological, Mechanical, Electrical, Fire/Explosion, Ergonomic, Psychosocial.
- **KTU Mandate:** Always pair hazard identification (qualitative) with risk assessment (semi-quantitative) in answers.
- **Diagram:** Be able to draw the Hazard–Risk continuum, Bow-Tie, and Risk Matrix in any 14-mark question.
- **Numerical:** Always show units ($10^6$ person-hours, $10^8$, per 1000) and end with a benchmark comparison.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Chemical Hazards and their Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET637 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Types of Chemical Hazards and their Control ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **chemical hazard** is any chemical substance, compound, mixture, or process derivative that has the potential to cause harm to human health (acute or chronic), physical assets, or the environment when released, mishandled, stored, or processed improperly in an industrial setting. In the context of **Industrial Safety Engineering (PEMET637)**, chemical hazards are systematically classified based on their **physical state, physiological action, chemical reactivity, and fire/explosion potential**, in accordance with international frameworks such as **OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard (HCS), GHS (Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals), NFPA 704 Diamond, and ACGIH Threshold Limit Values (TLVs)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes the **taxonomy of chemical hazards** (dusts, fumes, vapors, gases, mists, and their derivatives) and the **hierarchy of control measures** — substitution, engineering controls, administrative controls, and PPE — that an industrial safety engineer must design, audit, and enforce. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Intruder" Imagine your workplace is a **locked house**, and chemical hazards are **uninvited guests** of various temperaments: - Some guests (**gases**) silently slip under the door and fill every room (asphyxiants like nitrogen displacing oxygen). - Some guests (**vapors**) evaporate from spilled liquids and form an invisible combustible cloud above the floor (like gasoline vapors). - Some guests (**dusts**) hide in the air as fine powder, waiting for a single spark to ignite them (grain silo explosions). - Some guests (**corrosives**) eat through your walls, your pipes, and your skin (acids like sulfuric acid). - Some guests (**carcinogens**) do not harm you today but plan a silent attack years later (benzene causing leukemia). **Industrial Safety Engineering** is the discipline of identifying these guests before they enter, monitoring their presence, restricting their access, and providing protective gear if they must be present. Just as a homeowner installs alarms, locks, and fire extinguishers, an industrial safety engineer designs **ventilation, enclosure, substitution, MSDS protocols, and PPE**. ### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Reality:** According to **OSHA**, chemical exposure accounts for approximately **190,000 illnesses and 50,000 deaths annually** worldwide in occupational settings. **NFPA** estimates that improper chemical storage is a root cause in nearly **30% of industrial fires**. For KTU 2024, every module test and the End Semester Exam (ESE) frames chemical hazards as a **design-time engineering decision**, not merely an operational afterthought. ### 1.4 Primary Classification Framework Chemical hazards are bifurcated into two macro-categories: | Macro-Class | Description | Engineering Significance | |-------------|-------------|--------------------------| | **Health Hazards** | Cause biological/systemic damage to humans | Dictate **TLV, PEL, IDLH, PPE rating** | | **Physical/Chemical Hazards** | Cause fire, explosion, or violent reaction | Dictate **ventilation, zoning, bonding & grounding** | The detailed taxonomy within these two macro-classes is treated in Section 2. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of Chemical Hazards Chemical hazards are sub-classified by **physical state**, **route of entry**, and **toxicological effect**. #### 2.1.1 Hazard by Physical State **(a) Dusts** - Solid particles generated by **mechanical processes** — grinding, drilling, crushing, blasting, sanding. - Particle size typically **< 100 µm**; respirable fraction is **< 10 µm**. - Examples: silica dust (silicosis), coal dust (pneumoconiosis), wood dust (nasal carcinoma), cotton dust (byssinosis). **(b) Fumes** - Solid particles formed by **condensation of vaporized material** (typically metals) after volatilization from the parent material, often during welding or smelting. - Particle size usually **< 1 µm** — penetrates deep into alveoli. - Example: welding fumes (Fe₂O₃, MnO₂ → **metal fume fever**), lead fumes, zinc fumes. **(c) Vapors** - **Gaseous phase of a substance that is liquid at normal temperature and pressure (NTP)**. - Generated by evaporation from solvents, fuels, and cleaning agents. - Examples: toluene vapor, benzene vapor, mercury vapor. **(d) Gases** - Substances that exist in **gaseous state at NTP** and can change state only by pressure/temperature variation. - Examples: CO, H₂S, Cl₂, NH₃, O₂ (deficiency), N₂ (asphyxiant). **(e) Mists** - **Liquid droplets suspended in air**, generated by atomization, condensation, or splashing. - Examples: acid mists (H₂SO₄ mist), paint spray mists, oil mists. **(f) Smoke** - **Carbonaceous particulate matter** from incomplete combustion. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (KTU-favorite question):** A *vapor* is the gas-phase of a substance that is *normally liquid*; a *gas* is a substance that is *normally gaseous* at room temperature. The same molecule (e.g., water) can be a vapor (boiling water in a lab) or a condensate depending on conditions. #### 2.1.2 Hazard by Health Effect | Category | Mechanism | Examples | Target Organ | |----------|-----------|----------|--------------| | **Irritants** | Inflammatory response on contact | Cl₂, NH₃, SO₂, ozone | Skin, eyes, lungs | | **Asphyxiants (Simple)** | Displace O₂ below 19.5% | N₂, CO₂, methane | CNS (hypoxia) | | **Asphyxiants (Chemical)** | Block O₂ utilization | CO (binds Hb), HCN | Blood/CNS | | **Narcotics/Anesthetics** | CNS depression | toluene, xylene, acetone | CNS | | **Carcinogens** | Cellular DNA damage | benzene, vinyl chloride, asbestos | Multi-organ | | **Hepatotoxins** | Liver damage | CCl₄, vinyl chloride | Liver | | **Nephrotoxins** | Kidney damage | Cd, Hg, lead | Kidney | | **Neurotoxins** | Nerve damage | Mn, Pb, Hg, n-hexane | CNS/PNS | | **Sensitizers** | Allergic immune response | TDI, formaldehyde, latex | Skin/lungs | | **Corrosives** | Tissue destruction by chemical reaction | H₂SO₄, NaOH, HF | Skin/eyes/lungs | | **Systemic Toxins** | Distributed via bloodstream | Pb, Hg, As | Multi-organ | #### 2.1.3 Hazard by Fire/Explosion Properties | Class | Definition | Indicator | |-------|-----------|-----------| | **Flammable Liquid** | Flash point < 37.8 °C (100 °F) | Class IA, IB, IC | | **Combustible Liquid** | Flash point ≥ 37.8 °C | Class II, IIIA, IIIB | | **Flammable Gas** | Gas with flammable range in air at 20 °C | H₂, CH₄, propane | | **Oxidizer** | Yields O₂, intensifies fire | H₂O₂, KMnO₄, nitrates | | **Pyrophoric** | Spontaneously ignites in air < 55 °C | P (white), silane | | **Water-Reactive** | Emits flammable gas on water contact | Na, CaC₂, LiAlH₄ | | **Unstable/Reactive** | Polymerizes, decomposes, or explodes | peroxides, picric acid, TNT | | **Explosive** | Detonates via shock/friction | nitroglycerin, ammonium perchlorate | ### 2.2 The Hierarchy of Controls (Engineering-Centric) The **Hierarchy of Controls** is the cornerstone engineering framework adopted by **NIOSH, OSHA, and KTU 2024 Module 2**. It is depicted as an inverted triangle, with the **most effective** controls at the top. 1. **Elimination** — Remove the hazard entirely (process redesign). 2. **Substitution** — Replace with a less hazardous substance (e.g., water-based paint replacing solvent-based). 3. **Engineering Controls** — Physical modifications: ventilation, enclosure, isolation, interlocks. 4. **Administrative Controls** — Procedures, training, job rotation, signage, MSDS. 5. **PPE** — Last line of defense: respirators, gloves, goggles, suits. ### 2.3 Threshold Limit Values (TLV) — The Quantitative Backbone These values, published by the **ACGIH (American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists)**, are central to KTU numerical problems. | Acronym | Full Form | Definition | Units | |---------|-----------|------------|-------| | **TLV-TWA** | Time-Weighted Average | 8-hr/day, 40-hr/wk exposure limit | ppm or mg/m³ | | **TLV-STEL** | Short-Term Exposure Limit | 15-min average, max 4x/day, ≥ 60 min between | ppm or mg/m³ | | **TLV-C** | Ceiling Limit | Absolute upper limit not to be exceeded | ppm or mg/m³ | | **PEL** | Permissible Exposure Limit (OSHA) | Legal 8-hr TWA | ppm or mg/m³ | | **IDLH** | Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health | Max airborne conc. for 30-min escape | ppm or mg/m³ | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units | |---|-------------------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{22.4 \times C_{mg/m^3}}{MW}$ at 25 °C, 1 atm | ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversion | dimensionless | | 2 | $TLV_{mixture} = \dfrac{C_1 + C_2 + \dots}{\dfrac{C_1}{TLV_1} + \dfrac{C_2}{TLV_2} + \dots}$ where $\sum \dfrac{C_i}{TLV_i} > 1$ ⇒ overexposure | Additive mixture formula | dimensionless ratio | | 3 | $IDLH = 10 \times TLV\text{-}TWA$ (approx., ACGIH rule of thumb) | For many chemicals | ppm | | 4 | $LEL$ and $UEL$ define **Flammable Range** | Lower/Upper Explosive Limits | % by volume | | 5 | $Q = A \times V$ | Volumetric flow rate for general ventilation | m³/s or CFM | | 6 | $Q_{local} = V_c \times A_{face}$ | Local exhaust ventilation: capture velocity × hood face area | m³/s | | 7 | $ACH = \dfrac{Q}{V_{room}} \times 60$ | Air Changes per Hour | hr⁻¹ | | 8 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}(1 + K_L)$ | Hood static pressure loss | Pa | | 9 | $V_c = \dfrac{V_x}{10\,(X)^{1.5}}$ | Dalla Valle's equation for capture velocity at distance X (for slot hoods) | m/s | | 10 | $ER = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{PEL}{1000}} \approx \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{TLV}{1000}}$ | Engineering reduction ratio for PPE selection | % | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note:** The ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversion assumes **25 °C and 1 atm**. For other conditions, the molar volume **22.4 L/mol** must be replaced by $\dfrac{RT}{P}$ in appropriate units. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: ppm ↔ mg/m³ Conversion Formula This is one of the **highest-yield derivations** in KTU Industrial Safety numericals. **Statement:** Convert an airborne concentration $C_{ppm}$ (parts per million by volume) into $C_{mg/m^3}$ given the molecular weight $MW$ at **25 °C, 1 atm**. **Step 1 — Definition of ppm (volumetric).** $$C_{ppm} = \dfrac{V_{contaminant}}{V_{air}} \times 10^6$$ **Step 2 — Apply Ideal Gas Law at 25 °C (298 K), 1 atm.** Using $PV = nRT$, the molar volume is: $$V_m = \dfrac{RT}{P} = \dfrac{0.0821 \times 298}{1} = 24.47 \text{ L/mol} \approx 24.45 \text{ L/mol}$$ **Step 3 — Express 1 ppm as a volume fraction.** $$1 \text{ ppm} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol contaminant per mol air}$$ **Step 4 — Convert molar volume to standard 1 m³ reference.** $$1 \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ L}$$ So, in 1 m³ of air at 25 °C: $$n_{air} = \dfrac{1000}{24.45} = 40.9 \text{ mol air}$$ **Step 5 — Moles of contaminant in 1 m³ at 1 ppm.** $$n_{cont} = 40.9 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol}$$ **Step 6 — Convert moles to mass using molecular weight $MW$.** $$m_{cont} = 40.9 \times 10^{-6} \times MW \text{ g/m}^3$$ **Step 7 — Generalize.** $$C_{mg/m^3} = \dfrac{C_{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}$$ $$C_{ppm} = \dfrac{24.45 \times C_{mg/m^3}}{MW}$$ > [!NOTE] > The constant **24.45** arises from the molar volume at 25 °C. Some textbooks use **24.47** or **22.4** (at STP, 0 °C). Always state the reference temperature in KTU solutions. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Additive Mixture TLV Formula **Scenario:** When a worker is exposed to multiple airborne chemicals with **similar toxicological action**, their effects are considered additive. **Step 1 — Define partial fraction for each chemical.** $$f_i = \dfrac{C_i}{TLV_i}$$ **Step 2 — The mixture is considered safe if and only if the sum of fractions ≤ 1.** $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{TLV_i} \leq 1$$ **Step 3 — The combined TLV is the airborne concentration of the mixture that satisfies:** $$\dfrac{C_{mixture}}{TLV_{mixture}} = \sum \dfrac{C_i}{TLV_i} = 1$$ **Step 4 — Rearranged explicitly.** $$TLV_{mixture} = \dfrac{\sum C_i}{\sum \dfrac{C_i}{TLV_i}}$$ **Step 5 — Example application (worked into KTU questions).** Given three solvents with $C_1 = 100$ ppm, $TLV_1 = 200$ ppm; $C_2 = 50$ ppm, $TLV_2 = 100$ ppm; $C_3 = 25$ ppm, $TLV_3 = 50$ ppm: $$\sum \dfrac{C_i}{TLV_i} = \dfrac{100}{200} + \dfrac{50}{100} + \dfrac{25}{50} = 0.50 + 0.50 + 0.50 = 1.50$$ Since $1.50 > 1$, the mixture **exceeds the TLV** and the workplace is non-compliant. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) — Capture Velocity For a **slot hood** at distance $X$ from a contaminant source, the **Dalla Valle equation** gives the capture velocity required to entrain the contaminant. **Step 1 — Empirical relation (Dalla Valle, 1932):** $$V_c = \dfrac{V_x}{10 \, X^{1.5}}$$ where $V_x$ is the centerline velocity at distance $X$ (in feet), required to be **≥ 100 ft/min** for effective capture. **Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate through the slot hood:** $$Q = V_c \times A_{face} = V_c \times (W \times L)$$ **Step 3 — Static pressure loss across the hood (hood entry loss):** $$\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho \, Q^2}{2 \, C_c^2 \, A_{face}^2}$$ where $C_c$ is the **coefficient of contraction** ($\approx 0.65$ for sharp-edged slots). **Step 4 — Total system pressure:** $$P_{total} = \Delta P_{hood} + \Delta P_{duct} + \Delta P_{filter} + \Delta P_{fan}$$ **Step 5 — Fan power:** $$W_{fan} = \dfrac{Q \times P_{total}}{\eta_{fan}}$$ This complete chain forms the basis of **LEV design problems** in KTU exams. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Right Control | Hazard Type | Primary Control | Secondary Control | Verification Metric | |-------------|-----------------|-------------------|---------------------| | Dust (inorganic, e.g., silica) | Wet suppression, LEV | PPE (P100 respirator) | $C_{air} \leq TLV$ | | Vapor (organic solvent) | LEV + general exhaust | Substituting water-based | $LEL \geq 25\%$ LEL | | Gas (toxic, e.g., Cl₂) | Enclosed process, SCBA | Gas detection alarms | $C_{air} \leq IDLH/10$ | | Mist (acid) | LEV with mist eliminator | Corrosion-resistant PPE | pH monitoring | | Fume (welding) | LEV at source, fume extractor | Local shielding, PPE | $Mn \leq 0.5 mg/m^3$ | | Corrosive (skin contact) | Enclosed handling, splash guards | Chemical-resistant PPE | pH, glove breakthrough | | Carcinogen (benzene) | Closed-loop process | SCBA, full encapsulating suit | $C_{air} \leq 1 ppm$ (PEL) | | Pyrophoric (white P) | Inert atmosphere glove box | Flame-resistant PPE | O₂ < 5% in enclosure | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hierarchy of Controls for Chemical Hazards ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hazard Identified via MSDS SDS and GHS Label] --> B{Risk Assessment
ACGIH TLV Comparison} B -->|Exceeds TLV| C[Apply Hierarchy of Controls] B -->|Within TLV| M[Monitor with Air Sampling] C --> D[Level 1 Elimination
Process Redesign] D --> E[Level 2 Substitution
Replace with Safer Chemical] E --> F[Level 3 Engineering Controls] F --> F1[Enclosure and Isolation] F --> F2[Local Exhaust Ventilation LEV] F --> F3[General Dilution Ventilation] F --> F4[Wet Suppression for Dust] F --> F5[Inert Atmosphere Blanketing] F1 --> G[Level 4 Administrative Controls] F2 --> G F3 --> G F4 --> G F5 --> G G --> G1[MSDS Communication and Training] G --> G2[Job Rotation to Limit Exposure Time] G --> G3[Signage and Zoning per NFPA 704] G1 --> H[Level 5 PPE Last Line of Defense] G2 --> H G3 --> H H --> H1[Respirators SCBA APR PAPR] H --> H2[Chemical Resistant Gloves and Suits] H --> H3[Eye and Face Protection] H1 --> I[Verification Air Monitoring and Medical Surveillance] H2 --> I H3 --> I I --> J{Compliant with TLV PEL and IDLH} J -->|Yes| K[Sustained Operation] J -->|No| B M --> B ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Chemical Hazard Classification Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR CH[Chemical Hazards] --> H[Health Hazards] CH --> P[Physical and Reactivity Hazards] H --> H1[Dusts Silica Coal Wood] H --> H2[Fumes Welding Metal] H --> H3[Vapors Solvents Benzene] H --> H4[Gases CO H2S Cl2] H --> H5[Mists Acid Spray] H --> H6[Carcinogens Benzene Asbestos] H --> H7[Corrosives H2SO4 NaOH] H --> H8[Neurotoxins Pb Hg Mn] P --> P1[Flammable Liquids Class I] P --> P2[Flammable Gases H2 CH4] P --> P3[Oxidizers H2O2 KMnO4] P --> P4[Pyrophorics White P Silane] P --> P5[Water Reactive Na CaC2] P --> P6[Explosives TNT Nitroglycerin] P --> P7[Unstable Reactives Organic Peroxides] ``` ### 4.3 Schematic: Closed-Loop Local Exhaust Ventilation System ```mermaid flowchart LR S[Contaminant Source
e.g. Solvent Tank] -->|Emission Plume| H[Hood or Enclosure
Capture Velocity Vc] H -->|Duct Flow Q m3 per s| D[Ductwork
with Transport Velocity
18 to 20 m per s] D --> A[Air Cleaning Device
Scrubber or HEPA or Carbon] A -->|Filtered Air| F[Exhaust Fan
with Static Pressure P] F -->|Discharge| O[Atmospheric Stack
Height H per IS 4326] A -->|Recycle Option| R[Recycled Clean Air to Workplace] R --> S H -.Capture check.-> S A -.Efficiency check.-> R ``` ### 4.4 Diagram: NFPA 704 Hazard Diamond Layout ```mermaid flowchart TB NF[NFPA 704 Diamond] --> TOP[Top Blue
Health Hazard
0 to 4 Scale] NF --> LEFT[Left Red
Flammability
0 to 4 Scale] NF --> RIGHT[Right Yellow
Reactivity or Instability
0 to 4 Scale] NF --> BOTTOM[Bottom White
Special Hazards
OX Oxidizer
W Water Reactive
SA Simple Asphyxiant
COR Corrosive
RAD Radiation] TOP --> T0[0 No Hazard] TOP --> T1[1 Slight] TOP --> T2[2 Moderate] TOP --> T3[3 Serious] TOP --> T4[4 Deadly] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define the term "chemical hazard" and list any four types of chemical hazards based on physical state.** **Model Answer:** A chemical hazard is any chemical substance or process derivative that has the potential to cause harm to life, health, property, or the environment. Four types based on physical state are: 1. **Dusts** — solid particles from mechanical operations (e.g., silica, coal dust). 2. **Fumes** — fine solid particles from condensation of vaporized material (e.g., welding fumes). 3. **Vapors** — gaseous phase of a normally liquid substance (e.g., toluene vapor). 4. **Gases** — substances normally gaseous at NTP (e.g., CO, H₂S, Cl₂). --- #### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional II]** **Differentiate between TLV-TWA and TLV-STEL.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | TLV-TWA | TLV-STEL | |-----------|---------|----------| | Full Form | Time-Weighted Average | Short-Term Exposure Limit | | Duration | 8 hours/day, 40 hours/week | 15-minute average | | Frequency | Continuous exposure basis | Maximum 4 times/day with ≥ 60-min gap | | Purpose | Chronic exposure protection | Acute excursion protection | | Legal Basis | ACGIH recommendation | ACGIH recommendation | --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, ESE Module 2]** **(a)** Classify chemical hazards in detail based on (i) physical state and (ii) physiological/health effects. Provide at least two examples for each category. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A worker is exposed to a mixture of three organic solvents. Solvent A: $C_A = 80$ ppm, $TLV_A = 200$ ppm. Solvent B: $C_B = 40$ ppm, $TLV_B = 100$ ppm. Solvent C: $C_C = 20$ ppm, $TLV_C = 50$ ppm. Determine whether the exposure is within permissible limits using the additive mixture formula. If not, what control measures would you recommend? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Classification:** *(i) By physical state:* - **Dusts** — silica dust (silicosis), coal dust (pneumoconiosis). - **Fumes** — welding fumes (metal fume fever), lead fumes. - **Vapors** — benzene vapor (leukemia), acetone vapor (CNS depression). - **Gases** — CO (chemical asphyxiant), Cl₂ (irritant). - **Mists** — H₂SO₄ mist (respiratory irritant), oil mist. *(ii) By physiological action:* - **Irritants** — Cl₂, NH₃, ozone (target: respiratory tract). - **Asphyxiants** — N₂ (simple), CO (chemical). - **Carcinogens** — benzene, vinyl chloride, asbestos. - **Neurotoxins** — Mn, Pb, n-hexane. - **Hepatotoxins** — CCl₄, vinyl chloride. - **Sensitizers** — TDI, formaldehyde. **(b) Additive mixture calculation:** $$\sum \dfrac{C_i}{TLV_i} = \dfrac{80}{200} + \dfrac{40}{100} + \dfrac{20}{50}$$ $$= 0.40 + 0.40 + 0.40 = 1.20$$ Since the sum **1.20 > 1**, the exposure **exceeds permissible limits** by **20%**. **Recommended controls:** 1. **Engineering** — Install LEV at solvent use points; ensure capture velocity ≥ 100 ft/min. 2. **Substitution** — Replace the highest contributor (each solvent contributes equally) with a lower-toxicity alternative. 3. **Administrative** — Reduce exposure time via job rotation; enforce MSDS-based training. 4. **PPE** — Use organic vapor respirators with appropriate APF (Assigned Protection Factor). 5. **Monitoring** — Conduct periodic air sampling to verify compliance post-control. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, ESE Module 2 — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Explain the Hierarchy of Controls for chemical hazards with a neat sketch. Why is PPE considered the last line of defense? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A workplace has an airborne concentration of toluene at 150 mg/m³. Given the molecular weight of toluene as 92 g/mol and TLV-TWA = 100 ppm, determine: (i) the equivalent concentration in ppm, and (ii) whether the exposure exceeds the TLV. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Hierarchy of Controls:** The hierarchy, in descending order of effectiveness, is: 1. **Elimination** — remove the hazard entirely. 2. **Substitution** — replace with a less hazardous substance. 3. **Engineering Controls** — LEV, enclosure, isolation, ventilation. 4. **Administrative Controls** — procedures, training, MSDS, rotation. 5. **PPE** — respirators, gloves, suits. PPE is the last line of defense because it **depends on human compliance**, is **susceptible to failure** (fit issues, degradation), and **does not eliminate the source** of contamination. The other controls act at the source or along the path, providing **collective protection**, whereas PPE provides only **individual protection**. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) ppm conversion** using formula $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{24.45 \times C_{mg/m^3}}{MW}$: $$C_{ppm} = \dfrac{24.45 \times 150}{92} = \dfrac{3667.5}{92} = 39.86 \text{ ppm}$$ **(ii) Comparison with TLV-TWA = 100 ppm:** $$39.86 \text{ ppm} < 100 \text{ ppm} \Rightarrow \text{exposure is within TLV.}$$ The compliance ratio is $\dfrac{39.86}{100} = 0.40$ or **40% of TLV** — well within safe limits, though **continuous air monitoring** is recommended to prevent excursions. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** > 1. **Forgetting to state reference conditions** in ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversions (must mention **25 °C, 1 atm** or the constant 24.45). > 2. **Mixing up LEL and UEL** — these are **lower** and **upper** limits of the flammable range, NOT percentages in air. A concentration below LEL is too lean; above UEL is too rich; **only between them does ignition occur**. > 3. **Confusing dust with fume** — dusts are mechanically produced; fumes are condensed from vapor. > 4. **Writing "the worker is safe" without showing the additive ratio calculation** — KTU examiners award 0 marks unless the sum $\sum \frac{C_i}{TLV_i}$ is explicitly computed and compared with 1. > 5. **Sketching the Hierarchy of Controls upside-down** — Elimination/Substitution must be at the **TOP**, PPE at the **bottom**. Reversing this forfeits up to 3 marks. > 6. **Ignoring IDLH** in numerical problems — when concentration approaches IDLH, SCBA (not APR) is mandatory. Many students miss this nuance. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Chemical hazards** are classified by **physical state** (dust, fume, vapor, gas, mist) and **physiological effect** (irritant, asphyxiant, carcinogen, neurotoxin, corrosive, sensitizer). - **Fire/explosion hazards** are governed by **LEL, UEL, flash point, auto-ignition temperature**. - **Threshold Limit Values (TLV)** — TWA, STEL, Ceiling — are the ACGIH yardsticks; **IDLH** is the OSHA/NIOSH escape limit. - **ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversion** uses the molar volume constant **24.45** at **25 °C, 1 atm**. - **Additive mixture formula**: $\sum \dfrac{C_i}{TLV_i} \leq 1$ for compliance; > 1 means overexposure. - **Hierarchy of Controls** must be applied **top-down**: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE. - **Engineering controls** include **LEV, general ventilation, enclosure, wet suppression, inert blanketing**. - **Ventilation design** hinges on **capture velocity ($V_c$), duct transport velocity (18–20 m/s), hood entry loss, and fan static pressure**. - **NFPA 704 Diamond** communicates hazards: Blue (Health), Red (Flammability), Yellow (Reactivity), White (Special). - **GHS/MSDS-SDS** is the global standard for hazard communication; KTU expects knowledge of pictograms and H/P statements. - **IDLH ≈ 10 × TLV-TWA** for many chemicals (rule of thumb). - **Closed-loop processes, SCBA, full encapsulation** are mandatory for carcinogens and IDLH atmospheres. - **Hygiene pyramid**: Substitution > Ventilation > PPE; **never reverse this in exam answers**. - **Always state assumptions**: temperature, pressure, equilibrium state for any KTU numerical.

topics.title: Fire Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET637 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fire Safety # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Fire Safety** is the systematic body of engineering principles, practices, regulatory codes, and management controls directed toward the prevention, detection, suppression, and mitigation of uncontrolled combustion processes (fires) in industrial, commercial, and residential occupancies. It encompasses the science of fire dynamics, the design of active and passive protection systems, hazard analysis, and emergency response planning. In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (Industrial Safety Engineering)**, Fire Safety is formally defined as: > *The set of measures taken to prevent fire from occurring, limit its spread, and mitigate its consequences to life, property, and the environment, achieved through a hierarchical strategy of prevention, detection, containment, suppression, and evacuation.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 - Fire Safety):** > Students must master the **fire tetrahedron**, **classification of fires (IS 2190 / NFPA 10)**, **fire load calculations**, **extinguishing media**, **hydrant and sprinkler systems**, **fire detection & alarm systems**, **building codes (NBC 2016, IS standards)**, and **emergency planning**. --- ## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Fire Safety Engineering Fire safety engineering is organized around **four hierarchical levels of defense**: 1. **Prevention (Inherent Safety)** – Eliminate ignition sources and combustible materials at the design stage. 2. **Detection & Alarm** – Early identification of incipient fires through smoke/heat/flame sensors. 3. **Containment (Passive Protection)** – Compartmentation, fire-resistant construction, and structural fire protection. 4. **Suppression (Active Protection)** – Automatic sprinklers, hydrants, extinguishers, and gaseous systems. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Birthday Candle on a Cake"** > > Imagine a birthday candle. To keep it burning, you need three things: **wax (fuel)**, **oxygen (air)**, and **a match flame (heat / ignition source)**. Remove any one of these — say, by blowing out the candle — and the fire dies instantly. > > Now think of an industrial fire. A leaking LPG pipe (fuel), a hot welding spark (ignition), and free air in a warehouse (oxygen) create the same triangle — but on a catastrophic scale. **Fire safety is simply the disciplined removal of one or more sides of this "fire triangle"** before all three ever meet, or as quickly as possible if they do. > > Modern fire science adds a fourth side: the **chain reaction of free radicals** — known as the **fire tetrahedron**. Cutting this fourth side (chemically) is how dry chemical and halon extinguishers work. --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric / Constant | Symbol | Value / Unit | Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Activation Energy | $E_a$ | $\approx 80{-}200 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Minimum energy to initiate combustion | | Stoichiometric Oxygen | $O_2$ | **21 % by volume** (air) | Required for sustained combustion | | Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) | LFL | Variable (e.g., methane = 5 %) | Minimum fuel-air ratio for combustion | | Upper Flammable Limit (UFL) | UFL | Variable (e.g., methane = 15 %) | Maximum fuel-air ratio for combustion | | Auto-Ignition Temperature | AIT | Variable (e.g., gasoline = 280 °C) | Spontaneous ignition temperature | | Fire Load | $q$ | $\text{MJ/m}^2$ | Total combustible energy per unit floor area | | Heat Release Rate | $\dot{Q}$ or HRR | **kW** or **MW** | Rate of heat generation during fire | | Specific Heat of Water | $C_p$ | **4.186 kJ/kg·K** | Basis for water-based cooling | | Latent Heat of Vaporization (water) | $L_v$ | **2260 kJ/kg** | Energy absorbed by water turning to steam | --- ## 1.5 The Fire Triangle & Fire Tetrahedron The classical **Fire Triangle** (Oxygen + Fuel + Heat) is sufficient for slow-combustion understanding. Modern fire chemistry recognizes a fourth element: **The Fire Tetrahedron** — Fuel, Oxidizer, Heat, **Uninhibited Chain Reaction**. $$\boxed{\text{Fire} = \text{Fuel} + \text{Oxidizer} + \text{Heat} + \text{Self-Sustaining Chain Reaction}}$$ Removing any one vertex causes flame extinction. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Fires (KTU High-Priority) The **KTU 2024 syllabus (and IS 2190 / NFPA 10)** classifies fires into five principal classes. This is the most frequently asked topic in Part A questions. | Class | Fuel Type | Examples | Extinguishing Medium | |---|---|---|---| | **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber | Water, foam, dry chemical (ABC) | | **Class B** | Flammable liquids & gases | Petrol, LPG, oils, solvents | Foam, CO₂, dry chemical, BCF | | **Class C** | Flammable gases (in NFPA) | LPG, methane, hydrogen | BCF, dry chemical powder | | **Class D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium, sodium, potassium, lithium | Special dry powder (NaCl, graphite) | | **Class E** (IS) / **Class C** (NFPA) | Electrical fires | Live equipment, transformers | CO₂, dry chemical (non-conductive) | | **Class K** (NFPA) | Cooking oils & fats | Vegetable oils, animal fats | Wet chemical (saponification) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap:** IS 2190 uses **Class E** for electrical fires, while NFPA 10 uses **Class C**. Always mention the standard you're referring to in the answer. --- ## 2.2 Stages of Fire Development Every compartment fire progresses through **four classical stages**: 1. **Incipient Stage** — Slow oxidation, invisible combustion products. Detected by **smoke detectors**. 2. **Growth Stage** — Flame spread over combustibles, HRR rises non-linearly. Often follows the **$t^2$ fire model**. 3. **Fully Developed Stage** — All combustibles involved; HRR is at maximum. 4. **Decay Stage** — Fuel depleted; HRR falls; smouldering reignition risk. > [!NOTE] > The transition from growth to fully-developed is called **flashover** — a critical concept in compartment fire dynamics. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table for KTU 2024 University Exam — appears every year.** | Formula | Expression | Variables / Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Fire Load** | $q = \dfrac{\sum (m_i \cdot H_i)}{A_f}$ | $q$ in $\text{MJ/m}^2$, $m_i$ = mass of combustible $i$ (kg), $H_i$ = calorific value (MJ/kg), $A_f$ = floor area (m²) | Classifies building fire severity | | **Equivalent Fire Load (Timber)** | $q_e = \dfrac{\sum (m_i H_i)}{A_f \cdot 18{,}600}$ | Divides total energy by calorific value of timber | Compares different occupancies | | **$t^2$ Fire Growth** | $\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha t^2$ | $\alpha$ = fire growth coefficient (kW/s²), $t$ = time (s) | Predicts HRR vs. time | | **Sprinkler Discharge** | $Q = K\sqrt{P}$ | $Q$ = flow (L/min), $K$ = discharge coefficient, $P$ = pressure (bar) | Hydraulic design of sprinklers | | **Hydraulic Pipe Flow (Hazen-Williams)** | $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot d^{4.87}}$ | $h_f$ = head loss (m), $Q$ = flow (m³/s), $d$ = diameter (m), $C$ = Hazen-Williams constant | Sprinkler network design | | **Heat Release Rate (Combustion)** | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot H_c \cdot \eta$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass burning rate (kg/s), $H_c$ = heat of combustion (kJ/kg), $\eta$ = combustion efficiency | Fire size estimation | | **Ceiling Jet Temperature** | $T_{cj} = T_\infty + \dfrac{5.38 (\dot{Q}/r)^{2/3}}{H}$ | $r$ = radial distance (m), $H$ = ceiling height (m) | Smoke detector activation | | **Smoke Filling Time (in Compartment)** | $t_s = \dfrac{2 H_s}{(\dot{Q}/(A_f \cdot C_p \cdot \rho \cdot T_\infty))^{1/3}}$ | $H_s$ = smoke layer height, $\rho$ = air density | Detector placement | | **Water Cooling Energy** | $E_w = m_w \cdot (C_p \Delta T + L_v)$ | $m_w$ = water mass (kg), $\Delta T$ = temperature rise (K) | Extinguisher effectiveness | | **Fire Pump Power** | $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{1000 \cdot \eta}$ | $Q$ = discharge (m³/s), $H$ = head (m), $\eta$ = efficiency | Pump sizing | > [!NOTE] > **Units Convention:** In Indian Standards, fire flow is typically measured in **LPM (litres per minute)** or **LPS (litres per second)**. Pressure is in **bar** or **kg/cm²** for sprinkler systems. --- ## 2.4 Classification of Buildings by Fire Load (IS 1641) | Fire Load $q$ (kg of wood/m²) | Classification | Severity | |---|---|---| | $< 35$ | Low hazard | Light fire load | | $35 - 75$ | Moderate hazard | Ordinary | | $75 - 150$ | High hazard (Group I) | Heavy | | $> 150$ | Extra high hazard (Group II) | Severe | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the timber-equivalent fire load with total calorific fire load. Always specify the conversion factor (timber $\approx 18.6$ MJ/kg or $4400$ kcal/kg) when writing your answer. --- ## 2.5 Modes of Extinguishment (KTU Must-Know) Every fire extinguishing agent works by attacking **at least one side of the fire tetrahedron**: 1. **Cooling (Smothering Heat)** — Water absorbs latent heat ($L_v = 2260$ kJ/kg). 2. **Smothering (Oxygen Starvation)** — Foam, CO₂, inert gas blanket. 3. **Fuel Removal** — Shut off valves, isolate the leak. 4. **Chain-Breaking (Chemical Inhibition)** — Dry chemical powder (monoammonium phosphate), halons — disrupts free-radical chain reaction. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Fire safety principles are applied in: - **Oil & Gas:** Blast-resistant firewater systems, foam pourers on hydrocarbon tanks. - **Pharmaceuticals:** Clean-agent (FM-200, Novec 1230) suppression in server rooms. - **Power Plants:** Deluge systems on transformer yards; CO₂ flooding in turbine enclosures. - **High-Rise Buildings:** Stairwell pressurization, evacuation modeling (RSET vs. ASET). - **Manufacturing:** Dust explosion suppression, electrostatics control. - **Storage Warehousing:** ESFR (Early Suppression Fast Response) sprinklers. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fire Load Calculation ### Problem Setup A factory storeroom of floor area **$A_f = 200$ m²** contains: - **800 kg** of wooden crates ($H_c = 18{,}600$ kJ/kg) - **200 kg** of cardboard boxes ($H_c = 16{,}000$ kJ/kg) - **50 kg** of rubber sheets ($H_c = 44{,}000$ kJ/kg) Determine the **total fire load**, the **timber-equivalent fire load**, and the **fire severity classification per IS 1641**. --- ### Step 1: Identify Variables $$\begin{aligned} m_1 &= 800 \text{ kg} \quad &H_1 &= 18{,}600 \text{ kJ/kg} \\ m_2 &= 200 \text{ kg} \quad &H_2 &= 16{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} \\ m_3 &= 50 \text{ kg} \quad &H_3 &= 44{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} \\ A_f &= 200 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Step 2: Calculate Individual Energy Contributions Each combustible contributes its **mass × calorific value**: $$\begin{aligned} E_1 &= m_1 \times H_1 = 800 \times 18{,}600 \\ &= 14{,}880{,}000 \text{ kJ} = 14{,}880 \text{ MJ} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} E_2 &= m_2 \times H_2 = 200 \times 16{,}000 \\ &= 3{,}200{,}000 \text{ kJ} = 3{,}200 \text{ MJ} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} E_3 &= m_3 \times H_3 = 50 \times 44{,}000 \\ &= 2{,}200{,}000 \text{ kJ} = 2{,}200 \text{ MJ} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Step 3: Sum Total Energy Released $$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{total}} &= E_1 + E_2 + E_3 \\ &= 14{,}880 + 3{,}200 + 2{,}200 \\ &= 20{,}280 \text{ MJ} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Step 4: Compute Fire Load $q$ $$\begin{aligned} q &= \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{A_f} \\ &= \frac{20{,}280}{200} \\ &= 101.4 \text{ MJ/m}^2 \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Step 5: Convert to Timber-Equivalent Fire Load Using timber calorific value $H_{\text{timber}} = 18.6 \text{ MJ/kg}$: $$\begin{aligned} q_e &= \frac{q}{H_{\text{timber}}} = \frac{101.4}{18.6} \\ &= 5.45 \text{ kg of wood equivalent per m}^2 \\ &= 545 \text{ kg/m}^2 \text{ (commonly expressed)} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Step 6: Classify Severity (Per IS 1641) $$\begin{aligned} q_e \approx 545 \text{ kg/m}^2 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{Extra High Hazard (Group II)} \end{aligned}$$ This storeroom requires **automatic sprinkler protection, fire-rated compartment walls (≥ 4 hours), and dedicated firewater storage**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — $t^2$ Fire Growth Model ### Theoretical Background The **$t^2$ fire growth model** (Heskestad) is universally used in performance-based fire design. The Heat Release Rate (HRR) grows quadratically with time: $$\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha \cdot t^2$$ The growth coefficient $\alpha$ classifies fire growth speed: | Category | $\alpha$ (kW/s²) | Typical Material | |---|---|---| | Slow | 0.00293 | Dense wood cribs | | Medium | 0.01172 | Office furniture | | Fast | 0.0469 | Plastic stacking | | Ultra-Fast | 0.1876 | Flammable liquid pools | --- ### Step 1: Compute HRR at $t = 60$ s for a "Fast" growth fire $$\begin{aligned} \dot{Q}(60) &= \alpha \cdot t^2 = 0.0469 \times (60)^2 \\ &= 0.0469 \times 3600 \\ &= 168.84 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Step 2: Compute HRR at $t = 300$ s $$\begin{aligned} \dot{Q}(300) &= 0.0469 \times (300)^2 \\ &= 0.0469 \times 90{,}000 \\ &= 4221 \text{ kW} \approx 4.22 \text{ MW} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Step 3: Determine Time to Reach 1 MW HRR (activation trigger for detectors) Setting $\dot{Q}(t) = 1000$ kW: $$\begin{aligned} 1000 &= 0.0469 \cdot t^2 \\ t^2 &= \frac{1000}{0.0469} = 21{,}322.8 \\ t &= \sqrt{21{,}322.8} \\ t &= 146.04 \text{ s} \approx 2.43 \text{ min} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > This shows that an automatic sprinkler designed to activate at 1 MW HRR will be triggered within **~2.5 minutes** for a fast-growth fire — making early detection essential. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sprinkler Discharge Calculation ### Problem Setup A sprinkler head has a **K-factor of 80 LPM/bar$^{1/2}$**. The water pressure at the head is **3.5 bar**. Determine: 1. Discharge flow $Q$ 2. Number of heads served by a 1500 LPM pump --- ### Step 1: Apply the K-Factor Formula $$\begin{aligned} Q &= K \sqrt{P} \\ &= 80 \times \sqrt{3.5} \\ &= 80 \times 1.8708 \\ &= 149.66 \text{ LPM per head} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Step 2: Determine Number of Heads $$\begin{aligned} N &= \frac{Q_{\text{pump}}}{Q_{\text{head}}} = \frac{1500}{149.66} \\ &= 10.02 \approx 10 \text{ heads} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Step 3: Practical Design Check (NFPA 13) Per NFPA 13, design density is typically **12.5 LPM/m²** (ordinary hazard). For an area of **$20$ m × $20$ m = 400 m²**: $$\begin{aligned} Q_{\text{design}} &= 12.5 \times 400 = 5000 \text{ LPM} \\ N_{\text{heads}} &= \frac{5000}{149.66} \approx 34 \text{ heads} \end{aligned}$$ The pump must be upgraded to deliver at least 5000 LPM at required pressure. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Smoke Filling Time in a Compartment ### Problem Setup A room of floor area $50$ m², ceiling height $H = 4$ m. A fire of HRR $\dot{Q} = 1000$ kW begins. Estimate **smoke layer descent time** from ceiling to a critical height of $H_c = 2$ m above floor. --- ### Step 1: Plug into Empirical Equation (Zukoski) $$\begin{aligned} t_s &= \frac{2 (H - H_c)}{\left(\dfrac{\dot{Q}}{A_f \cdot C_p \cdot \rho_\infty \cdot T_\infty}\right)^{1/3}} \end{aligned}$$ Using $C_p = 1.0$ kJ/kg·K, $\rho_\infty = 1.2$ kg/m³, $T_\infty = 293$ K: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Denominator} &= \left(\frac{1000}{50 \times 1.0 \times 1.2 \times 293}\right)^{1/3} \\ &= \left(\frac{1000}{17{,}580}\right)^{1/3} \\ &= (0.0569)^{1/3} \\ &= 0.384 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Step 2: Compute Filling Time $$\begin{aligned} t_s &= \frac{2 \times (4 - 2)}{0.384} = \frac{4}{0.384} \\ &= 10.42 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Result:** Smoke descends to 2 m in just **~10 seconds**. This is why **detector activation must be sub-10-second** in high-occupancy zones. KTU examiners expect numerical justification of detector placement. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Extinguishing Media vs. Fire Class | Extinguishing Medium | Class A | Class B | Class C (Gas) | Class D (Metal) | Electrical | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Water | ✅ Best | ❌ (Splash risk) | ❌ | ❌ (Reacts!) | ❌ Conducts | | Water Spray/Fog | ✅ | ✅ (small spill) | ❌ | ❌ | ⚠ Only de-energized | | Foam (AFFF) | ✅ | ✅ Best | ⚠ Limited | ❌ | ❌ | | Dry Chemical Powder (ABC) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ (use D-specific) | ✅ | | CO₂ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ Best | | Halon / Clean Agent | ⚠ Limited | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ Best | | Wet Chemical | ❌ | ⚠ (Class K only) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | Special Metal Powders | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Best | ❌ | --- ## 3.6 Hydraulic Calculation — Friction Loss in Sprinkler Riser ### Given - Pipe length $L = 50$ m, diameter $d = 100$ mm, flow $Q = 50$ LPS, Hazen-Williams $C = 120$. ### Step 1: Apply Hazen-Williams Formula (SI) $$\begin{aligned} h_f &= \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot d^{4.87}} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} Q^{1.852} &= (50)^{1.852} = 1562.5 \\ C^{1.852} &= (120)^{1.852} = 7393.6 \\ d^{4.87} &= (0.1)^{4.87} = 1.349 \times 10^{-5} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Step 2: Substitute $$\begin{aligned} h_f &= \frac{10.67 \times 50 \times 1562.5}{7393.6 \times 1.349 \times 10^{-5}} \\ &= \frac{833{,}437.5}{0.09976} \\ &= 8.354 \times 10^{6} \text{ ??} \end{aligned}$$ **Correction:** The Hazen-Williams in SI yields head in metres when $Q$ is in **m³/s** and $d$ is in **m**. Convert $Q = 50$ LPS $= 0.05$ m³/s: $$\begin{aligned} Q^{1.852} &= (0.05)^{1.852} = 4.51 \times 10^{-3} \\ h_f &= \frac{10.67 \times 50 \times 4.51 \times 10^{-3}}{7393.6 \times 1.349 \times 10^{-5}} \\ &= \frac{2.405}{0.0998} \\ &= 24.10 \text{ m of head loss} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Methodology Note:** Always verify SI vs. US-customary units. Most Indian design offices use **Hazen-Williams** with $Q$ in LPS and $d$ in cm, which gives a different empirical constant. State the convention used. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Fire Tetrahedron — Concept Map ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["FUEL

5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Explain the "Fire Tetrahedron" and state how it differs from the classical Fire Triangle.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks — Structure: 1 + 2) **Definition (1 mark):** The **Fire Tetrahedron** is a modern combustion model representing the four essential elements required simultaneously for sustained combustion: **Fuel, Oxidizer, Heat**, and **Uninhibited Chain Reaction (Free Radicals)**. **Difference from Fire Triangle (2 marks):** | Aspect | Fire Triangle | Fire Tetrahedron | |---|---|---| | Sides | 3 (Fuel, O₂, Heat) | 4 (Fuel, O₂, Heat, Chain Reaction) | | Model | Classical, slow combustion | Modern, flame chemistry | | Explains | Why fire occurs | Why fire persists after ignition | | Extinguishing insight | Remove fuel/O₂/heat | Add chain-breaker (dry chemical, halon) | **KTU Key Point:** Dry chemical and halon extinguishers specifically attack the **chain-reaction** side — which a triangle cannot explain. --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Classify fires as per IS 2190 and state the suitable extinguishing medium for each class.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular format preferred) | Class | Fuel Type | Extinguishing Medium | |---|---|---| | **Class A** | Wood, paper, cloth | Water / ABC powder | | **Class B** | Flammable liquids | Foam / CO₂ / DCP | | **Class C** | Gases | BCF / DCP + shut-off valve | | **Class D** | Metals | Special dry powder (NaCl/Graphite) | | **Class E** | Electrical (per IS) | CO₂ / Clean agent | (3 marks for correct classification with one example each) --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — **14 Marks** **(a)** Explain in detail the **various methods of fire extinguishment** with reference to the fire tetrahedron. Discuss suitable extinguishing media for different classes of fires. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A factory storeroom has floor area **$180 \text{ m}^2$**. The combustibles present are: - **600 kg** of wooden pallets ($H_c = 18{,}600 \text{ kJ/kg}$) - **150 kg** of cardboard ($H_c = 16{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$) - **40 kg** of foam rubber ($H_c = 42{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$) Calculate (i) **Total fire load**, (ii) **Timber-equivalent fire load**, (iii) Classify the fire hazard as per **IS 1641**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): The four modes of extinguishment correspond to removal of one side of the fire tetrahedron: **1. Cooling (1.5 marks):** Water absorbs heat. With $L_v = 2260$ kJ/kg, water cools fuel below ignition temperature. Most effective for Class A fires. **2. Smothering (1.5 marks):** Foam, CO₂, dry chemical powder blanket the fire, reducing oxygen concentration below **15 %** (extinction limit). **3. Fuel Removal (1 mark):** Valve closure on gas lines, drainage of liquid spills, isolation of electrical supply. **4. Chemical Chain Breaking (1 mark):** Dry chemical powder (monoammonium phosphate) and halons (BCF, BTM) absorb free radicals (H•, OH•), terminating the flame reaction. Most effective on Class B/C. **Suitable Media Mapping (2 marks):** | Fire Class | Best Medium | Mode | |---|---|---| | A | Water spray | Cooling | | B | AFFF Foam | Smothering | | C | DCP | Chain break + smothering | | D | NaCl powder | Smothering (specialized) | | E (Electrical) | CO₂ / Novec | Smothering + chain break | --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** $$\begin{aligned} A_f &= 180 \text{ m}^2 \\ m_1 &= 600 \text{ kg}, \quad H_1 = 18{,}600 \text{ kJ/kg} \\ m_2 &= 150 \text{ kg}, \quad H_2 = 16{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} \\ m_3 &= 40 \text{ kg}, \quad H_3 = 42{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 1: Energy from wood pallets** $$\begin{aligned} E_1 &= 600 \times 18{,}600 = 11{,}160{,}000 \text{ kJ} \\ &= 11{,}160 \text{ MJ} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Energy from cardboard** $$\begin{aligned} E_2 &= 150 \times 16{,}000 = 2{,}400{,}000 \text{ kJ} \\ &= 2{,}400 \text{ MJ} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Energy from foam rubber** $$\begin{aligned} E_3 &= 40 \times 42{,}000 = 1{,}680{,}000 \text{ kJ} \\ &= 1{,}680 \text{ MJ} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Total Energy** $$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{total}} &= 11{,}160 + 2{,}400 + 1{,}680 = 15{,}240 \text{ MJ} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5: Fire Load** $$\begin{aligned} q &= \frac{15{,}240}{180} = 84.67 \text{ MJ/m}^2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6: Timber-Equivalent Fire Load** (using $H_{\text{timber}} = 18.6$ MJ/kg) $$\begin{aligned} q_e &= \frac{84.67}{18.6} = 4.55 \text{ kg/m}^2 \text{ (timber equivalent)} \\ &= 455 \text{ kg/m}^2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 7: Classification per IS 1641** $$\boxed{q_e \approx 455 \text{ kg/m}^2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Extra High Hazard (Group II)}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - (1) Many students **forget to divide by floor area** — losing 2 marks. > - (2) Writing "high fire load" without quoting IS 1641 thresholds gets **0 marks** for classification. > - (3) Units mismatch: writing kJ/m² when problem expects MJ/m² loses **1 mark**. --- ### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` — **14 Marks** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **automatic sprinkler system**.
