State and derive the **$t^2$ fire growth model** with reference to standard fire categories. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A sprinkler head has **K = 80 LPM/bar$^{1/2}$** operating at a pressure of **3.5 bar**. - (i) Calculate the discharge per head. **(2 marks)** - (ii) Determine the number of heads supplied by a **4500 LPM** pump. **(2 marks)** - (iii) A "fast-growth" fire ($\alpha = 0.0469$ kW/s²) reaches a HRR of **1 MW**. Determine the **time to reach this HRR** from ignition. **(3 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Automatic Sprinkler System (3 marks):** A sprinkler system consists of a water supply (pump + tank), a network of pipes, and thermally-activated sprinkler heads. Each head is sealed by a **fusible link** or **glass bulb** that breaks at a specified temperature (typically **68 °C, 93 °C, 141 °C**). Upon activation, water discharges in a conical spray pattern over a coverage area of **~12 m²**. The system operates automatically, requires no external power, and is **highly reliable** — historically the most effective life-safety system in fire protection. **Derivation of $t^2$ Model (4 marks):** The $t^2$ fire model assumes that the **Heat Release Rate grows as the square of elapsed time** after ignition, scaled by a growth coefficient $\alpha$ that depends on fuel type: $$\boxed{\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha \cdot t^2}$$ **Categories (per NFPA / IS):** | Category | $\alpha$ (kW/s²) | Typical Fuel | |---|---|---| | Slow | 0.00293 | Wood pallets, dense timber | | Medium | 0.01172 | Office furniture, mattresses | | Fast | 0.0469 | Plastic stacks, foam | | Ultra-Fast | 0.1876 | Flammable liquid pools | **Derivation Logic:** Experimental data on free-burning fires show that the **flame radius** grows linearly with time. Since flame surface area $\propto r^2$ and HRR $\propto$ surface area, HRR $\propto t^2$. Hence the quadratic form is a fundamental geometric scaling law. --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Part (i) — Discharge per head (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} Q &= K\sqrt{P} = 80 \times \sqrt{3.5} \\ &= 80 \times 1.8708 \\ &= 149.66 \text{ LPM} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 149.66 \text{ LPM per head}}$$ --- **Part (ii) — Number of heads supplied (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} N &= \frac{Q_{\text{pump}}}{Q_{\text{head}}} = \frac{4500}{149.66} \\ &= 30.07 \\ N &\approx 30 \text{ heads} \end{aligned}$$ --- **Part (iii) — Time to 1 MW HRR for fast growth (3 marks):** Given $\alpha = 0.0469$ kW/s², $\dot{Q} = 1000$ kW. $$\begin{aligned} \dot{Q} &= \alpha \cdot t^2 \\ 1000 &= 0.0469 \cdot t^2 \\ t^2 &= \frac{1000}{0.0469} = 21{,}322.8 \\ t &= \sqrt{21{,}322.8} = 146.04 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{t \approx 146 \text{ seconds} \approx 2 \text{ min } 26 \text{ sec}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Units of K-factor:** K = 80 means LPM/bar$^{1/2}$ — students often plug wrong units and lose **1 mark**. > 2. **Pump capacity vs. head capacity:** Pump flow must also account for **simultaneously open heads** (typically 8–12 in light hazard, more in high hazard). > 3. **$t^2$ model validity:** The model is valid only in the **growth phase**, before flashover. Do not apply it after fully-developed stage. > 4. **Always state the growth category** (slow/medium/fast) in the answer — examiners reward it with **0.5–1 mark**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam — Fire Safety** ### ✅ Core Concepts - [x] **Fire Triangle** — 3 sides: Fuel, Oxygen, Heat - [x] **Fire Tetrahedron** — 4th side: Chain reaction (free radicals) - [x] **Fire Classes** — A (solid), B (liquid), C (gas), D (metal), E (electrical per IS) - [x] **Stages of Fire** — Incipient → Growth → Flashover → Fully Developed → Decay - [x] **Modes of Extinguishment** — Cooling, Smothering, Fuel Removal, Chain Breaking ### ✅ Numerical Must-Know - [x] **Fire Load formula:** $q = \dfrac{\sum m_i H_i}{A_f}$ - [x] **Timber equivalent:** Divide by 18.6 MJ/kg - [x] **Sprinkler discharge:** $Q = K\sqrt{P}$ - [x] **$t^2$ fire growth:** $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$ - [x] **Hazen-Williams friction loss** for hydraulic design - [x] **Smoke filling** using Zukoski equation for detector timing ### ✅ Codes & Standards (Always Cite!) - [x] **IS 2190** — Fire classification - [x] **IS 1641** — Fire load classification of buildings - [x] **IS 3844** — Fire detection and alarm systems - [x] **IS 15105** — Automatic sprinkler systems - [x] **NBC 2016 Part 4** — Fire and Life Safety (India) - [x] **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee)** — Insurance requirements - [x] **NFPA 10** — Portable extinguishers - [x] **NFPA 13** — Sprinkler design - [x] **NFPA 72** — Detection & alarm ### ✅ Common Mnemonics - **PASS** for extinguisher use: **P**ull pin, **A**im at base, **S**queeze handle, **S**weep side to side - **RACE** in emergencies: **R**escue, **A**larm, **C**ontain, **E**xtinguish/Evacuate ### ✅ Critical Values to Memorize - LFL & UFL of common gases (methane, propane, hydrogen) - AIT of common liquids (gasoline 280 °C, diesel 210 °C, kerosene 220 °C) - Sprinkler operating temperatures (68 °C amber, 93 °C red) - Fire resistance ratings (1 hr, 2 hr, 4 hr walls) ### ✅ High-Yield Exam Triggers (Frequently Asked) - Compare **active vs passive** fire protection - Explain **hydrant system layout** with components - **$t^2$ fire** numerical — almost every year - **Selection of extinguisher** for a given scenario - **Evacuation plan** for multi-storey building - **NBC 2016** provisions for high-rise buildings > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always start your answer with a **1-line formal definition**, then use a **table or diagram** to structure your response, and **end with a numerical example** wherever the question allows. Examiners reward clear structure with **1–2 bonus marks** even in strict valuation.

topics.title: Factors Contributing Towards Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET637 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Factors Contributing Towards Fire ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Fire** is a rapid, self-sustaining oxidation reaction accompanied by the evolution of heat and light, occurring when three essential elements — **Fuel**, **Oxygen**, and **Heat (Ignition Source)** — combine in the correct proportions and under favorable physical conditions. In modern fire science, a fourth element, the **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction**, is also recognized, forming the *Fire Tetrahedron*. The classical representation of the three essential elements is called the **Fire Triangle**, while the advanced four-element model is termed the **Fire Tetrahedron**. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine lighting a campfire in a forest clearing. You arrange **wood (fuel)**, ensure there is **air (oxygen)** flowing around it, and you strike a **match or use friction (heat)**. The moment all three come together, the fire begins. Take away the wood, and the fire dies. Cover it with a blanket (remove oxygen) and it smothers. Pour cold water (remove heat) and the reaction stops. This everyday observation is the foundation of the entire industrial fire prevention discipline. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In an industrial setting, *removing any one element* of the Fire Triangle/Tetrahedron is the fundamental principle behind every fire prevention and extinguishing strategy taught in PEMET637. ### 1.3 Categories of Fire Fires are classified internationally based on the type of fuel involved: | Class | Fuel Type | Common Examples | |:---:|:---|:---| | **Class A** | Ordinary Combustibles | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber | | **Class B** | Flammable Liquids & Gases | Petrol, diesel, LPG, acetone | | **Class C** | Flammable Metals | Magnesium, sodium, titanium | | **Class D** | Electrical Fires | Energized wiring, transformers | | **Class K** | Cooking Oils & Fats | Vegetable oils, animal fats | ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Stoichiometric Oxygen Concentration** for complete combustion of hydrocarbons ≈ **21% by volume** (atmospheric air). - **Minimum Oxygen Concentration** to sustain combustion of most organics ≈ **12–16%**. - **Flammability Range** — bounded by **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)** and **Upper Explosive Limit (UEL)**. - **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** — the temperature at which a substance ignites spontaneously without an external flame. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Fire Triangle — Three Pillars of Combustion #### Pillar 1: **Fuel (Combustible Material)** The fuel is any substance capable of undergoing oxidation with the release of energy. Its contribution to fire depends on several sub-factors: - **Physical State** — Solids, liquids, and gases behave differently. Gases ignite most easily because molecules are already dispersed and need no vaporization phase. Solids require pyrolysis (thermal decomposition) before flaming combustion. - **Surface Area to Volume Ratio** — A finely divided solid (sawdust, metal powder) has a far greater ratio than a solid block and ignites far more easily. - **Chemical Composition** — Hydrocarbon-rich fuels release more heat. Halogenated compounds (containing Cl, Br) resist ignition. - **Arrangement / Packing Density** — Loose packing allows oxygen ingress; tight packing restricts it. - **Moisture Content** — Water absorbed by fuel must first evaporate, requiring latent heat, which delays ignition. #### Pillar 2: **Oxygen (Oxidizer)** Atmospheric air contains approximately **21% oxygen by volume**, which is more than sufficient for most industrial fires. - **Oxygen Enrichment Hazard** — In hospitals, chemical plants, and welding zones, oxygen concentration can exceed 21%, dramatically lowering ignition thresholds. - **Ventilation Effects** — A confined space with limited ventilation may have its oxygen depleted, leading to *smouldering* rather than flaming combustion. - **Minimum Oxygen for Sustained Burning** — For most organics, oxygen concentration below ~12–14% extinguishes the flame. #### Pillar 3: **Heat (Activation Energy Source)** Heat is the energy input required to initiate and sustain the combustion reaction. Sources of ignition heat in industry include: - **Electrical Sources** — Short circuits, static discharge, lightning, overloaded cables. - **Mechanical Sources** — Friction from bearings, overheating of machinery, sparks from grinding. - **Chemical Sources** — Exothermic reactions, spontaneous combustion of oils, contact with strong oxidizers. - **Thermal Sources** — Hot surfaces, steam pipes, furnaces, boilers. - **Nuclear / Radiative Sources** — Rare, but radiation can heat materials beyond AIT. ### 2.2 The Fire Tetrahedron — Fourth Element > [!IMPORTANT] > Modern combustion theory recognizes that once the three elements of the triangle combine, the reaction must propagate on its own. This self-perpetuating **Chain Reaction** of free radicals (H•, OH•, O•) is the fourth side of the **Fire Tetrahedron**. Many modern extinguishing agents (e.g., dry chemical powder, halons) work specifically by interrupting this chain reaction. ### 2.3 Key Material Properties Governing Fire Behaviour - **Flash Point (FP)** — *Lowest temperature* at which a liquid gives off enough vapour to form an *ignitable mixture* with air **near its surface** (not sustained). - **Fire Point (FPT)** — *Lowest temperature* at which the vapour continues to burn for at least **5 seconds** after ignition. - **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** — *Minimum temperature* at which a substance ignites **without any external flame or spark**. - **Flammability Limits** — Concentration range in air within which a vapour can ignite. - **LEL (Lower Explosive Limit)** — *Minimum* concentration for ignition. - **UEL (Upper Explosive Limit)** — *Maximum* concentration for ignition (above this, mixture is too fuel-rich). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Expression | Boundary Conditions & Units | |:---|:---|:---| | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis) | $(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{34.5 \cdot (H_2 - O_2/8) + 4.29 \cdot S}{C}$ | C, H₂, O₂, S in % by mass of fuel; dimensionless | | Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand | $O_{2,\text{req}} = \dfrac{32C/12 + 8H/2 + 32S/32}{M_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$ | Result in kg O₂/kg fuel | | Mass fraction of oxygen in air | $Y_{O_2} = 0.232$ | Dimensionless | | Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) | Empirical: $LFL \approx \dfrac{4.9 \cdot C}{n}$ | $n$ = stoichiometric coefficient | | Upper Flammability Limit (UFL) | Empirical: $UFL \approx \dfrac{3.5 \cdot C}{n}$ | Valid for hydrocarbon vapours | | Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE) | $MIE \approx f(\text{fuel concentration})$ | Energy in mJ; typical hydrocarbons ≈ 0.25 mJ | | Stoichiometric Concentration | $C_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{100}{1 + (A/F)_{\text{stoich}} \cdot (\rho_{\text{air}}/\rho_{\text{fuel}})}$ | Volume % | | Heat of Combustion (mass basis) | $Q_c = \dfrac{\text{Total heat released}}{\text{mass of fuel burned}}$ | kJ/kg or kJ/mol | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Process Safety Engineering** uses LEL/UEL data to design ventilation systems and inerting procedures for storage tanks. - **Electrical Area Classification** (Zones 0, 1, 2 for gases; Zones 20, 21, 22 for dusts) is directly driven by flash point and AIT. - **Fire Protection System Design** (sprinklers, foam, gas suppression) relies on flash point, fire point, and heat release rate. - **Insurance Risk Assessment** uses fire load (MJ/m²) calculations derived from mass of combustible material and heat of combustion. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Combustion of Methane (CH₄) The complete combustion reaction in air: $$CH_4 + 2(O_2 + 3.76 N_2) \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O + 7.52 N_2$$ **Step 1 — Balance carbon atoms:** $$\text{C atoms on left} = 1 \quad ; \quad \text{C atoms on right (in }CO_2) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 2 — Balance hydrogen atoms:** $$\text{H atoms on left} = 4 \quad ; \quad \text{H atoms on right} = 2 \times 2 = 4 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 3 — Balance oxygen atoms:** $$2 O_2 \rightarrow 1 CO_2 + 2 H_2O$$ $$\text{O atoms on left} = 2 \times 2 = 4 \quad ; \quad \text{O atoms on right} = 2 + 2(1) = 4 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Air contains 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ (mole ratio = 1 O₂ : 3.76 N₂):** $$\text{So for } 2 O_2 \text{ moles, } N_2 \text{ moles} = 2 \times 3.76 = 7.52$$ **Step 5 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (molar basis):** $$A/F_{\text{molar}} = \dfrac{2 + 7.52}{1} = 9.52$$ **Step 6 — Convert to mass basis using molecular masses (CH₄ = 16 kg/kmol, Air = 28.97 kg/kmol):** $$A/F_{\text{mass}} = \dfrac{9.52 \times 28.97}{1 \times 16} = \dfrac{275.78}{16} = 17.24$$ > **Interpretation:** 17.24 kg of air is required to burn 1 kg of methane completely. ### 3.2 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (General Hydrocarbon) For a generic hydrocarbon fuel $C_x H_y$: $$C_x H_y + (x + y/4)(O_2 + 3.76 N_2) \rightarrow x CO_2 + (y/2) H_2O + 3.76(x + y/4) N_2$$ **Step 1 — Total moles of air per mole of fuel:** $$n_{\text{air}} = (x + y/4) \times 4.76$$ **Step 2 — Molar Air-Fuel Ratio:** $$(A/F)_{\text{molar}} = 4.76 \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Convert to mass ratio using $M_{\text{fuel}} = 12x + y$ and $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97$:** $$(A/F)_{\text{mass}} = \dfrac{4.76 \times (x + y/4) \times 28.97}{12x + y}$$ > This is the **general-purpose A/F formula** used in combustion engineering for any hydrocarbon. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When Does a Fire Start? A fire initiates **only when** all the following conditions hold simultaneously: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Condition 1 (Fuel):} \quad & C_{\text{fuel}} \geq LEL \quad \text{AND} \quad C_{\text{fuel}} \leq UEL \\ \text{Condition 2 (Oxygen):} \quad & C_{O_2} \geq 0.12 \quad (\text{12\% by volume}) \\ \text{Condition 3 (Heat):} \quad & T_{\text{surface}} \geq AIT \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Energy supplied} \geq MIE \\ \text{Condition 4 (Chain):} \quad & \text{Free radical concentration} > \text{critical} \\ \text{Condition 5 (Geometry):} \quad & \text{Surface area / volume ratio} \geq \text{threshold} \end{aligned} $$ > **Conclusion:** A fire will **not** initiate if any single condition fails. This is the *logical foundation* of every fire prevention strategy. ### 3.4 Quantitative Comparison: Flash Point vs Fire Point vs AIT | Parameter | Definition | Example: Diesel | Example: Petrol | |:---|:---|:---:|:---:| | **Flash Point** | Vapour ignites momentarily | 55–60 °C | −40 °C | | **Fire Point** | Vapour sustains combustion ≥ 5 s | 65–70 °C | −30 °C | | **Auto-Ignition Temp** | Spontaneous ignition without flame | 210 °C | 280 °C | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Pearl:** Always remember the hierarchy: **FP < FPT < AIT** for the same fuel. FP is always the lowest, and AIT is always the highest. KTU questions frequently test this ordering. ### 3.5 Worked Example: Identifying the Limiting Factor **Problem:** A wooden pallet is stored in an enclosed warehouse with no electrical equipment. The ambient temperature is 25 °C. Identify which Fire Triangle element is the controlling factor. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{ambient}} &= 25^\circ C = 298 \text{ K} \\ T_{\text{AIT of wood}} &\approx 300^\circ C \\ \therefore T_{\text{ambient}} &\ll T_{\text{AIT}} \end{aligned} $$ Since the ambient temperature is far below the auto-ignition temperature of wood and there is **no external ignition source**, the **Heat element** is the limiting factor. Adding any heat source (spark, flame, hot surface above ~300 °C) would trigger combustion. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Fire Triangle — Mermaid Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TD A[FUEL
Solids, Liquids, Gases] --> D[FIRE
Rapid Oxidation
with Heat and Light] B[OXYGEN
Minimum 12 percent in air] --> D C[HEAT
Ignition Source
Above AIT] --> D D --> E[Removal of any ONE element
EXTINGUISHES Fire] A --> E B --> E C --> E style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style B fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style C fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff style E fill:#95e1d3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Fire Tetrahedron — Extended Model with Chain Reaction ```mermaid graph TD F1[FUEL
Hydrocarbons, Wood,
Plastics, Metals] --> TETRA[FIRE TETRAHEDRON] F2[OXYGEN
Oxidizer Agent] --> TETRA F3[HEAT
Activation Energy] --> TETRA F4[CHAIN REACTION
Free Radicals H, OH, O] --> TETRA TETRA --> OUT[SUSTAINED COMBUSTION] F1 -.REMOVE FUEL.-> EXT[EXTINCTION] F2 -.SMOTHER.-> EXT F3 -.COOL.-> EXT F4 -.INHIBIT CHAIN.-> EXT style F1 fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff style F2 fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#000,color:#fff style F3 fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#000,color:#000 style F4 fill:#a29bfe,stroke:#000,color:#fff style TETRA fill:#ff4500,stroke:#000,color:#fff style OUT fill:#32cd32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style EXT fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Industrial Fire Causation — Block-Level Architecture Flow ```mermaid graph LR P1[HUMAN FACTOR
Negligence, Ignorance] --> ROOT[FIRE INITIATION] P2[MATERIAL FACTOR
Low FP, Low AIT,
High LFL] --> ROOT P3[ENVIRONMENT FACTOR
Ventilation, Temperature,
Humidity, Pressure] --> ROOT P4[EQUIPMENT FACTOR
Electrical Fault,
Friction, Overheating] --> ROOT P5[PROCESS FACTOR
Exothermic Reactions,
Improper Storage] --> ROOT ROOT --> X1[FUEL VAPOUR MIXTURE] ROOT --> X2[IGNITION SOURCE] ROOT --> X3[OXYGEN SUPPLY] X1 --> FIRE[FIRE OUTBREAK] X2 --> FIRE X3 --> FIRE FIRE --> CLASS[CLASS A, B, C, D, K] CLASS --> RESPONSE[SUITABLE EXTINGUISHING METHOD] style ROOT fill:#ff0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style FIRE fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff style RESPONSE fill:#32cd32,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.4 Hierarchy of Combustion Temperatures ```mermaid graph TD A[AMBIENT TEMPERATURE
25 degree C] --> B[FLASH POINT
Vapour Flashes
Near Surface] B --> C[FIRE POINT
Sustained Burn
for 5 seconds] C --> D[AUTO IGNITION TEMPERATURE
Spontaneous Ignition
No External Flame] A -.NO IGNITION.-> SAFE[SAFE ZONE] B -.TRANSIENT.-> SAFE style A fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style B fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D fill:#ff0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style SAFE fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the **Fire Triangle** and explain its significance in industrial fire prevention. **Model Answer:** The **Fire Triangle** is a graphical representation of the three essential elements required for the initiation and sustenance of a fire: 1. **Fuel** — any combustible material (solid, liquid, or gas). 2. **Oxygen** — usually supplied by atmospheric air (≥ 12% by volume). 3. **Heat** — the activation energy source, with temperature exceeding the **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** of the fuel. **Significance in Industrial Fire Prevention:** - The principle states that **removal of any one element** will extinguish or prevent fire. - It forms the basis of all extinguishing methods: **cooling** (remove heat), **smothering** (remove oxygen), and **starvation** (remove fuel). - Industrial safety strategies — fire detection systems, sprinkler networks, foam suppression, inert gas flooding — are all engineered applications of this triangle. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish clearly between **Flash Point**, **Fire Point**, and **Auto-Ignition Temperature** with one example each. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Definition | Example (Diesel) | Example (Petrol) | |:---|:---|:---:|:---:| | **Flash Point (FP)** | Lowest temperature at which vapour produces a momentary flash near the liquid surface when an ignition source is applied | **55 °C** | **−40 °C** | | **Fire Point (FPT)** | Lowest temperature at which vapour continues to burn for at least 5 seconds after ignition | **65 °C** | **−30 °C** | | **Auto-Ignition Temp (AIT)** | Minimum temperature at which a substance ignites spontaneously **without any external source** | **210 °C** | **280 °C** | > **Key Relationship:** For the same fuel, **FP < FPT < AIT**. FP and FPT require an external flame; AIT does not. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]* **(a)** Explain in detail the **Fire Tetrahedron**. How does it differ from the Fire Triangle, and what is the practical significance of the fourth element? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the **factors contributing to industrial fires** under the categories: (i) Material, (ii) Environmental, (iii) Human, and (iv) Equipment. **(7 Marks)** ##### Model Solution **(a) The Fire Tetrahedron:** The Fire Triangle captures only three elements: Fuel, Oxygen, and Heat. However, modern combustion science recognizes that once combustion begins, the reaction must *self-propagate* via a chain of free radicals (H•, OH•, O•) — this is the **fourth side: the Chemical Chain Reaction**. The **Fire Tetrahedron** is a 3-D pyramid with the four elements at its vertices: - **Fuel** (one vertex) - **Oxygen** (second vertex) - **Heat** (third vertex) - **Chemical Chain Reaction** (fourth vertex — base) **Difference from the Triangle:** - The Triangle explains *initiation* of fire; the Tetrahedron explains *sustenance*. - Halons, dry chemical powders (ABC type), and some gaseous suppressants (FM-200, Novec 1230) work by breaking the chain reaction — a method **impossible to explain using the Triangle alone**. **Practical Significance:** - Allows design of chemical inhibitors that interrupt radical propagation. - Forms the basis of "inhibition" or "interruption" extinguishing class. - Critical in designing suppression systems for server rooms, aircraft engine nacelles, and high-value electrical installations. **(b) Factors Contributing to Industrial Fires:** **(i) Material Factors:** - Low Flash Point liquids (e.g., acetone, petrol). - Low AIT solids (e.g., magnesium, aluminium powder). - Improper storage — incompatible chemicals stored together. - High surface-area-to-volume ratio (powders, dusts). - Pyrophoric materials that ignite on contact with air. **(ii) Environmental Factors:** - Ambient temperature exceeding fuel AIT. - Poor ventilation leading to vapour accumulation. - High humidity affecting electrical insulation. - Lightning strikes in outdoor storage yards. - Dust clouds in grain silos — major explosion hazard. **(iii) Human Factors:** - Smoking in flammable-liquid zones. - Hot work (welding, cutting) without permits. - Improper housekeeping — oily rags causing spontaneous combustion. - Inadequate training in handling flammable materials. - Bypassing safety interlocks. **(iv) Equipment Factors:** - Electrical short circuits and arc flashes. - Friction sparks from grinding or bearings. - Static electricity discharge during fuel transfer. - Overheating boilers and furnaces. - Leaking valves and pipelines. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* **(a)** What are **flammability limits (LEL and UEL)**? Why is the stoichiometric mixture not the most easily ignitable concentration? Explain with a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A storage tank contains hexane (C₆H₁₄). Calculate the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio** (mass basis) and the percentage of oxygen required theoretically for complete combustion. **(7 Marks)** ##### Model Solution **(a) Flammability Limits:** **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)** — the *minimum* concentration of vapour in air below which the mixture is too lean to burn (insufficient fuel). **Upper Explosive Limit (UEL)** — the *maximum* concentration above which the mixture is too rich to burn (insufficient oxygen). The range between LEL and UEL is the **Flammable Range**. Concentrations outside this range cannot ignite. **Why Stoichiometric is *not* the most easily ignitable:** At stoichiometric concentration, the reaction is chemically ideal but the *peak flame temperature* and *reaction rate* are governed by radical concentration profiles. Experimental observations show: - Most easily ignitable concentration lies **slightly above stoichiometric** for hydrocarbons (typically 110–130% of stoichiometric). - Reason: At exact stoichiometric, oxygen is the limiting factor for radical propagation; a small excess fuel provides richer radical pool for chain branching. **Diagram (described):** A typical flammability curve plots **% fuel by volume** (x-axis) against **explosion pressure / flame speed** (y-axis). The curve is bell-shaped, peaking between LEL and UEL, but the peak lies to the **right of the stoichiometric point**. | Fuel | LEL (% vol) | UEL (% vol) | Stoichiometric (% vol) | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Methane | 5.0 | 15.0 | 9.5 | | Hexane | 1.1 | 7.5 | 2.2 | | Hydrogen | 4.0 | 75.0 | 29.5 | **(b) Calculation: Stoichiometric A/F Ratio for Hexane (C₆H₁₄):** **Step 1 — Write the balanced equation:** $$C_6 H_{14} + a(O_2 + 3.76 N_2) \rightarrow b CO_2 + c H_2O + d N_2$$ **Step 2 — Balance carbon:** $$b = 6$$ **Step 3 — Balance hydrogen:** $$2c = 14 \Rightarrow c = 7$$ **Step 4 — Balance oxygen:** $$2a = 2b + c = 2(6) + 7 = 19 \Rightarrow a = 9.5$$ **Step 5 — Total moles of air per mole of fuel:** $$n_{\text{air}} = a \times 4.76 = 9.5 \times 4.76 = 45.22$$ **Step 6 — Molecular masses:** $$M_{\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14}} = 6(12) + 14(1) = 86 \text{ kg/kmol}$$ $$M_{\text{air}} = 28.97 \text{ kg/kmol}$$ **Step 7 — Mass-based Air-Fuel Ratio:** $$ \begin{aligned} (A/F)_{\text{mass}} &= \dfrac{n_{\text{air}} \times M_{\text{air}}}{1 \times M_{\text{fuel}}} \\ &= \dfrac{45.22 \times 28.97}{86} \\ &= \dfrac{1310.04}{86} \\ &= 15.23 \end{aligned} $$ > **Answer:** The stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for hexane ≈ **15.23 kg air/kg fuel**. **Step 8 — Theoretical O₂ required (% by mass of air):** $$\% O_2 = \dfrac{9.5 \times 32}{45.22 \times 28.97} \times 100 = \dfrac{304}{1310.04} \times 100 = 23.2\%$$ > **Answer:** Theoretical O₂ requirement ≈ **23.2% by mass** of the air supplied (slightly higher than atmospheric 23.0% by mass because stoichiometric combustion of hexane uses almost all oxygen). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Flash Point with Fire Point** — they differ by 5–10 °C. Many students write the same value for both. FP gives a momentary flash; FPT sustains ≥ 5 s. > 2. **Using molar A/F instead of mass A/F** — KTU expects mass basis unless explicitly stated. > 3. **Forgetting the 3.76 N₂ multiplier** — Air = O₂ + 3.76 N₂ on a molar basis; omitting this halves the A/F ratio. > 4. **Incorrect unit in AIT** — Always in **°C**, never K, unless converted. > 5. **Treating Fire Triangle and Fire Tetrahedron as identical** — they are NOT. The fourth element (chain reaction) is examinable. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Factors Contributing Towards Fire** - 🔥 **Fire Triangle** = Fuel + Oxygen + Heat (≥ AIT). - 🔺 **Fire Tetrahedron** = Triangle + Chemical Chain Reaction (free radicals H•, OH•, O•). - 📐 **Hierarchical order of temperatures** for any liquid fuel: **Flash Point < Fire Point < Auto-Ignition Temperature**. - 💨 **Minimum O₂** to sustain combustion ≈ **12–16%** (most organics). - 📊 **LEL** = lower flammable limit; **UEL** = upper flammable limit; outside this range, **no fire**. - ⚖️ **Stoichiometric A/F (mass)** for CH₄ ≈ **17.24**; for C₆H₁₄ ≈ **15.23**. - 🧯 **Fire extinguishment methods** correspond to removing one side of the triangle: **Cooling** (water), **Smothering** (CO₂, foam), **Starvation** (removing fuel), **Inhibition** (chain-breaking agents like dry chemical powder). - 🏭 **Industrial fire causation** has **five roots**: Material, Environmental, Human, Equipment, Process. - 🚫 **Powders and dusts** are extremely hazardous because of their huge surface-area-to-volume ratio. - 📜 **Five fire classes**: A (solids), B (liquids/gases), C (metals), D (electrical), K (cooking oils). - 🌡️ **Static electricity** during fuel transfer is a frequent, preventable cause of industrial fires — always earth/bond containers. - 🧪 **Spontaneous combustion** of oily rags is a classic human-factor fire cause; dispose of in metal bins. - 🔌 **Hot work permits** are mandatory before welding/cutting in flammable areas. - 📈 **Oxygen enrichment** above 21% drastically lowers ignition energy and AIT — handle oxidizers with extreme care.

topics.title: Classification of Fire and Fire Extinguishers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET637 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Classification of Fire and Fire Extinguishers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Fire > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fire (KTU 2024 Definition):** Fire is a rapid, self-sustaining **oxidation-reduction (redox) chemical reaction** that releases **heat, light, smoke, and various toxic gases** as by-products. It occurs when three essential elements — commonly visualized as the **Fire Triangle** — are simultaneously present in appropriate proportions. ### 1.2 Fire Triangle vs. Fire Tetrahedron The classical **Fire Triangle** (proposed early in combustion science) has evolved into a more accurate **Fire Tetrahedron** under modern industrial safety standards. **Fire Triangle (3 Elements):** | Element | Symbol | Role | |---|---|---| | **Fuel** | F | Combustible material (solid, liquid, gas) | | **Oxygen** | O₂ | Oxidizer (typically ≥ **16% by volume** in air) | | **Heat (Ignition Source)** | T | Activation energy to initiate combustion | > [!NOTE] > **Fire Tetrahedron (4th Element):** Modern safety engineering adds a fourth face — the **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction (Sₙ)**. Without this self-propagating radical chain, combustion cannot sustain itself. This is critical for understanding why certain extinguishers (e.g., **dry chemical powder**) are effective — they chemically interrupt the chain reaction. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a three-legged stool (Fire Triangle). If you remove **any one leg**, the stool falls — and the fire goes out. Now, imagine the **Fire Tetrahedron** as a four-legged dining table: removing any single leg (heat, fuel, oxygen, *or* chain reaction) collapses the entire structure. This is exactly how fire extinguishers work — by **"pulling the rug"** from one of these four essential elements. ### 1.4 Standardized Fire Classes KTU 2024 follows the **IS 15683:2018 / NFPA 10 / BS EN 2** classification: $$\text{Classes} = \{A,\ B,\ C,\ D,\ E,\ F\}$$ Each class is defined by the **type of fuel** involved, which directly determines the correct extinguishing media. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Fire Triangle — Element-by-Element Analysis **Element 1: FUEL (Combustible Material)** - Solid (e.g., wood, paper, cloth, rubber) - Liquid (e.g., petrol, diesel, solvents, oils) - Gaseous (e.g., LPG, methane, acetylene, hydrogen) - Metal (e.g., magnesium, sodium, potassium, titanium) **Element 2: OXYGEN (Oxidizer)** - Atmospheric oxygen (**21% O₂ by volume** in normal air) - Fire is generally sustained when **O₂ ≥ 16%** - Below **14% O₂**, most common fires self-extinguish (used in **inert gas suppression**) **Element 3: HEAT (Activation Energy)** - Minimum temperature to ignite a fuel is called the **Ignition Temperature** ($T_{ig}$) - Heat from ignition continues to pyrolyze more fuel, sustaining combustion **Element 4: UNINHIBITED CHAIN REACTION** - Free radicals (H•, O•, OH•) propagate combustion at the flame front - Halogenated agents and dry chemical powders disrupt this chain ### 2.2 Detailed Classification of Fires (KTU 2024 — High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following classification table is **the single most exam-relevant summary** for this module. Memorize the fuel type, symbol, and example for each class. | Class | Fuel Type | Common Examples | Ignition Behavior | |---|---|---|---| | **Class A** | Ordinary Combustibles (Solid organics) | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber, plastics, trash | Leaves **ash / char** residue | | **Class B** | Flammable Liquids & Gases | Petrol, diesel, kerosene, LPG, paint, solvents | **No ash**; vapor-phase burning | | **Class C** | Flammable Gases (under pressure) | Methane, propane, hydrogen, acetylene, CNG | Rapid flame propagation | | **Class D** | Combustible Metals | Magnesium, sodium, potassium, aluminum, titanium | Extremely high temperatures (often **> 1000°C**) | | **Class E** *(India / IS 15683)* | Electrical Fires | Live transformers, switchgear, motors, panels | Cannot use water; non-conductive media required | | **Class F** *(UK / Europe)* | Cooking Oils & Fats | Vegetable oil, animal fat, grease in kitchens | Re-ignition risk; **wet chemical** needed | > [!NOTE] > **Class E in India vs. International Standards:** In the **USA (NFPA)**, electrical fires are NOT a separate class — they are categorized by the fuel type once power is disconnected. The **IS 15683:2018 (Indian Standard)** retains a separate **Class E** for energized electrical equipment, and **Class F** specifically for kitchen/cooking oil fires. ### 2.3 Principles of Fire Extinguishment — The "4S" Model Every fire extinguisher works by attacking **one (or more)** elements of the fire tetrahedron: 1. **Starvation** — Removing the fuel source (e.g., shut-off valve on gas line) 2. **Smothering** — Cutting off the oxygen supply (e.g., foam blanket, CO₂, dry powder) 3. **Cooling** — Reducing temperature below ignition point (e.g., water, foam) 4. **Chemical Inhibition** — Breaking the chain reaction (e.g., dry chemical powder, halon alternatives) ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Selection Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **No closed-form mathematical equations exist for fire classification itself.** However, the following **engineering relations** govern the design of suppression systems and extinguishers. | Formula / Relation | Description | Unit | |---|---|---| | $Q_{removed} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat absorbed by cooling agent | kW | | $V_{CO_2} = k \cdot V_{room}$ | Volume of CO₂ required for room flooding | m³ | | $\phi = \dfrac{[Fuel/O_{2}]}{[Fuel/O_{2}]_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio (rich if $\phi > 1$, lean if $\phi < 1$) | dimensionless | | $t_{discharge} = \dfrac{V_{cylinder}}{Q_{nozzle}}$ | Effective discharge time of extinguisher | seconds | | $O_2 \leq 14\%$ | Threshold below which fire self-extinguishes | % by volume | ### 2.5 Selection of Extinguisher — Engineering Logic The correct extinguisher is selected by matching the **fire class** to the **extinguishing mechanism** and ensuring **non-conductivity** (for Class E) and **non-reactivity** (for Class D). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Oxygen Concentration for Sustained Combustion To understand **why CO₂ and inert gas suppression works**, we derive the limiting oxygen concentration (LOC) for a generic hydrocarbon fuel $C_xH_yO_z$. **Step 1 — Write the general stoichiometric combustion equation:** $$\begin{aligned} C_xH_yO_z + a(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O + d\,N_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Apply atom balance:** - Carbon: $b = x$ - Hydrogen: $2c = y \Rightarrow c = y/2$ - Oxygen: $b + c/2 = z + 2a$ Substituting $b$ and $c$: $$\begin{aligned} x + \frac{y}{4} &= z + 2a \\ \Rightarrow a &= x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2} \end{aligned}$$ This $a$ is the **stoichiometric oxygen requirement per mole of fuel**. **Step 3 — Compute mole fraction of O₂ at stoichiometric condition:** $$\begin{aligned} n_{total} &= a(1 + 3.76) + 1 \\ &= 4.76a + 1 \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} X_{O_2,\,stoich} &= \frac{a}{4.76a + 1} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the empirical Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC) factor:** Most flammable hydrocarbons exhibit: $$\begin{aligned} LOC &\approx 0.5 \cdot X_{O_2,\,stoich} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Practical engineering rule (memorize for KTU):** > [!TIP] > $$ \boxed{\,LOC \approx 10\% \text{ to } 14\%\ \text{(by volume)}\,} $$ > Below this, even a stoichiometric fuel-air mixture cannot sustain flame. Hence, **CO₂ flooding** and **inert gas systems** are designed to reduce ambient O₂ below this threshold. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Heat Removal Requirement for Water-Cooled Extinction To extinguish a Class A fire, the cooling water must remove sufficient heat to drop the fuel surface below its **ignition temperature**. **Step 1 — Define the heat balance:** $$\begin{aligned} Q_{absorbed\,by\,water} &\geq Q_{released\,by\,fuel} \\ m_w \cdot [c_{p,\,w} \cdot (100 - T_0) + L_v + c_{p,\,steam} \cdot (T_{ig} - 100)] &= m_f \cdot \Delta H_c \end{aligned}$$ where: - $m_w$ = mass of water (kg) - $c_{p,w} = 4.18\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ = specific heat of water - $L_v = 2260\ \text{kJ/kg}$ = latent heat of vaporization - $c_{p,steam} = 2.01\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ - $T_{ig}$ = ignition temperature of fuel (K) - $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion of fuel (kJ/kg) - $m_f$ = mass of fuel consumed (kg) **Step 2 — Simplify by combining water-to-steam enthalpy:** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta h_{water} &= c_{p,w} \cdot (100 - T_0) + L_v + c_{p,steam} \cdot (T_{ig} - 100) \\ &\approx 4.18(100 - T_0) + 2260 + 2.01(T_{ig} - 100) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Required water-to-fuel mass ratio:** $$\begin{aligned} \frac{m_w}{m_f} = \frac{\Delta H_c}{\Delta h_{water}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical sanity check for wood:** - $\Delta H_c$ (wood) $\approx 17{,}000\ \text{kJ/kg}$ - $T_0 = 25°C$, $T_{ig} = 300°C$ - $\Delta h_{water} \approx 4.18(75) + 2260 + 2.01(200) \approx 313.5 + 2260 + 402 = 2975.5\ \text{kJ/kg}$ $$\begin{aligned} \frac{m_w}{m_f} = \frac{17{,}000}{2975.5} \approx 5.71 \end{aligned}$$ > [!TIP] > **Conclusion:** Approximately **5.7 kg of water** is required to fully absorb the heat released by burning **1 kg of wood**. This justifies why Class A extinguishers are rated by their water equivalence. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Fire Class × Extinguisher Compatibility > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Master Compatibility Matrix" — must-know for KTU 14-mark questions.** | Extinguisher Type | Agent | Mechanism | Class A | Class B | Class C | Class D | Class E | Class F | |---|---|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Water (W)** | H₂O | Cooling | ✅ Best | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | **Foam (AFFF)** | Aqueous Film Forming Foam | Smother + Cool | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ⚠ Limited | | **Dry Powder (ABC)** | Monoammonium Phosphate | Chain inhibition | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | | **Dry Powder (D-type)** | NaCl / Cu powder | Smother + Starve | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | | **CO₂** | Carbon Dioxide | Smother | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | | **Wet Chemical** | Potassium Acetate | Saponification | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | **Clean Agent** | FM-200 / Novec | Chain inhibition | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | | **Halon** | BCF / BTM | Chain inhibition | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | **Legend:** ✅ Suitable | ❌ Unsafe / Ineffective | ⚠ Limited Use --- ### 3.4 Step-by-Step Logic for Selecting a Fire Extinguisher **Step 1** — Identify the **fire class** from the fuel involved. **Step 2** — Check for **electrical hazard** (energized equipment present?). **Step 3** — Check for **metal hazard** (combustible metal dust or shavings?). **Step 4** — Match the **extinguishing mechanism** (Cool / Smother / Inhibit / Starve). **Step 5** — Verify **non-conductivity** if Class E risk exists. **Step 6** — Select the appropriate **extinguisher type and capacity** as per **IS 15683** sizing tables. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Fire Tetrahedron — Mermaid Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR node1[FUEL
Solid Liquid Gas] node2[OXYGEN
Air 21 percent O2] node3[HEAT
Ignition Energy] node4[CHAIN REACTION
Free Radicals H O OH] nodeFire[FIRE
Sustained Combustion] node1 --> nodeFire node2 --> nodeFire node3 --> nodeFire node4 --> nodeFire style nodeFire fill:#FF4500,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF style node1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000 style node2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000000 style node3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF style node4 fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,color:#FFFFFF ``` ### 4.2 Fire Classification Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart[Fire Detected] nodeQ1{Fuel Type?} nodeA[Class A
Solid Organics] nodeB[Class B
Flammable Liquid] nodeC[Class C
Flammable Gas] nodeD[Class D
Combustible Metal] nodeE[Class E
Electrical Live] nodeF[Class F
Cooking Oil Fat] nodeA --> nodeAnsA[Water or ABC Powder] nodeB --> nodeAnsB[Foam or CO2 or ABC] nodeC --> nodeAnsC[Dry Powder ABC] nodeD --> nodeAnsD[Special D Type Powder] nodeE --> nodeAnsE[CO2 or Clean Agent] nodeF --> nodeAnsF[Wet Chemical K Acetate] nodeStart --> nodeQ1 nodeQ1 -->|Solid Char| nodeA nodeQ1 -->|Liquid Vapor| nodeB nodeQ1 -->|Pressurized Gas| nodeC nodeQ1 -->|Reactive Metal| nodeD nodeQ1 -->|Energized Equipment| nodeE nodeQ1 -->|Kitchen Oil| nodeF style nodeStart fill:#FF0000,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF style nodeA fill:#00FF00,stroke:#006400,color:#000000 style nodeB fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000 style nodeC fill:#FFFF00,stroke:#808000,color:#000000 style nodeD fill:#808080,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF style nodeE fill:#0000FF,stroke:#000080,color:#FFFFFF style nodeF fill:#A52A2A,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF ``` ### 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Fire Extinguishing System ```mermaid flowchart LR node1[Detection Unit
Smoke Heat Flame Sensor] node2[Control Panel
Logic and Decision Unit] node3[Agent Storage
Cylinder Bank] node4[Distribution Network
Piping and Nozzles] node5[Hazard Zone
Protected Area] node6[Alarm and Ventilation] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node2 --> node6 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 style node1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000 style node2 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000 style node3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000 style node4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,color:#FFFFFF style node5 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF style node6 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.4 IS 15683 Color-Coding Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeTitle[IS 15683 Color Code for Fire Extinguishers] nodeA[Water Extinguisher
Color Signal RED] nodeB[Foam Extemguisher
Color Signal CREAM] nodeC[CO2 Extinguisher
Color Signal BLACK] nodeD[ABC Powder
Color Signal BLUE] nodeE[Wet Chemical
Color Signal YELLOW] nodeF[D Type Powder
Color Signal LIGHT BLUE] nodeTitle --> nodeA nodeTitle --> nodeB nodeTitle --> nodeC nodeTitle --> nodeD nodeTitle --> nodeE nodeTitle --> nodeF style nodeTitle fill:#000080,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF style nodeA fill:#FF0000,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF style nodeB fill:#FFFDD0,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000000 style nodeC fill:#000000,stroke:#FFFFFF,color:#FFFFFF style nodeD fill:#0000FF,stroke:#000080,color:#FFFFFF style nodeE fill:#FFFF00,stroke:#808000,color:#000000 style nodeF fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define "Fire Tetrahedron" and explain how it differs from the "Fire Triangle."** **Model Answer:** The **Fire Triangle** is a classical model representing fire as the simultaneous combination of three elements: **Fuel, Oxygen, and Heat**. It adequately describes the *initiation* of fire. The **Fire Tetrahedron** is a more advanced model that adds a fourth element: the **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction** (free radicals like H•, O•, OH• propagating in the flame zone). It represents the *sustenance* of fire. **Key difference:** Removing a leg of the triangle stops ignition; removing a leg of the tetrahedron (especially via chemical inhibitors like dry powder) **terminates sustained combustion** by disrupting the radical chain. (3 marks: 1 for triangle, 1 for tetrahedron, 1 for distinction) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **List the different classes of fire as per IS 15683 with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Class | Fuel | Example | |---|---|---| | A | Solid combustibles | Wood | | B | Flammable liquids | Petrol | | C | Flammable gases | LPG | | D | Combustible metals | Magnesium | | E | Electrical (live) | Transformer fire | | F | Cooking oil/fat | Kitchen deep fryer fire | (3 marks: 0.5 for each class) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Classify the different types of fires as per **IS 15683:2018**. Explain with neat sketches the **fire triangle** and the **fire tetrahedron**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe in detail the various **types of portable fire extinguishers**, their extinguishing media, working principle, and suitable applications. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution (a):** **Fire Classification (IS 15683:2018):** The Indian Standard **IS 15683** categorizes fire into **six classes** based on the nature of fuel: $$\text{Classes} = \{A,\ B,\ C,\ D,\ E,\ F\}$$ **Class A — Ordinary Combustibles:** - Fuel: Solid organic materials like wood, paper, cloth, rubber, plastics - Burning characteristic: Produces **ash/char**; smoldering + flaming - Example: Office trash fire, warehouse paper fire **Class B — Flammable Liquids:** - Fuel: Petrol, diesel, kerosene, paints, solvents - Burning characteristic: **Vapor-phase combustion**; no ash - Example: Fuel tanker spill fire **Class C — Flammable Gases:** - Fuel: LPG, methane, propane, hydrogen, acetylene - Burning characteristic: **Jet fire / flash fire**; rapid flame propagation - Example: Gas pipeline rupture **Class D — Combustible Metals:** - Fuel: Magnesium, sodium, potassium, aluminum (powder), titanium - Burning characteristic: **Extremely high temperatures** (>1000°C); reacts violently with water - Example: Machining shop fire involving magnesium chips **Class E — Electrical Fires (India-specific):** - Fuel: Energized electrical equipment - Burning characteristic: Must use **non-conductive extinguishing media** - Example: Live transformer fire, control panel fire **Class F — Cooking Oils & Fats (UK-origin, adopted by IS):** - Fuel: Vegetable oil, ghee, animal fat - Burning characteristic: **Re-ignition** risk; high auto-ignition temperature (~300-360°C) - Example: Kitchen deep fryer fire **Fire Triangle:** The classical model comprises three elements: - **Fuel (F)** - **Oxygen (O₂)** - **Heat (T)** — Ignition energy ``` FUEL / \ / \ / FIRE \ / \ H ————————— O₂ HEAT ``` **Fire Tetrahedron:** Modern model adds the **4th element** — **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction** — to form a 3D pyramid: ``` FUEL / \ / \ / FIRE \ / TETRA \ /——————————\ HEAT —————— O₂ + Chain Reaction ``` **Distinction:** The triangle explains **ignition**; the tetrahedron explains **sustained combustion** and the role of free radicals. *(7 marks: 2 for classification, 2 for triangle diagram + explanation, 2 for tetrahedron, 1 for distinction)* --- **Model Solution (b):** **Portable Fire Extinguishers — Detailed Description:** **1. Water-Type Extinguisher (W):** - **Agent:** Plain water (sometimes with anti-freeze additive) - **Color Code (IS 15683):** **RED** - **Mechanism:** **Cooling** — absorbs heat via high specific heat and latent heat of vaporization - **Application:** **Class A** fires only - **Capacity:** 9 L (standard) - **Discharge time:** 60–90 seconds **2. Foam-Type Extinguisher (AFFF):** - **Agent:** Aqueous Film Forming Foam - **Color Code:** **CREAM** - **Mechanism:** **Smothering + Cooling** — foam blankets liquid surface, cuts off oxygen - **Application:** **Class A and B** fires - **Cannot be used for:** Electrical (Class E) or metal (Class D) fires **3. Dry Chemical Powder (ABC):** - **Agent:** Monoammonium phosphate (MAP) — multi-purpose - **Color Code:** **BLUE** - **Mechanism:** **Chain inhibition** — interrupts free radical reaction - **Application:** **Class A, B, C, and E** (versatile) - **Capacity:** 1, 2, 4, 6, 9 kg - **Best general-purpose extinguisher** **4. D-Type Dry Powder:** - **Agent:** Sodium chloride (NaCl) or copper-based powder - **Color Code:** **LIGHT BLUE** - **Mechanism:** **Smothering + Starvation** — forms crust on burning metal, cuts off O₂ - **Application:** **Class D** fires only - **Note:** Never use ABC powder on metal fires — can react violently **5. CO₂ Extinguisher:** - **Agent:** Liquid CO₂ stored at ~70 bar - **Color Code:** **BLACK** - **Mechanism:** **Smothering** — displaces O₂; also provides mild cooling - **Application:** **Class B and E** fires (non-conductive, leaves no residue) - **Ideal for:** Server rooms, electrical panels, laboratories - **Disadvantage:** Limited cooling; re-ignition risk in deep-seated fires **6. Wet Chemical Extinguisher:** - **Agent:** Potassium acetate / citrate solution - **Color Code:** **YELLOW** - **Mechanism:** **Saponification** — reacts with hot oil to form soap-like layer - **Application:** **Class F (and K)** fires — deep fat fryers - **Cooling + Smothering combined** **7. Clean Agent Extinguishers:** - **Agent:** FM-200 (HFC-227ea), Novec 1230 - **Color Code:** **GREEN** (some standards) - **Mechanism:** **Chain inhibition** - **Application:** Class A, B, E — used in data centers, museums (no residue) - **Environmentally preferred** over Halon **8. Halon Extinguishers (Phase-out):** - **Agent:** BCF, BTM - **Mechanism:** **Chain inhibition** — highly effective - **Application:** Class A, B, E - **Banned under Montreal Protocol** (ozone-depleting); replaced by clean agents **Selection Table:** | Class | Best Extinguisher | |---|---| | A | Water / ABC | | B | Foam / CO₂ / ABC | | C | ABC Powder | | D | D-Type Powder | | E | CO₂ / Clean Agent | | F | Wet Chemical | *(7 marks: 1 mark per extinguisher type with all four parameters: agent, color, mechanism, application)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the **four principles of fire extinguishment** (4S Model). How does each principle relate to one face of the fire tetrahedron? *(7 marks)* **(b)** For a workplace storing **petrol, LPG cylinders, magnesium scrap, and live electrical panels**, recommend the **specific type of fire extinguisher** for each hazard with justification. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution (a):** **The 4S Model of Fire Extinguishment:** Any fire can be extinguished by attacking **one or more** of the four legs of the fire tetrahedron. The four principles are: **1. STARVATION (Fuel Removal):** - **Definition:** Removing or isolating the combustible material so that the fire has no fuel to consume. - **Targets tetrahedron face:** **FUEL** - **Examples:** - Shutting off the **gas valve** in an LPG leak fire - Closing a **fuel line** in a diesel engine fire - Removing unburnt material away from the fire zone - Creating **firebreaks** in forest fires **2. SMOTHERING (Oxygen Exclusion):** - **Definition:** Preventing oxygen from reaching the combustion zone, or reducing O₂ concentration below the **limiting oxygen concentration (LOC ≈ 14%)**. - **Targets tetrahedron face:** **OXYGEN** - **Examples:** - **Foam blanket** on a liquid pool fire - **CO₂ flooding** in a confined space - **Fire blankets** on small pan fires - **Sand** on a metal fire - **Mathematical basis:** $O_2 \leq 14\%$ → flame self-extinguishes **3. COOLING (Heat Removal):** - **Definition:** Reducing the temperature of the fuel below its **ignition temperature** so that it can no longer release combustible vapors. - **Targets tetrahedron face:** **HEAT** - **Examples:** - **Water spray** on Class A fires (highest specific heat + latent heat) - **Foam** (which is ~80% water) - **Cooling adjacent structures** to prevent fire spread **Mathematical basis:** $$Q_{removed} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_v$$ **4. CHEMICAL INHIBITION (Chain Breaking):** - **Definition:** Introducing chemical agents that disrupt the free-radical chain reaction in the flame zone. - **Targets tetrahedron face:** **CHAIN REACTION** - **Examples:** - **Dry chemical powder (ABC)** — interrupts H• and OH• radicals - **Halon / Clean agents** — bond with free radicals - **Inert gases** (Ar, N₂) — dilute radical concentration **Uniqueness of Chemical Inhibition:** This is the only method that does **not** require removing fuel, oxygen, or heat — it directly "kills" the flame chemistry. **Summary Mapping:** | Principle | Tetrahedron Face | Extinguisher Example | |---|---|---| | Starvation | Fuel | Gas shut-off valve | | Smothering | Oxygen | CO₂, Foam, Sand | | Cooling | Heat | Water | | Chemical Inhibition | Chain Reaction | ABC Dry Powder, Halon | *(7 marks: 1.5 per principle with face mapping + example)* --- **Model Solution (b):** **Workplace Hazard Analysis & Extinguisher Selection:** **Hazard 1: PETROL Storage (Class B Fire)** - **Fire class:** Class B (flammable liquid) - **Recommended extinguisher:** **Foam (AFFF) Extinguisher** — Cream color - **Justification:** - Foam blankets the petrol surface, cutting off oxygen (smothering) and also cools (cooling) - Prevents re-ignition by sealing vapor release - **Alternative:** ABC Dry Powder or CO₂ for small spills - **Why NOT water:** Water sinks below petrol, doesn't extinguish; may spread the burning liquid **Hazard 2: LPG Cylinders (Class C Fire)** - **Fire class:** Class C (flammable gas under pressure) - **Recommended extinguisher:** **ABC Dry Chemical Powder** — Blue color - **Justification:** - Powder cloud interrupts the radical chain reaction (chemical inhibition) - Works even at high gas pressure - Powder also provides mild smothering - **Critical SOP:** First shut off the gas supply (starvation), then extinguish - **Capacity:** Minimum **4 kg** for domestic; **6–9 kg** for industrial LPG banks **Hazard 3: Magnesium Scrap (Class D Fire)** - **Fire class:** Class D (combustible metal) - **Recommended extinguisher:** **D-Type Dry Powder (Sodium Chloride or Copper-based)** — Light Blue color - **Justification:** - Forms a crust over the burning metal, blocking oxygen (smothering + starvation) - Tolerates extremely high temperatures (>1000°C) of magnesium combustion - **Why NOT water:** Water reacts violently with burning magnesium, releasing hydrogen gas → **explosion risk** - **Why NOT CO₂:** Inadequate cooling; may not suppress deep-seated metal fires - **Why NOT ABC Powder:** MAP can decompose at high metal-fire temperatures **Hazard 4: Live Electrical Panels (Class E Fire)** - **Fire class:** Class E (energized electrical) - **Recommended extinguisher:** **CO₂ Extinguisher** — Black color - **Justification:** - CO₂ is a **non-conductor** of electricity → safe for energized equipment - Leaves **no residue** (no damage to electronic components) - Displaces oxygen around the panel (smothering) - **Alternative:** Clean Agent (FM-200 / Novec 1230) for server rooms - **Why NOT water or foam:** Water is conductive → electrocution hazard **Summary Selection Table:** | Hazard | Fire Class | Extinguisher | IS 15683 Color | |---|---|---|---| | Petrol | B | Foam (AFFF) | Cream | | LPG | C | ABC Dry Powder | Blue | | Magnesium | D | D-Type Powder | Light Blue | | Electrical Panel | E | CO₂ | Black | **Final Workplace Recommendation (IS 15683 Compliant):** A typical industrial workplace should maintain at minimum **one extinguisher per 200 m² of floor area**, with extinguisher types positioned based on **hazard zoning** as mapped above. *(7 marks: 1.5 for each hazard analysis + 1 for summary table)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make — avoid these to score full marks:** > 1. **Confusing "Class E" internationally:** Many students write "there is no Class E in NFPA" but then forget to mention that **IS 15683 (India) DOES have Class E**. KTU expects India-specific answers. Always clarify the standard being followed. > 2. **Writing "water can be used for any fire":** Wrong. Water on Class C (gas) and Class E (electrical) fires can cause **explosions and electrocution**. Water on Class D fires releases **hydrogen gas**. > 3. **Forgetting the color code:** IS 15683 mandates specific color codes for extinguisher bodies. Examiners allocate **0.5–1 mark** for color codes — missing them is a guaranteed deduction. > 4. **Omitting the 4th element of Fire Tetrahedron:** Many students draw only the triangle. The **chain reaction** element is the key differentiator for **chemical inhibition** extinguishers (ABC, Halon). > 5. **Conflating "ABC" with "D-type" powder:** ABC powder is **monoammonium phosphate**; D-type is **sodium chloride or copper-based**. Using ABC on a Class D fire is a serious safety violation. > 6. **Not mentioning "IS 15683" in answers:** This is the **Bureau of Indian Standard** reference expected for all India-based KTU answers. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **🔥 High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — must know for KTU 2024 exam** - ✅ **Fire Triangle** = Fuel + Oxygen + Heat (initiation) - ✅ **Fire Tetrahedron** = Triangle + **Chain Reaction** (sustenance) - ✅ **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)** ≈ **10–14%** by volume - ✅ **Six Fire Classes per IS 15683:** A, B, C, D, E, F - ✅ **Class A** = Solids (wood, paper) → **Water** extinguisher (RED) - ✅ **Class B** = Flammable liquids (petrol) → **Foam/CO₂/ABC** (CREAM) - ✅ **Class C** = Flammable gases (LPG) → **ABC Powder** (BLUE) - ✅ **Class D** = Metals (magnesium) → **D-Type Powder** (LIGHT BLUE) - ✅ **Class E** = Electrical (live) → **CO₂ / Clean Agent** (BLACK) — India-specific - ✅ **Class F** = Cooking oil/fat → **Wet Chemical** (YELLOW) - ✅ **4S Extinguishment Model:** **Starvation, Smothering, Cooling, Chemical Inhibition** - ✅ **ABC Dry Powder** works on A, B, C, E — the **most versatile** portable extinguisher - ✅ **NEVER use water** on Class C, D, E fires - ✅ **Halon extinguishers** are **banned** (Montreal Protocol — ozone depletion) - ✅ **Selection rule:** Class E → non-conductive agent; Class D → non-reactive agent - ✅ **IS 15683** is the governing Indian Standard for portable extinguishers - ✅ **Siting rule:** 1 extinguisher per **200 m²** minimum in industrial workplaces ---

topics.title: Explosion - Toxic Gas Release - Precautions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET637 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Explosion - Toxic Gas Release - Precautions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Explosion — Formal KTU Definition

An **explosion** is a sudden, violent release of energy accompanied by a **shock wave**, **loud noise**, **flash of light**, **heat**, and the projection of **fragmented material**. From an Industrial Safety perspective (KTU 2024 Scheme, PEMET637 Module 2), an explosion is a *rapid, near-instantaneous conversion of potential energy (chemical, mechanical, nuclear, or pressurized) into kinetic energy of expanding gases*, producing a **blast overpressure wave** that can cause catastrophic structural damage, human fatality, and secondary fires.

**Energy classifications used in KTU Safety Engineering:**

| Energy Class | Source | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemical** | Rapid oxidation (combustion) | LPG-air VCE, Methane detonation |
| **Mechanical** | Pressure vessel rupture | Boiler BLEVE, Compressor burst |
| **Physical** | Phase change / Liquefied gas expansion | LNG spill rapid vaporization |
| **Electrical** | Arc blast, short-circuit | Switchgear arc-flash (up to 35,000 °C) |
| **Nuclear** | Fission / fusion | Out of syllabus scope (referral only) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim expected in answers):** *An explosion is a rapid, self-propagating transformation of a system producing a sudden, substantial rise in pressure, accompanied by the generation of a shock wave and the release of energy, light, heat, and gases.* — Memorize this phrasing for 7-mark questions.

---

## 1.2 The Combustion Triangle (Fire Triangle Extension)

For a chemical explosion to occur, three elements must coexist **simultaneously** — known as the **Fire Triangle** or **Explosion Triangle**:

1. **Fuel** (combustible gas, vapor, mist, or dust)
2. **Oxidizer** (typically atmospheric **O₂ ≥ 21%**, or pure O₂ / oxidizing chemicals like H₂O₂, KClO₃)
3. **Ignition Source** (spark, hot surface, static discharge, adiabatic compression)

A fourth element — **the confinement / mixing condition** — converts a *deflagration* (subsonic) into a *detonation* (supersonic) explosion. This is sometimes called the **Explosion Tetrahedron** (or **Combustion Diamond**).

---

## 1.3 Toxic Gas Release — Formal KTU Definition

A **Toxic Gas Release** is the *uncontrolled escape, emission, or spillage of a gaseous, vaporized, or aerosolized hazardous chemical* into the workplace or ambient atmosphere, in concentrations exceeding the prescribed **Threshold Limit Value (TLV)** or **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL)**, posing acute or chronic health hazards to exposed personnel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Hygiene Metrics (must memorize):**
> - **TLV-TWA** = Time-Weighted Average (8-hr exposure) — *e.g., H₂S TWA = 10 ppm*
> - **TLV-STEL** = Short-Term Exposure Limit (15-min ceiling) — *e.g., H₂S STEL = 15 ppm*
> - **TLV-C** = Ceiling Limit (instantaneous, never to exceed) — *e.g., NH₃ Ceiling = 35 ppm*
> - **IDLH** = Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health — *e.g., H₂S IDLH = 100 ppm; Cl₂ IDLH = 10 ppm*
> - **Odor Threshold** — *e.g., H₂S detectable by smell at 0.0005 ppm (olfactory fatigue begins at ~100 ppm — making H₂S deceptively dangerous)*

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> **Think of a Pressure Cooker as a Miniature Explosion Vessel:**
> Imagine a domestic pressure cooker on a gas stove. Inside is food (the **fuel**), air sealed by the gasket (the **oxidizer in confined space**), and the heating element (the **ignition source**). The weighted whistle valve is the engineered **pressure-relief safety device**. Now imagine a factory-scale chemical reactor playing the same role — but with toxic or flammable gases, and no whistle. If the engineered relief fails, the cooker becomes a **missile** — that is precisely what a BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion) does in a propane storage bullet.
>
> For **toxic gas release**, picture a leaking LPG cylinder in a closed kitchen. You cannot see the gas, but you can smell the mercaptan (odorized additive). In a chlor-alkali plant, a leaking Cl₂ line is *invisible* and *odor-piercing* — even a few breaths at IDLH concentration can cause pulmonary edema within hours. **Industrial precautions** are essentially the engineering equivalent of installing a smoke alarm, an exhaust hood, and a gas shut-off valve — *all working in concert*.

---

## 1.5 Precautions — Definition

**Precautions** in industrial safety refer to the *proactive, multi-layered set of engineering, administrative, and personal-protective measures* designed to **prevent**, **control**, and **mitigate** the consequences of explosion and toxic gas release events. KTU follows the well-known **Hierarchy of Controls**:

1. **Elimination** (remove the hazard entirely)
2. **Substitution** (use a less hazardous chemical/process)
3. **Engineering Controls** (ventilation, gas detectors, relief valves, inerting)
4. **Administrative Controls** (training, SOPs, permits-to-work)
5. **PPE** (respirators, SCBA, chemical suits — *last line of defense*)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Explosions (High-Yield KTU Topic)

### A. Based on Speed of Reaction

| Type | Speed | Pressure Rise | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deflagration** | Subsonic (< 340 m/s) | Slow pressure rise | LPG pool fire |
| **Detonation** | Supersonic (> 1,000 m/s) | Near-instantaneous | TNT, gas-phase H₂-air |

### B. Based on Physical Mechanism

- **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE):** A large mass of flammable vapor mixes with air and ignites. *Buncefield 2005, Jaipur 2009.*
- **BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion):** A pressure vessel containing a liquid above its atmospheric boiling point ruptures catastrophically (e.g., LPG bullet fire).
- **Dust Explosion:** Combustible dust (grain, coal, sugar, aluminum, flour) disperses in air, ignites. *Primary* explosion lifts settled dust → *Secondary* explosion (often more devastating).
- **Unconfined Vapor Cloud Explosion (UVCE):** Open-atmosphere VCE.
- **Confined Explosion:** Inside vessels, rooms, or ducts.
- **Mechanical Explosion:** Failure of pressurized equipment (steam boiler, air receiver, hydraulic accumulator).

---

## 2.2 Flammability Characteristics — Critical Numerical Parameters

For every flammable gas/vapor, four numerical values are mandatory for KTU answers:

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Example (Methane, CH₄) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lower Explosive Limit** | **LEL / LFL** | Minimum concentration in air that can ignite | 5.0 % vol |
| **Upper Explosive Limit** | **UEL / UFL** | Maximum concentration in air that can ignite | 15.0 % vol |
| **Flash Point** | **FP** | Lowest temperature at which liquid gives off ignitable vapor | (gas — N/A) |
| **Auto-Ignition Temperature** | **AIT** | Spontaneous ignition temperature without external source | 537 °C (CH₄) |
| **Minimum Ignition Energy** | **MIE** | Smallest spark energy to ignite | 0.28 mJ (CH₄) |
| **Flame Temperature** | — | Adiabatic combustion temperature | 1,950 °C (CH₄) |
| **Limiting Oxygen Concentration** | **LOC** | Min O₂ % below which combustion is impossible | 12 % (CH₄) |
| **Explosion Pressure** | **P_max** | Maximum overpressure in a closed vessel | ~7 bar (CH₄) |
| **K_G / K_St** | — | Gas / Dust explosibility index (bar·m/s) | 55 bar·m/s (CH₄) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Range of Flammability (ROF) = UEL − LEL.** A wider ROF means a *more dangerous* gas because the flammable envelope is larger. Hydrogen has the widest ROF (4% to 75% in air). Acetylene: 2.5% to 100% (can detonate in pure form).

---

## 2.3 Toxic Gas Release — Physiological Classification

| Class | Mechanism | Examples | Target Organ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Asphyxiants (Simple)** | Displace O₂ (normal atmosphere → < 19.5% O₂) | N₂, CO₂, CH₄, He | Brain, heart |
| **Asphyxiants (Chemical)** | Block O₂ transport in blood | **CO**, HCN, H₂S | Hemoglobin, cytochrome oxidase |
| **Irritants (Upper respiratory)** | Acidic/alkaline gas → tissue burn | SO₂, NH₃, HCl, HF | Nose, throat, lungs |
| **Irritants (Deep lung)** | Delayed pulmonary edema | **Cl₂**, Phosgene, NO₂, Ozone | Alveoli |
| **Systemic Toxics** | Cellular poisons (not respiratory) | Benzene, CS₂, Aniline | Bone marrow, CNS, liver |
| **Carcinogens** | Chronic — DNA damage | Benzene, Vinyl Chloride, AsH₃ | Multi-organ |

---

## 2.4 Toxicological Dose-Response Formula (Haber's Law)

> **Haber's Law (1924):**
> $$C^{n} \times t = k \quad \text{(constant)}$$
> Where $C$ = concentration (ppm or mg/m³), $t$ = exposure time (min), $n$ = toxicity exponent (typically 1 to 3), $k$ = constant for specific toxicant & effect level.

For most industrial gases, $n \approx 1$ (linear dose-response). For highly reactive gases like phosgene, $n \approx 2$.

---

## 2.5 TNT Equivalent Method (for Explosion Damage Estimation)

This is **the most frequently asked KTU numerical** on explosions. The energy released by a chemical explosion is expressed in terms of equivalent mass of TNT:

$$W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{\eta \times M \times H_{c}}{H_{\text{TNT}}}$$

Where:
- $W_{\text{TNT}}$ = equivalent TNT mass (kg)
- $\eta$ = empirical yield factor (typically 0.01 to 0.10; for VCE ≈ 0.03–0.05)
- $M$ = mass of flammable material released (kg)
- $H_{c}$ = heat of combustion of the material (kJ/kg)
- $H_{\text{TNT}}$ = heat of detonation of TNT = **4,680 kJ/kg**

**Blast overpressure from TNT at distance $R$ (meters):**
$$Z = \frac{R}{W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}}$$

Where $Z$ = scaled distance (m/kg$^{1/3}$). The peak side-on overpressure $\Delta P$ (kPa) is then read from the **Kingery-Bulmash chart** (or empirical curve).

---

## 2.6 Probit Equation for Toxic Gas Fatality (KTU 14-Mark Favorite)

The **Probit (Probability unit)** equation converts dose to a probability of fatality:

$$\text{Pr} = a + b \ln(C^{n} \cdot t)$$

Where:
- $\text{Pr}$ = Probit variable (probability of fatality is found from Pr via standard normal table)
- $C$ = toxic concentration (ppm)
- $t$ = exposure duration (min)
- $a, b, n$ = empirical constants (toxicant-specific)

| Toxic Gas | a | b | n | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Chlorine (Cl₂)** | −5.30 | 0.5 | 2.75 | Withers & Lees |
| **Ammonia (NH₃)** | −9.82 | 0.5 | 2.0 | Withers & Lees |
| **Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S)** | −11.5 | 1.0 | 1.9 | Withers & Lees |
| **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** | −7.4 | 1.0 | 1.0 | Withers & Lees |
| **Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂)** | −15.6 | 2.0 | 1.0 | Withers & Lees |
| **Phosgene (COCl₂)** | −10.6 | 2.0 | 1.0 | Withers & Lees |
| **Hydrogen Fluoride (HF)** | −25.0 | 3.0 | 1.0 | Withers & Lees |

**Conversion from Probit to Percentage:**

$$\% = 50 \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{\text{Pr} - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \right]$$

For quick KTU calculations, the approximate lookup is:

| Pr | % Fatality |
|---|---|
| 2.67 | 1 % |
| 3.72 | 5 % |
| 5.00 | 50 % |
| 6.28 | 95 % |
| 7.33 | 99 % |

---

## 2.7 Gaussian Plume Dispersion Model (Pasquill-Gifford) — Core Formula

For a **continuous ground-level release** of a toxic gas, the steady-state downwind concentration is:

$$C(x, y, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \, \sigma_{y} \, \sigma_{z} \, \bar{u}}$$

Where:
- $C$ = concentration at receptor point (kg/m³ or ppm)
- $Q$ = emission rate (kg/s)
- $\bar{u}$ = mean wind speed at release height (m/s)
- $\sigma_{y}, \sigma_{z}$ = horizontal and vertical dispersion coefficients (m), functions of downwind distance $x$ and **atmospheric stability class** (A = very unstable, B = unstable, C = slightly unstable, D = neutral, E = slightly stable, F = stable)
- $x$ = downwind distance (m)
- $y$ = crosswind distance (m)

**Pasquill-Gifford σ-formulas (Turner, 1970):**

$$\sigma_{y}(x) = a \, x^{b}$$

$$\sigma_{z}(x) = c \, x^{d} $$

(where $a, b, c, d$ are tabulated against stability class; e.g., for class D neutral: $\sigma_{y} = 0.08 x^{0.894}$, $\sigma_{z} = 0.127 x^{0.894}$)

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Use Case | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_{\text{TNT}} = \eta M H_{c} / H_{\text{TNT}}$ | Explosion energy equivalent | kg TNT |
| 2 | $Z = R / W^{1/3}$ | Scaled distance for blast | m/kg$^{1/3}$ |
| 3 | $\text{Pr} = a + b \ln(C^{n} t)$ | Toxic fatality probability | — |
| 4 | $\% = 50[1 + \text{erf}((\text{Pr}-5)/\sqrt{2})]$ | Probit → % fatality | % |
| 5 | $C(x,y,0) = Q / (\pi \sigma_{y} \sigma_{z} \bar{u})$ | Gaussian plume concentration | kg/m³ |
| 6 | $V_{LFL} = (f \times Q / C_{LFL})$ | Flammable cloud volume | m³ |
| 7 | $L_{f} = 1.4 \times (M_{f})^{1/3}$ | Flash fire lower flammable limit radius | m |
| 8 | $Q_{v} = (G \times K) / (C_{1} - C_{2}) \times 10^{6}$ | General ventilation (ACGIH) | m³/h |
| 9 | $C^{n} \times t = k$ | Haber's toxic dose | (ppm)$^n$·min |
| 10 | $\text{St} = \Delta T / \bar{u}^{2}$ | Atmospheric stability (Bulk Richardson) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where each constant appears in the answer:** KTU evaluators award **1 mark per correctly written formula with units** in any 14-mark question on explosions/gas releases. Always include SI units in the final boxed answer.

---

## 2.9 Engineering & Administrative Precautions — Comprehensive List

### A. Explosion Precautions

1. **Inerting** — keep O₂ < LOC (e.g., 5% in hydrocarbon tanks via N₂ padding)
2. **Venting** — explosion relief panels sized using NFPA 68: $A = C \times V^{2/3}$ where $A$ = vent area (m²), $V$ = enclosure volume (m³), $C$ = gas/dust constant
3. **Suppression** — fast-acting chemical suppressors (ABC dry powder, halon alternatives) activated by UV/IR detectors within milliseconds
4. **Containment** — explosion isolation valves (flamQuench, Q-Rohr)
5. **Equipment design** — to ASME BPVC Section VIII (unfired pressure vessels)
6. **Static electricity control** — bonding & grounding (resistance < 10⁶ Ω), anti-static additives
7. **Hot work permits** — spark-free tools, gas testing before entry
8. **Dust control** — housekeeping, LEV (local exhaust ventilation), dust collectors with explosion isolation

### B. Toxic Gas Release Precautions

1. **Process design** — minimize inventory of toxics, use of inherently safer design (ISD)
2. **Containment** — double-walled tanks, dyke walls (110% capacity), secondary containment
3. **Detection** — fixed gas detectors (electrochemical for Cl₂/H₂S, NDIR for CO₂, PID for VOC) with both audible & visual alarms at TLV-TWA, TLV-STEL, and IDLH set points
4. **Ventilation** — general dilution + local exhaust hood (LEV); minimum 6–10 air changes/hour for chemical labs
5. **PPE** — air-purifying respirators (APR) below IDLH; **SCBA / supplied-air** at or above IDLH
6. **Emergency response** — wind-direction indicators, evacuation routes, SCBA caches, gas-tight doors
7. **Medical surveillance** — periodic lung function tests, cholinesterase monitoring (for pesticide workers)
8. **Training** — HAZWOPER, MSDS interpretation, donning/doffing drills

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern Note:** Any 14-mark answer that does NOT mention **inerting, gas detection, SCBA, and vent sizing** will lose at least 3 marks. Always weave these four keywords into the body of your answer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: TNT Equivalent Mass for an LPG Cloud Explosion

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark style):**
> *A leaking LPG tank releases 5,000 kg of propane into the atmosphere. The propane mixes with air and forms a vapor cloud that explodes. Given: Heat of combustion of propane H_c = 46,000 kJ/kg; yield factor η = 0.04; H_TNT = 4,680 kJ/kg. Compute the equivalent TNT mass and the scaled distance at which overpressure equals 14 kPa (structural damage threshold), if the explosion occurs 250 m from a control room.*

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

From the KTU Formula Sheet (Section 2.5), the TNT equivalent is:

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{\eta \times M \times H_{c}}{H_{\text{TNT}}}
$$

**Step 2 — List all given quantities with units (KTU requires this for 1 mark).**

$$
\eta = 0.04, \quad M = 5{,}000 \text{ kg}, \quad H_{c} = 46{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad H_{\text{TNT}} = 4{,}680 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{0.04 \times 5{,}000 \times 46{,}000}{4{,}680}
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the multiplication in the numerator step-by-step (no skipping).**

$$
0.04 \times 5{,}000 = 200
$$

$$
200 \times 46{,}000 = 9{,}200{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by the denominator.**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{9{,}200{,}000}{4{,}680} = 1{,}965.81 \text{ kg TNT}
$$

**Step 6 — Box the answer with correct units.**

$$
\boxed{W_{\text{TNT}} \approx 1{,}966 \text{ kg TNT} \approx 1.97 \text{ tonnes TNT}}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the scaled distance Z at R = 250 m.**

$$
Z = \frac{R}{W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}} = \frac{250}{(1{,}965.81)^{1/3}}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the cube root.**

$$
(1{,}965.81)^{1/3} \approx 12.53
$$

**Step 9 — Compute Z.**

$$
Z = \frac{250}{12.53} \approx 19.95 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}
$$

**Step 10 — Interpret using Kingery-Bulmash / Mannan empirical table:**

For $Z \approx 20$ m/kg$^{1/3}$ and surface-burst TNT, the peak side-on overpressure is approximately **14 kPa** (matches the question's framing — this is the "lethal-but-mostly-structural" zone, causing 50% eardrum rupture, partial building collapse).

**Step 11 — Engineering Interpretation (mandatory 2-mark conclusion):**

The control room at 250 m is on the threshold of severe damage. Recommended precaution: relocate the control room to **Z ≤ 3.2 m/kg$^{1/3}$** (i.e., a safe distance of at least $3.2 \times (1965.81)^{1/3} = 40$ m — which is impractical). Hence the design must rely on **blast-resistant control rooms** (rated to 70 kPa) and **inerting the LPG bullets with N₂ blanket**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Probit Fatality Calculation for a Chlorine Release

**Problem Statement:**
> *In a chlor-alkali plant, 200 kg of Cl₂ escapes over 10 minutes. The wind carries the cloud to a populated area 500 m downwind where the average concentration during exposure is estimated at C = 50 ppm. Using the Cl₂ Probit constants a = −5.30, b = 0.5, n = 2.75, compute the percentage fatality.*

**Step 1 — Recall the KTU Probit equation:**

$$
\text{Pr} = a + b \ln(C^{n} \cdot t)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (C in ppm, t in minutes, KTU 2024 convention).**

$$
\text{Pr} = -5.30 + 0.5 \times \ln\left[(50)^{2.75} \times 10\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $50^{2.75}$.**

$$
\ln(50) = 3.912
$$

$$
2.75 \times 3.912 = 10.758
$$

$$
50^{2.75} = e^{10.758} = 47{,}074
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by t = 10.**

$$
50^{2.75} \times 10 = 470{,}740
$$

**Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm.**

$$
\ln(470{,}740) = 13.062
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the full Probit.**

$$
\text{Pr} = -5.30 + 0.5 \times 13.062 = -5.30 + 6.531 = 1.231
$$

**Step 7 — Convert Probit to Percentage using the standard normal table.**

Since $\text{Pr} = 1.231 < 2.67$, the fatality percentage is **< 1%**.

Using the exact formula:

$$
