At **1.6 kA**, the ratio to Iₙ = 1600 / 32 = **50 × Iₙ**, which is **far above** the magnetic threshold. The MCB will trip instantaneously (magnetic region, < 10 ms). ✓

**Step 4: Compute let-through energy to check cable protection.**

If the MCB is current-limiting and disconnects in ~5 ms:
$$I^2 t = (1600)^2 \times 0.005 = 1.28 \times 10^7 \, A^2 \cdot s$$

For a 6 mm² copper XLPE cable, $K^2 S^2 = 143^2 \times 6^2 = 7.37 \times 10^5 \, A^2 \cdot s$.

**Problem:** $I^2 t = 1.28 \times 10^7$ is **17 times greater** than cable withstand. The cable insulation will be damaged.

**Step 5: Corrective measure.**

- **Option 1:** Upgrade cable to 16 mm² → $K^2 S^2 = 5.24 \times 10^6 \, A^2 \cdot s$ → still insufficient.
- **Option 2:** Use **back-up current-limiting MCB** (I_cu = 25 kA) with very low let-through I²t.
- **Option 3:** Install **series HRC fuse** upstream of MCB to provide current-limiting action.

**Conclusion:** Reduce fault loop impedance by using shorter cable runs or larger cable size; ensure proper discrimination with upstream device.

#### **Question B (Option 2) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Set B]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an **HRC fuse**. Derive the expression for the pre-arcing time based on Joule's integral. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial motor rated **15 kW, 400 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz, η = 0.9, cos φ = 0.85** is to be protected by a Class 10 thermal overload relay and a back-up HRC fuse. Determine: (i) full-load current, (ii) overload relay setting range, and (iii) HRC fuse rating. Justify your selections. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution (a):**

**Construction of HRC fuse (as per IS 13703 / IEC 60269):**

1. **Ceramic body (Steatite / Porcelain)** — high mechanical strength, heat resistant.
2. **Silver fuse element** — pure silver ribbon with **multiple restriction notches** (typically 2–5) for predictable melting at reduced cross-section.
3. **Chemically pure quartz sand (SiO₂)** — grain size **0.2–0.4 mm**, packed under controlled density; absorbs energy and quenches arc.
4. **Brass / copper end caps** — provide electrical contact and mechanical sealing.
5. **Indicator pin (striker type)** — ejects on blowing for trip indication.

**Working principle:**

1. Normal current: element carries current within rated value without significant heating.
2. Overload / SC: current exceeds threshold; the **notch regions** (lowest cross-section) heat fastest and melt.
3. Multiple arcs form at each notch; the **metal vapour arc** is instantly surrounded by sand.
4. Sand particles **de-ionise** the arc, converting it from a conducting plasma to an insulating medium.
5. Within **< 1 ms**, the arc is extinguished — even before the natural current zero — providing **current-limiting** action.

**Derivation of pre-arcing time:**

The Joule's heat generated in the element:
$$Q = I^2 R t = K$$

For constant $R$ during the short interval, and $K$ being the energy required to raise the element from ambient to melting temperature plus latent heat of fusion:

$$t_{pre-arcing} = \frac{K}{I^2}$$

Or, expressed in A²·s (standard fuse notation):
$$I^2 t = K_{fuse}$$

The **virtual time** $t_v$ for any current $I$:
$$t_v = \frac{K_{fuse}}{I^2}$$

This relationship is plotted on **log-log axes** as a straight line, defining the **time-current characteristic (TCC)** of the fuse.

##### **Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Full-load current:**

For 3-phase motor:
$$I_{FL} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot \eta \cdot \cos \varphi} = \frac{15{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 0.9 \cdot 0.85} = 28.2 \, A$$

**(ii) Overload relay setting:**

Class 10 thermal relay is selected. Standard setting range is **0.8 to 1.0 × Iₙ** of the relay. Set at **1.0 × I_FL** = **28.2 A**.

A relay with current range **24–32 A** should be selected. For starting current 6–7 × Iₙ ≈ 170–200 A, the Class 10 relay will pass the starting transient (trip time at 6 × Iₙ = 10 s) safely.

**(iii) HRC fuse rating:**

Per IS / IEC guidelines, fuse rating = **1.2 to 1.5 × I_FL** for direct-on-line starting motors. However, since the HRC fuse serves as **back-up short-circuit protection** only, its rating may be higher (2–2.5 × I_FL) to allow overload relay to handle sustained overcurrent.

Recommended:
$$I_{fuse} = 1.5 \times 28.2 \approx 40 \, A \, \text{to} \, 63 \, A$$

**Final selection:** Use a **63 A HRC fuse** (NH00 size) as back-up SC protection, coordinated with a **32 A Class 10 overload relay** and **40 A contactor**.

**Coordination check:**
- Overload up to **~10 × Iₙ** → relay trips within seconds.
- SC above **10 × Iₙ** → HRC fuse operates within milliseconds, protecting contactor and cabling.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - In **(b) part of Q-A**, students **commonly forget** to verify the **let-through energy (I²t)** against cable withstand — a 7-mark question often loses **2–3 marks** here.
> - In **(b) part of Q-B**, students often confuse the **fuse rating** with the **relay rating**. Remember: fuse = back-up SC protection (high I²t), relay = sustained overload (low current, slow).
> - Always state **units** explicitly: 1.6 kA ≠ 1600 A in your answer unless the question's context makes the order clear.
> - Cite **IS / IEC standard numbers** whenever describing a device (e.g., **IS 8828** for MCB) — this is a KTU board marking expectation worth 1 mark each time.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Means of Cutting of Power, Overload & SC Protection**

- **Two families of protection devices:** **Fuses (sacrificial, one-time)** and **Circuit Breakers (re-settable, re-closable)**.
- **Fuse key formula:** $I^2 t = K$ — energy let-through depends on current squared times duration.
- **HRC fuse breaking capacity:** Up to **120 kA** — the industry workhorse.
- **Fusing factor:** Always **> 1**; lower for semiconductor fuses (**≈ 1.1**).
- **MCB trip curves:** B (3–5×), C (5–10×), D (10–20×) — choose by load inrush.
- **MCCB range:** 63 A to **1600 A**; current range 25 kA–100 kA.
- **Trip Class of Overload Relay:** Class 5, 10, 20, 30 — pick by motor starting inertia.
- **PSCC calculation:** $I_{SC} = \dfrac{U_0}{Z_s}$ — must be **less than I_cu** of CB.
- **Adiabatic equation:** $I^2 t \leq K^2 S^2$ — protects cable insulation.
- **Discrimination ratio:** ≥ **1.6** for partial, ≥ **2.0** for full selectivity.
- **RCCB sensitivity:** **30 mA** for life protection, **300 mA** for fire prevention.
- **Arc-quenching medium:** Air (ACB), Oil (OCB), Vacuum (VCB), SF₆ gas, CO₂, H₂O.
- **Standards to remember:** IS 8828 (MCB), IS 12640 (RCCB), IS 13947 (MCCB), IEC 60269 (Fuses), IEC 60898, IEC 60947-2.
- **Coordination principle:** The **device closest to the fault** trips first; upstream devices stay intact.
- **Indian Electricity Rules 1956 Rule 50:** Every motor above 0.375 kW must have a **switch and fuse/CB** for isolation.
- **Let-through I²t** of current-limiting MCB is typically **10%–20%** of prospective value.
- **Critical difference:** Overload = thermal device, slow; SC = magnetic/electromagnetic device, instantaneous.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET637 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA)** is a systematic, structured, and iterative engineering methodology used in process industries, manufacturing plants, and occupational environments to **identify potential hazards**, **evaluate the associated risks**, and **implement appropriate control measures** to reduce risk to a tolerable or acceptable level as defined by organizational, regulatory, and statutory standards (e.g., OSHA, IS 3786, OISD, OSHA 29 CFR 1910).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** HIRA is the cornerstone of Process Safety Management (PSM) and is a mandatory prerequisite for any Safety Management System (SMS) audit under the KTU Industrial Safety Engineering curriculum.

**Hazard:** An intrinsic physical, chemical, biological, ergonomic, or psychosocial condition with the potential to cause harm to people, property, environment, or business continuity.

**Risk:** A quantitative or qualitative measure of the probability of a hazardous event occurring combined with the severity of its consequence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Road Crossing Model":**
>
> Imagine a pedestrian crossing a busy highway.
> - The **moving trucks** = *Hazard* (source of potential harm).
> - The *probability* that a truck hits the pedestrian and the *severity* of injury if it does = *Risk*.
> - Installing a traffic signal, zebra crossing, or pedestrian foot-over-bridge = *Risk Control Measures*.
>
> **Hazard Identification** is recognizing that trucks exist on the road. **Risk Assessment** is estimating how likely an accident is and how bad it would be. **Risk Control** is building safety infrastructure.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

The following standard metrics are used industry-wide and are **bold-marked** as per KTU valuation expectations:

- **Acceptable Risk (Tolerable Risk):** Typically defined as **10⁻⁶ fatality probability per person per year** for workers and **10⁻⁷** for the general public.
- **FAR (Fatal Accident Rate):** Number of fatalities per **10⁸ person-hours** of exposure.
- **Fatality Risk per Annum:** Probability of death of an individual per year.
- **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable): A risk reduction principle stating that residual risk must be reduced unless the cost (in money, time, or effort) is grossly disproportionate to the safety benefit gained.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module 2 specifically tests your understanding of the *difference between hazard and risk*, the *risk matrix concept*, and the *hierarchy of controls*. Examiners frequently frame 2-mark and 3-mark questions around these distinctions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Concepts: Hazard vs. Risk vs. Consequence

- **Hazard:** The latent capability to cause harm (e.g., a pressure vessel containing LPG under high pressure).
- **Risk:** The combination of the **likelihood** of the hazard causing an incident and the **severity (consequence)** of the harm.
- **Consequence:** The actual outcome if the hazardous event occurs (e.g., fire, explosion, toxic release).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why:** Engineers and safety managers must distinguish hazards from risks because eliminating all hazards is impossible in any industrial process — but reducing risk to acceptable levels is achievable and is the practical engineering objective.

## 2.2 The HIRA Process — Structured Logic Flow

1. **Step 1 — System Definition:** Define the scope, boundaries, interfaces, and operational context of the system being assessed.
2. **Step 2 — Hazard Identification:** Apply techniques (HAZOP, FMEA, Checklist, What-If, FTA, ETA) to systematically recognize hazards.
3. **Step 3 — Risk Estimation:** Assign **likelihood (L)** and **severity (S)** scores using a standardized risk matrix.
4. **Step 4 — Risk Evaluation:** Compare estimated risk against acceptance/tolerance criteria.
5. **Step 5 — Risk Control:** Apply the **Hierarchy of Controls** to reduce risk.
6. **Step 6 — Documentation & Review:** Maintain records and periodically revalidate the assessment.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fundamental Risk Equation | $R = f(L, S)$ where $R$ = Risk, $L$ = Likelihood, $S$ = Severity | Dimensionless scoring |
| 2 | Quantitative Risk | $R = P \times C$ | $P$ = Probability (dimensionless, 0 to 1); $C$ = Consequence magnitude (in damage units, fatalities, or monetary loss) |
| 3 | Risk Score (Matrix Method) | $R_{score} = L \times S$ | Score range: **1 to 25** (typical 5×5 matrix) |
| 4 | Residual Risk | $R_{residual} = R_{inherent} - R_{controlled}$ | $R_{residual} \geq R_{acceptable}$ |
| 5 | Fatal Accident Rate (FAR) | $FAR = \dfrac{N_f \times 10^{8}}{T_h}$ | $N_f$ = number of fatalities; $T_h$ = total exposure hours |
| 6 | Individual Risk per Annum | $IR = \sum_{i} f_i \times p_i$ | Frequency $f_i$ in events/year; $p_i$ = probability of fatality |
| 7 | FMEA Risk Priority Number | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | $S$ = Severity; $O$ = Occurrence; $D$ = Detection; Range: 1 to 1000 |
| 8 | ALARP Grossly Disproportionate Test | $\dfrac{Benefit}{Cost} \geq 10$ (industry heuristic) | Cost must not be 10× higher than safety benefit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** When a numerical risk matrix question is asked, examiners expect the student to show the **R = L × S** multiplication explicitly. Do **not** skip to the qualitative interpretation without showing the numerical step.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The HIRA framework is used in:
- **Oil & Gas Industry:** HAZOP studies for refinery units (mandatory under OISD-150).
- **Chemical Process Plants:** Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) for siting of LNG terminals.
- **Manufacturing:** Machine safety risk assessments per ISO 12100.
- **Construction:** Permit-to-work systems and Job Safety Analysis (JSA).
- **Aerospace:** Failure Modes, Effects, and Criticality Analysis (FMECA).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Risk Score via the 5×5 Risk Matrix

A **Risk Matrix** is the most frequently tested concept under KTU Module 2. The derivation and evaluation steps are shown exhaustively below.

### Step 1 — Define the Likelihood Scale

A standard 5-level likelihood scale is adopted industry-wide.

$$
L_{score} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}
$$

$$
L_{score} =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{Rare (occurs once in 30+ years)} \\
2 & \text{Unlikely (occurs once in 10–30 years)} \\
3 & \text{Possible (occurs once in 1–10 years)} \\
4 & \text{Likely (occurs multiple times per year)} \\
5 & \text{Almost Certain (occurs frequently)}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Severity Scale

$$
S_{score} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}
$$

$$
S_{score} =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{Negligible (no injury, minor discomfort)} \\
2 & \text{Minor (first aid injury, small financial loss)} \\
3 & \text{Moderate (medical treatment, lost-time injury)} \\
4 & \text{Major (serious injury, fatality possible)} \\
5 & \text{Catastrophic (multiple fatalities, plant destruction)}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Risk Score

$$
R_{score} = L_{score} \times S_{score}
$$

### Step 4 — Classify the Risk Level

$$
\text{Risk Level} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Low} & \text{if } 1 \leq R_{score} \leq 4 \\
\text{Medium} & \text{if } 5 \leq R_{score} \leq 12 \\
\text{High} & \text{if } 13 \leq R_{score} \leq 19 \\
\text{Extreme} & \text{if } 20 \leq R_{score} \leq 25
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The risk score is the product of two normalized indices. A "Low" risk implies both severity and likelihood are minimal. A "High" or "Extreme" risk demands immediate corrective action — this is the principle examiners test.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

**Problem:** A chemical reactor has been identified to have a likelihood rating of **4** and severity rating of **5**. Calculate the risk score and classify the risk level.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs from the problem statement.**

$$
L_{score} = 4 \quad ; \quad S_{score} = 5
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the risk equation.**

$$
R_{score} = L_{score} \times S_{score} = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the risk classification boundaries.**

$$
R_{score} = 20 \quad \text{and} \quad 20 \leq 20 \leq 25
$$

**Step 4 — Classify the risk level.**

$$
\text{Since } 20 \leq R_{score} \leq 25 \implies \text{Risk Level} = \textbf{Extreme}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** This risk is **EXTREME**. Operations must be halted immediately, and engineering controls (e.g., pressure relief valves, interlocks) along with administrative controls must be deployed before restart. The KTU examiner expects this conclusion in a 14-mark answer.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Hazard Identification Technique

| Technique | Best Suited For | Data Required | Output | Quantitative? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Checklist Analysis** | Routine, well-understood systems | Historical accident data, regulatory standards | Pass/Fail compliance gaps | No |
| **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** | Early design / concept stage | Plant layout, materials inventory | Hazard list with broad consequences | No |
| **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** | Continuous chemical processes | P&ID, process flow diagrams | Deviations and causes | Semi-quantitative |
| **FMEA / FMECA** | Mechanical / electrical systems | Component failure rate data | Risk Priority Number (RPN) | Quantitative |
| **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** | Complex accident pathways | Boolean logic, failure probabilities | Top event probability | Quantitative |
| **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** | Consequence modeling | Initiating event frequency | Outcome frequency | Quantitative |
| **What-If Analysis** | Brainstorming sessions | Expert experience | Hypothetical scenarios | Qualitative |
| **Bow-Tie Analysis** | Major hazard management | Barriers, controls inventory | Cause-consequence linkage | Semi-quantitative |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **FMEA** with **FTA**. Remember — *FMEA is a bottom-up, inductive* approach (component → system failure), while *FTA is a top-down, deductive* approach (system failure → root causes).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The HIRA Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: System Definition] --> B[Step 2: Hazard Identification]
    B --> C[Step 3: Risk Estimation via Matrix]
    C --> D[Step 4: Risk Evaluation vs Acceptance Criteria]
    D --> E{Risk Acceptable?}
    E -- No --> F[Step 5: Apply Hierarchy of Controls]
    F --> G[Step 6: Documentation and Review]
    G --> B
    E -- Yes --> H[Approval and Operations]
    H --> I[Periodic Revalidation]
    I --> B
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Controls (Engineering Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Identify Hazard and Estimate Risk] --> R1[Step 1: Elimination]
    R1 --> R2[Step 2: Substitution]
    R2 --> R3[Step 3: Engineering Controls]
    R3 --> R4[Step 4: Administrative Controls]
    R4 --> R5[Step 5: PPE - Personal Protective Equipment]
    R5 --> R6[Residual Risk Reassessment]
    R6 --> R7{Risk Acceptable?}
    R7 -- No --> R1
    R7 -- Yes --> R8[Document and Approve]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** The Hierarchy of Controls is a downward cascade of decreasing effectiveness. **Elimination** is the most effective (removes the hazard entirely), while **PPE** is the least effective (depends on human behavior). KTU examiners may ask you to rank these.

## 4.3 Bow-Tie Risk Model Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[TREATMENT: Consequence Reduction] --> BT((Bow-Tie Model))
    P[PREVENTION: Threat Reduction] --> BT
    BT --> B1[Barrier 1: Engineering]
    BT --> B2[Barrier 2: Procedural]
    BT --> B3[Barrier 3: Detection and Mitigation]
    BT --> KE[Top Event: Loss of Containment]
    KE --> C1[Consequence A: Fire]
    KE --> C2[Consequence B: Explosion]
    KE --> C3[Consequence C: Toxic Release]
```

## 4.4 HAZOP Study Node Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Process Section Identified] --> n[Define HAZOP Node]
    n --> p[Apply Guide Words: NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, OTHER THAN]
    p --> dev[Identify Deviations]
    dev --> cause[Determine Causes]
    cause --> cons[Determine Consequences]
    cons --> safe[Identify Safeguards]
    safe --> rec[Generate Recommendations]
    rec --> n
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Bow-Tie model is the visual language of modern Safety Management Systems. The **left side** represents prevention barriers (stopping the event from occurring), and the **right side** represents mitigation barriers (reducing consequences once the top event has occurred).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Module 2 - KTU Pattern)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
Differentiate between **Hazard** and **Risk** with one industrial example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hazard | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Intrinsic property to cause harm | Combination of probability and consequence |
| **Nature** | Qualitative (source) | Quantitative + Qualitative (measure) |
| **Example** | Flammable solvent stored in plant | Probability of fire × severity of fire damage |

**Scoring Allocation (as per KTU valuation key):**  
- Correct definitions (2 marks)  
- Industrial examples (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 (Module 2 - KTU Pattern)

**[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Sample Paper]**  
List any **three Hazard Identification techniques** and state one limitation of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **HAZOP** — Requires highly experienced multidisciplinary team; cannot be applied effectively without detailed P&ID.
2. **FMEA** — Becomes cumbersome for very large systems with thousands of components; relies on accurate failure rate data.
3. **What-If Analysis** — Highly dependent on facilitator expertise; may miss hazards not anticipated by the team.

**Scoring Allocation:**  
- Three techniques named (1.5 marks)  
- One limitation each (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Past Paper Pattern]**

(a) **Explain in detail the Risk Matrix Method for risk assessment.** Construct a **5×5 risk matrix** and classify the risks into Low, Medium, High, and Extreme categories. **(7 marks)**

(b) **Explain the Hierarchy of Controls with a neat block diagram.** Why is PPE considered the *last* resort in the hierarchy? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

The **Risk Matrix Method** is a semi-quantitative risk assessment tool that combines **Likelihood (L)** and **Severity (S)** on a numerical scale.

**Step 1 — Define the Likelihood Scale (L):** Values from 1 (Rare) to 5 (Almost Certain).

**Step 2 — Define the Severity Scale (S):** Values from 1 (Negligible) to 5 (Catastrophic).

**Step 3 — Compute Risk Score:**

$$
R_{score} = L \times S \quad ; \quad R_{score} \in [1, 25]
$$

**Step 4 — 5×5 Risk Matrix Table (using \vert for separators to preserve markdown):**

$$
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c}
\textbf{L \textbackslash{} S} & \textbf{1 Negligible} & \textbf{2 Minor} & \textbf{3 Moderate} & \textbf{4 Major} & \textbf{5 Catastrophic} \\
\hline
\textbf{5 Almost Certain} & 5M & 10M & 15H & 20E & 25E \\
\textbf{4 Likely} & 4L & 8M & 12M & 16H & 20E \\
\textbf{3 Possible} & 3L & 6M & 9M & 12M & 15H \\
\textbf{2 Unlikely} & 2L & 4L & 6M & 8M & 10M \\
\textbf{1 Rare} & 1L & 2L & 3L & 4L & 5M \\
\end{array}
$$

*(Legend: L = Low, M = Medium, H = High, E = Extreme)*

**Step 5 — Classification Boundaries:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Low Risk:} &\quad 1 \leq R_{score} \leq 4 \\
\text{Medium Risk:} &\quad 5 \leq R_{score} \leq 12 \\
\text{High Risk:} &\quad 13 \leq R_{score} \leq 19 \\
\text{Extreme Risk:} &\quad 20 \leq R_{score} \leq 25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The matrix offers a fast, visual, and consensus-driven method for risk prioritization. However, its main drawback is **subjectivity** in assigning L and S scores.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

The **Hierarchy of Controls** is a tiered framework that prioritizes control measures based on their effectiveness in reducing risk.

**Ranked from Most Effective to Least Effective:**

1. **Elimination** — Physically remove the hazard (e.g., cease using a toxic chemical).
2. **Substitution** — Replace with a less hazardous alternative.
3. **Engineering Controls** — Isolate workers from the hazard (e.g., machine guarding, ventilation).
4. **Administrative Controls** — Change work procedures (e.g., training, signage, rotation).
5. **PPE** — Personal protective equipment (e.g., helmets, respirators, gloves).

**Why PPE is the Last Resort:**

PPE is considered the **least effective** control because:

- It protects only the **individual** who wears it and does not eliminate the source.
- It depends heavily on **human compliance** and correct usage.
- It has limited effectiveness in severe hazard scenarios.
- It can be damaged, worn out, or improperly fitted.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students *only list* the hierarchy but fail to *justify the order*. Marks are lost when the rationale (effectiveness vs. dependence on human behavior) is missing. Always include the reasoning.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Past Paper Pattern]**

(a) **Explain the HAZOP (Hazard and Operability) study methodology.** Describe the **guide words** used and the procedure to conduct a HAZOP study. **(7 marks)**

(b) **Explain FMEA with reference to the calculation of the Risk Priority Number (RPN).** An automotive brake system has $S = 8$, $O = 5$, $D = 4$. Calculate the RPN and recommend control actions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** is a structured, team-based hazard identification technique originally developed by ICI (Imperial Chemical Industries) in the 1960s. It is the most widely used technique in the chemical and process industries.

**Key Guide Words and their Meanings:**

| Guide Word | Meaning | Example Deviation |
|---|---|---|
| **NO / NOT** | Complete negation of design intent | No flow in a pipeline |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase | Higher pressure than design |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | Lower temperature than design |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite | Reverse flow direction |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution | Wrong material in vessel |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional activity | Extra phase present |

**HAZOP Procedure:**

1. **Step 1:** Define the scope, objectives, and boundaries of the study.
2. **Step 2:** Assemble a multidisciplinary team (process engineer, safety engineer, operations, instrumentation).
3. **Step 3:** Divide the plant into **nodes** (sections).
4. **Step 4:** For each node, apply each **guide word** to each **process parameter** (flow, pressure, temperature, level, composition).
5. **Step 5:** Identify deviations, determine **causes** and **consequences**.
6. **Step 6:** Identify existing **safeguards** and recommend additional ones if inadequate.
7. **Step 7:** Document findings in a HAZOP worksheet and assign action responsibilities.

**Conclusion:** HAZOP is exhaustive, systematic, and highly effective for continuous processes involving complex chemistry, fluid flow, and instrumentation.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** is a bottom-up, inductive hazard identification technique that analyzes each component of a system for potential failure modes.

**Risk Priority Number (RPN) Formula:**

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:  
- $S$ = Severity of failure consequence (1 to 10)  
- $O$ = Occurrence rating (likelihood of failure, 1 to 10)  
- $D$ = Detection rating (difficulty of detecting the failure, 1 to 10)

**Given Data:**

$$
S = 8 \quad ; \quad O = 5 \quad ; \quad D = 4
$$

**Calculation:**

$$
RPN = 8 \times 5 \times 4 = 160
$$

**Interpretation:**

$$
RPN = 160 \quad \text{(on a scale of 1 to 1000)}
$$

**Risk Classification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Low:} &\quad RPN < 50 \\
\text{Moderate:} &\quad 50 \leq RPN \leq 150 \\
\text{High:} &\quad 150 < RPN \leq 300 \\
\text{Critical:} &\quad RPN > 300
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $RPN = 160 > 150$, this failure mode is classified as **High** priority.

**Recommended Control Actions:**

1. **Engineering Controls:** Install redundant brake sensors with anti-lock logic.
2. **Detection Enhancement:** Implement real-time brake pad wear monitoring sensors.
3. **Administrative Controls:** Mandate 6-monthly brake system inspections by certified mechanics.
4. **PPE Mitigation:** Driver-side airbag as the last line of defense.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake is forgetting the **D (Detection)** parameter in the RPN formula. Students often compute $RPN = S \times O$ only — losing up to **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question. Always write out the full $S \times O \times D$ form.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: HIRA**

- **Hazard ≠ Risk.** Hazard is the latent source of harm; Risk = Probability × Consequence.
- The **Risk Matrix Method** uses a 5×5 table; $R = L \times S$ with scores from 1 to 25.
- Risk levels: **Low (1–4), Medium (5–12), High (13–19), Extreme (20–25).**
- **Hierarchy of Controls** order: *Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE*.
- **PPE is the LAST resort** because it depends on human compliance.
- **HAZOP** uses **guide words** (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, OTHER THAN, AS WELL AS, PART OF) applied to process parameters.
- **FMEA** uses $RPN = S \times O \times D$; **FTA** is top-down deductive; **ETA** is forward consequence modeling.
- **ALARP** principle: Reduce risk unless the cost is grossly disproportionate (factor of ~10).
- **Acceptable individual risk:** typically **10⁻⁶ per year** for workers, **10⁻⁷ per year** for the public.
- **FAR (Fatal Accident Rate):** Number of fatalities per $10^8$ person-hours of exposure.
- The **Bow-Tie Model** visually links prevention barriers (left) and mitigation barriers (right) to a top event.
- Always document HIRA outputs and ensure **periodic revalidation** (typically every 3–5 years or after process modifications).
- For KTU 14-mark answers, always include a **diagram (block diagram or flowchart)** and a **concluding engineering recommendation**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventory Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET637 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inventory Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Inventory Analysis** in Industrial Safety Engineering is the systematic process of *identifying, classifying, quantifying, and documenting* all hazardous materials present within a process plant, storage facility, or industrial establishment, including their physical state, quantity, location, and the conditions under which they are stored. It forms the foundational step (often Stage 1) of consequence analysis under the **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** framework and is a mandatory requirement under regulatory codes such as **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (Process Safety Management)** and **EPA 40 CFR Part 68 (Risk Management Programs)**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, inventory analysis provides the quantitative input data for subsequent hazard assessment modules — specifically **fire/explosion modeling, toxic release dispersion, and damage estimation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Framework):**
> Inventory analysis refers to the assessment of the type, quantity, and physical conditions of hazardous materials stored, handled, or processed in an industrial facility, to determine the *Maximum Credible Inventory* available for release during an accidental scenario.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the safety officer of a large chemical warehouse. Before you can design a fire-protection system, plan evacuation routes, or estimate the size of a potential disaster, you must first **open every register and write down exactly what is stored, where it is stored, how much is stored, and how dangerous it is.** That is *inventory analysis*.

Think of it like a doctor's diagnosis:
- **Symptom** = A possible accident scenario
- **Diagnosis** = Inventory Analysis (what chemicals exist, in what quantity, with what properties)
- **Prescription** = Mitigation, fire-protection, and emergency response design

Without an accurate diagnosis (inventory), no treatment (safety measure) can be prescribed correctly.

## 1.3 Key Inventory Categories in Industry

| Category | Typical Examples | Primary Hazard |
|----------|-----------------|----------------|
| Raw Materials | Propylene, Ammonia, Chlorine | Flammable / Toxic |
| Intermediates | Vinyl Chloride, Acrylonitrile | Reactive / Toxic |
| Finished Products | Gasoline, LPG, LPG cylinders | Flammable |
| By-products / Wastes | Hydrogen sulfide, Acid sludges | Toxic / Corrosive |
| Utilities | Hydrogen (fuel), Diesel (generator) | Flammable |

## 1.4 Standard Thresholds & Regulatory Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Regulatory Threshold Limits (OSHA PSM List & EPA RMP):**
> - **Threshold Quantity (TQ)** for flammable liquids: **10,000 lbs (4,535 kg)**
> - **TQ** for flammable gases: **10,000 lbs**
> - **TQ** for Chlorine: **1,500 lbs (680 kg)**
> - **TQ** for Anhydrous Ammonia: **10,000 lbs**
> - **TQ** for Hydrogen Fluoride: **1,000 lbs (454 kg)**

These are the *minimum* quantities above which a facility falls under mandatory PSM/RMP regulations, and therefore **mandatory inventory analysis** applies.

## 1.5 Importance of Inventory Analysis

1. **Hazard Identification** — Determines if a facility contains *sufficient* hazardous material to trigger PSM coverage.
2. **Worst-Case Scenario Definition** — Provides the *Maximum Credible Release (MCR)* for consequence analysis.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** — Required for permit issuance (e.g., Factories Act, Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules — MSIHC, India).
4. **Insurance & Liability** — Used for risk-based premium calculation.
5. **Emergency Planning** — Defines the Off-Site Consequence Analysis (OCAP) radius.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Inventory Analysis

The inventory analysis follows a strict structured procedure, which can be summarized in six logical steps:

1. **Process Boundary Definition** — Draw the *battery limits* of the unit under study (e.g., one reactor, one storage tank farm, one pipe rack).
2. **Material Identification** — List *every* chemical species present, using Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS/SDS).
3. **State Classification** — Categorize each material by:
   - **Phase:** Solid / Liquid / Gas / Vapor
   - **Temperature:** Ambient / Heated / Cryogenic / Refrigerated
   - **Pressure:** Atmospheric / Pressurized / Vacuum
4. **Quantity Determination** — Measure or estimate:
   - Maximum design inventory
   - Operating inventory
   - Minimum inventory
5. **Hazard Classification** — Classify under:
   - **Flammability** (Flash Point, LEL/UEL, Auto-Ignition Temperature)
   - **Toxicity** (TLV, IDLH, LC₅₀)
   - **Reactivity** (NFPA Reactivity Rating 0–4)
   - **Corrosivity / Radioactivity**
6. **Threshold Comparison** — Compare actual inventory against **Threshold Quantities (TQ)** from regulatory tables to identify *regulated substances*.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas form the high-yield numerical toolkit for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 questions.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Mass Inventory** | $M_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i$ | $M_i$ = mass of material $i$ (kg) | Total hazardous inventory |
| 2 | **Vapor Mass in Pressurized Liquid** | $m_v = f_v \cdot M_{\text{liquid}}$ | $f_v$ = vapor mass fraction, dimensionless | Flash fraction from sudden release |
| 3 | **TNT Equivalence — Explosion Energy** | $W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{\text{TNT}}}$ | $\eta$ = yield factor (1–10% typical), $H_c$ = heat of combustion (kJ/kg), $H_{\text{TNT}} = 4686$ **kJ/kg** | Condensed phase explosion |
| 4 | **Fireball Radius (TNT Method)** | $R_{\text{fb}} = 0.39 \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}$ | $W_{\text{TNT}}$ in kg, $R$ in meters | BLEVE fireball sizing |
| 5 | **Fireball Duration** | $t_{\text{fb}} = 0.49 \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/6}$ | $t$ in seconds | Fireball persistence |
| 6 | **Pasquill–Gifford Concentration (Downwind)** | $C(x,y,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z \cdot u}$ | $Q$ = release rate (g/s), $u$ = wind speed (m/s), $\sigma$ = dispersion coefficients | Ground-level toxic concentration |
| 7 | **Probit Equation — Lethal Dose** | $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$ | $C$ = concentration (ppm), $t$ = exposure (min) | Toxic fatality probability |
| 8 | **Equivalent Hydrocarbon Mass (EHM)** | $m_{\text{eq}} = M \cdot \dfrac{H_c}{H_{\text{ref}}}$ | $H_{\text{ref}}$ = reference HC heat (~46 MJ/kg) | Cross-fuel explosion scaling |
| 9 | **Maximum Credible Release (MCR)** | $\text{MCR} = f(\text{Inventory}, \text{Failure Mode}, \text{Isolation Time})$ | Empirical | Risk assessment input |
| 10 | **Threshold Quantity Sum Check** | $\sum_{i} \dfrac{q_i}{TQ_i} \ge 1$ → regulated | $q_i$ = actual quantity, $TQ_i$ = threshold | Facility classification |

## 2.3 Why This System Matters — Real-World Utility

- **Refineries and Petrochemical Plants** use inventory analysis to size flare systems, dikes, and foam suppression networks.
- **LNG Terminals** require inventory analysis to model vapor cloud dispersion under marine boundary-layer conditions.
- **Pharmaceutical Plants** assess solvent inventories against **NFPA 30 / NFPA 45** to determine maximum allowable quantity (MAQ) per control area.
- **Ammonia Storage Facilities** must compute worst-case toxic endpoint distances for the EPA's *Look-Up Table* under **RMP*Comp**.

## 2.4 Hazard Classification — NFPA Diamond Mapping

The **NFPA 704 Hazard Diamond** standardizes inventory hazard communication:

- **Blue (Left)** — Health Hazard (0–4)
- **Red (Top)** — Flammability (0–4)
- **Yellow (Right)** — Reactivity/Instability (0–4)
- **White (Bottom)** — Special Hazards (OX = Oxidizer, W = Water-reactive, SA = Simple Asphyxiant)

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024:** Any substance scoring $\ge 3$ in any category is treated as a *high-priority inventory item* in safety audits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Inventory Aggregation

**Problem Statement:** A process unit contains the following materials. Compute total mass and identify the dominant hazard.

- Propylene (flammable gas): **5,000 kg**
- Liquid Ammonia (toxic): **2,000 kg**
- Diesel (flammable liquid): **8,000 kg**
- Sulfuric Acid (corrosive): **500 kg**

**Solution:**

$$
M_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i
$$

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 5000 + 2000 + 8000 + 500
$$

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 15{,}500 \text{ kg}
$$

Now check threshold compliance for each substance:

- Propylene: $5{,}000 / 10{,}000 = 0.50$ (50% of TQ)
- Ammonia: $2{,}000 / 10{,}000 = 0.20$ (20% of TQ)
- Diesel: $8{,}000 / 10{,}000 = 0.80$ (80% of TQ)

$$
\sum \dfrac{q_i}{TQ_i} = 0.50 + 0.20 + 0.80 = 1.50
$$

Since the sum $\ge 1$, the facility **is regulated** under PSM/RMP even though individual fractions are below 1.0. This is the **synergistic threshold rule**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — TNT Equivalent Explosion Mass

**Problem:** A storage sphere contains **20,000 kg** of propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$). The sphere ruptures catastrophically and the vapor cloud detonates. Estimate the TNT-equivalent mass. Given:
- Heat of combustion of propane, $H_c = 46,000$ kJ/kg
- Empirical yield factor, $\eta = 0.05$ (5%)
- TNT detonation energy, $H_{\text{TNT}} = 4686$ kJ/kg

**Step 1 — Write the Governing Equation:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{\text{TNT}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{0.05 \cdot 20{,}000 \cdot 46{,}000}{4686}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator Calculation:**

$$
0.05 \cdot 20{,}000 = 1000
$$

$$
1000 \cdot 46{,}000 = 4.6 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Division:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{4.6 \times 10^7}{4686}
$$

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = 9816.5 \text{ kg of TNT equivalent}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

A 20-ton propane sphere releases energy equivalent to **≈ 9.8 tonnes of TNT** — sufficient to cause catastrophic structural damage at distances of several hundred meters.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fireball Radius and Duration

Using the TNT-equivalent mass $W_{\text{TNT}} = 9816.5$ kg from above:

**Step 1 — Fireball Radius:**

$$
R_{\text{fb}} = 0.39 \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}
$$

$$
W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3} = 9816.5^{1/3}
$$

$$
9816.5^{1/3} \approx 21.40
$$

$$
R_{\text{fb}} = 0.39 \cdot 21.40 = 8.35 \text{ meters}
$$

**Step 2 — Fireball Duration:**

$$
t_{\text{fb}} = 0.49 \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/6}
$$

$$
W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/6} = 9816.5^{1/6}
$$

$$
9816.5^{1/6} \approx 4.62
$$

$$
t_{\text{fb}} = 0.49 \cdot 4.62 = 2.26 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Conclusion:**

The BLEVE fireball will be approximately **8.35 m radius** and last about **2.3 seconds**. Any personnel within this radius will be exposed to lethal thermal radiation ($> 12.5$ kW/m²).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Vapor Cloud Mass (Flash Fraction Method)

**Given:** A pressurized vessel contains **10,000 kg** of liquid propane at 21 °C. The vessel ruptures. Calculate the mass of vapor instantaneously formed (flash fraction).

**Step 1 — Use the Flash Fraction Formula:**

$$
f_v = 1 - \exp\left[-\dfrac{C_p \cdot (T_s - T_b)}{H_v}\right]
$$

Where:
- $C_p$ = Specific heat of liquid ≈ 2.25 kJ/(kg·K)
- $T_s$ = Storage temperature = 294 K
- $T_b$ = Boiling point of propane = 231 K
- $H_v$ = Latent heat of vaporization = 425 kJ/kg

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
f_v = 1 - \exp\left[-\dfrac{2.25 \cdot (294 - 231)}{425}\right]
$$

$$
f_v = 1 - \exp\left[-\dfrac{2.25 \cdot 63}{425}\right]
$$

$$
f_v = 1 - \exp\left[-\dfrac{141.75}{425}\right]
$$

$$
f_v = 1 - \exp(-0.3335)
$$

$$
f_v = 1 - 0.7165 = 0.2835 \approx 28.35\%
$$

**Step 3 — Vapor Mass:**

$$
m_v = f_v \cdot M = 0.2835 \cdot 10{,}000 = 2835 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Liquid Mass Remaining (Aerosol/Droplets):**

$$
m_{\text{aerosol}} = 10{,}000 - 2835 = 7165 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Conclusion:**

About **2.8 tonnes** instantaneously vaporize and form a flammable cloud, while 7.2 tonnes form a cold liquid pool that may re-vaporize or ignite as a *pool fire*.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Threshold Sum Compliance Check

**Problem:** A facility stores:
- 4,000 kg chlorine ($TQ = 680$ kg)
- 800 kg hydrogen fluoride ($TQ = 454$ kg)
- 12,000 kg LPG ($TQ = 4535$ kg)

Determine if the facility falls under RMP regulation.

**Step 1 — Compute Each Fraction:**

$$
\text{Cl}_2: \dfrac{4000}{680} = 5.88
$$

$$
\text{HF}: \dfrac{800}{454} = 1.76
$$

$$
\text{LPG}: \dfrac{12000}{4535} = 2.65
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the Fractions:**

$$
\sum \dfrac{q_i}{TQ_i} = 5.88 + 1.76 + 2.65 = 10.29
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

Since $10.29 \gg 1.0$, the facility is **heavily regulated** under EPA RMP. Each individual fraction already exceeds 1.0, meaning even a single-substance analysis would mandate RMP coverage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inventory Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the systematic flow of inventory analysis as practiced in process safety engineering:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Facility Boundary Definition] --> B[Process Unit Segmentation]
    B --> C[Material Identification via SDS or MSDS]
    C --> D[State Classification: Solid, Liquid, Gas, Vapor]
    D --> E[Quantity Measurement: Maximum, Operating, Minimum]
    E --> F[Hazard Classification: Flammable, Toxic, Reactive, Corrosive]
    F --> G[Threshold Quantity Comparison Against OSHA PSM and EPA RMP]
    G --> H{Any Fraction Exceeds TQ or Sum Equals 1?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Regulated Facility - Apply PSM and RMP Rules]
    H -- No --> J[Non-Regulated - Maintain Internal Inventory Register]
    I --> K[Determine Maximum Credible Release MCR]
    K --> L[Select Worst-Case Release Scenarios]
    L --> M[Input to Consequence Analysis - Fire, Explosion, Dispersion]
    J --> M
    M --> N[Finalize Off-Site Consequence Analysis OCAP]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Inventory Hierarchy Inside a Process Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P[Plant Boundary]
        A1[Raw Material Storage Tanks]
        A2[Intermediate Storage Vessels]
        A3[Reactor and Process Units]
        A4[Piping and Manifold Systems]
        A5[Finished Product Storage]
        A6[Waste and By-product Handling]
    end
    A1 --> B[Inventory Database]
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    A4 --> B
    A5 --> B
    A6 --> B
    B --> C[Threshold Screening Matrix]
    C --> D[PSM Coverage Decision]
    C --> E[RMP Coverage Decision]
    C --> F[Hazard Communication per NFPA 704]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Material Classification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify Material] --> Q1{Is Material Flammable?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> ClassA[Flammable Category: Compute Heat of Combustion, LEL/UEL, Flash Point]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is Material Toxic?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> ClassB[Toxic Category: Compute TLV, IDLH, LC50]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is Material Reactive?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ClassC[Reactive Category: Check NFPA Reactivity Rating]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is Material Corrosive or Radioactive?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> ClassD[Special Hazard Category]
    Q4 -- No --> ClassE[Non-Hazardous - Standard Storage]
    ClassA --> Reg[Compare Against TQ - Apply Regulatory Threshold]
    ClassB --> Reg
    ClassC --> Reg
    ClassD --> Reg
    ClassE --> End[Inventory Recorded Without Special Controls]
    Reg --> End2[Regulatory Compliance Triggered]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — From Inventory to Consequence Modeling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I[Inventory Analysis Output] --> J[Identify Failure Scenarios: Pipe Rupture, Tank Rupture, Valve Failure]
    J --> K[Estimate Release Rate and Mass]
    K --> L1[Fire Modeling: Pool Fire, Jet Fire, Fireball]
    K --> L2[Explosion Modeling: VCE, BLEVE, Confined Explosion]
    K --> L3[Dispersion Modeling: Neutral, Heavy Gas, Passive Plume]
    L1 --> M[Damage Estimation: Thermal Radiation]
    L2 --> M[Damage Estimation: Overpressure]
    L3 --> M[Damage Estimation: Toxic Concentration]
    M --> N[Risk Characterization and Emergency Planning]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define **Inventory Analysis** in Industrial Safety Engineering. List any **four** key data fields that must be captured for every material in an inventory register.

**Model Answer:**

Inventory Analysis is the systematic identification, classification, and quantification of all hazardous materials present in a process plant, storage facility, or industrial establishment. It is the *first step* in consequence analysis and is mandatory under OSHA PSM and EPA RMP regulations.

Four key data fields are:
1. **Material Name and CAS Number** — Unique chemical identifier
2. **Quantity** — Maximum, operating, and minimum inventory (in kg or tonnes)
3. **Physical State** — Solid, liquid, gas, or vapor
4. **Storage Conditions** — Temperature (K), Pressure (kPa), and container type

*(Additional acceptable fields: Hazard classification per NFPA 704, threshold quantity, location)*

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Explain the significance of the **NFPA 704 Hazard Diamond** in inventory analysis. What does each of the four colored quadrants represent?

**Model Answer:**

The **NFPA 704 Hazard Diamond** is a standardized placard system used to communicate the intrinsic hazard of materials in storage. It enables rapid hazard recognition by emergency responders.

| Color | Position | Represents | Rating Scale |
|-------|----------|------------|--------------|
| **Blue** | Left | Health Hazard | 0 to 4 |
| **Red** | Top | Flammability | 0 to 4 |
| **Yellow** | Right | Reactivity / Instability | 0 to 4 |
| **White** | Bottom | Special Hazards (OX, W, SA) | Symbol |

A rating of **4** in any category denotes the *most severe* hazard. For KTU 2024, any material with rating $\ge 3$ in any quadrant is treated as high-priority in inventory analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** A bulk-storage terminal contains the following:

| Material | Mass (kg) | Heat of Combustion (kJ/kg) | LEL (%) | TQ (kg) |
|----------|-----------|---------------------------|---------|---------|
| Propane | 18,000 | 46,000 | 2.1 | 4,535 |
| Butane | 12,000 | 45,500 | 1.8 | 4,535 |
| Methanol | 25,000 | 22,000 | 6.0 | 22,680 |

**Tasks:**

**(a)** Compute the **TNT-equivalent explosion mass** assuming a vapor cloud explosion (VCE) with yield factor $\eta = 0.04$ for propane and butane only. Determine which material contributes the highest explosion risk. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **fireball radius and duration** for the most explosive material using the standard empirical correlations. Assume $H_{\text{TNT}} = 4686$ kJ/kg. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — TNT Equivalent Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Apply the TNT Equivalence Formula:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{\text{TNT}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute for Propane:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT, propane}} = \dfrac{0.04 \cdot 18{,}000 \cdot 46{,}000}{4686}
$$

$$
W_{\text{TNT, propane}} = \dfrac{3.312 \times 10^7}{4686}
$$

$$
W_{\text{TNT, propane}} \approx 7067 \text{ kg TNT}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute for Butane:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT, butane}} = \dfrac{0.04 \cdot 12{,}000 \cdot 45{,}500}{4686}
$$

$$
W_{\text{TNT, butane}} = \dfrac{2.184 \times 10^7}{4686}
$$

$$
W_{\text{TNT, butane}} \approx 4661 \text{ kg TNT}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare:**

Propane ($\approx 7067$ kg TNT) > Butane ($\approx 4661$ kg TNT)

**Conclusion:** **Propane** is the most explosive material due to higher inventory and slightly higher heat of combustion. Methanol is excluded due to its lower vapor pressure and slower flame speeds, and is treated as a *fire hazard* not explosion hazard.

---

#### Part (b) — Fireball Sizing (7 Marks)

Using $W_{\text{TNT}} = 7067$ kg for propane.

**Step 1 — Fireball Radius:**

$$
R_{\text{fb}} = 0.39 \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}
$$

$$
W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3} = 7067^{1/3} \approx 19.16
$$

$$
R_{\text{fb}} = 0.39 \cdot 19.16 \approx 7.47 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Fireball Duration:**

$$
t_{\text{fb}} = 0.49 \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/6}
$$

$$
W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/6} = 7067^{1/6} \approx 4.39
$$

$$
t_{\text{fb}} = 0.49 \cdot 4.39 \approx 2.15 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Interpretation:**

The BLEVE fireball from full propane inventory detonation would have a **radius of ≈ 7.47 m** persisting for **≈ 2.15 seconds**. The thermal radiation flux at this distance would be lethal (~$12.5$ kW/m²). The emergency planning distance must extend at least **3× to 5× the fireball radius** ($\approx 22$–37 m) for personnel protection.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** A facility stores the following toxic chemicals. Using **NFPA 704 Hazard Rating** logic and **Threshold Quantity comparison**, classify the facility's regulatory status under EPA RMP. **(7 Marks)**

| Chemical | Mass (kg) | TQ (kg) | Toxicity LC₅₀ (ppm) | NFPA Health |
|----------|-----------|---------|---------------------|-------------|
| Chlorine | 3,000 | 680 | 10 | 4 |
| Ammonia | 8,000 | 4,535 | 4,000 | 3 |
| Sulfur Dioxide | 1,500 | 1,000 | 2,500 | 3 |

**(b)** Compute the **flash fraction** and **instantaneous vapor mass** when a pressurized vessel containing 6,000 kg of liquid ammonia at 25 °C fails catastrophically. Given: $C_p = 4.7$ kJ/(kg·K), $T_b = 239.8$ K, $H_v = 1370$ kJ/kg. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Threshold Sum and Regulatory Classification (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Each Fraction:**

$$
\text{Cl}_2: \dfrac{3000}{680} = 4.41
$$

$$
\text{NH}_3: \dfrac{8000}{4535} = 1.76
$$

$$
\text{SO}_2: \dfrac{1500}{1000} = 1.50
$$

**Step 2 — Sum:**

$$
\sum \dfrac{q_i}{TQ_i} = 4.41 + 1.76 + 1.50 = 7.67
$$

**Step 3 — NFPA Health Rating Assessment:**

All three substances have NFPA Health Rating $\ge 3$ — they qualify as **high-hazard inventory items**.

**Step 4 — Regulatory Conclusion:**

Since $7.67 \gg 1$, the facility is **mandated under EPA RMP** and **OSHA PSM** regulations. The dominant regulated substance is **Chlorine** (ratio = 4.41), requiring the most stringent off-site consequence analysis (worst-case endpoint distance ≥ several km depending on atmospheric stability).

---

#### Part (b) — Flash Fraction of Ammonia (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Apply the Flash Fraction Equation:**

$$
f_v = 1 - \exp\left[-\dfrac{C_p \cdot (T_s - T_b)}{H_v}\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
T_s = 25 + 273 = 298 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_b = 239.8 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_s - T_b = 298 - 239.8 = 58.2 \text{ K}
$$

$$
f_v = 1 - \exp\left[-\dfrac{4.7 \cdot 58.2}{1370}\right]
$$

$$
f_v = 1 - \exp\left[-\dfrac{273.54}{1370}\right]
$$

$$
f_v = 1 - \exp(-0.1997)
$$

$$
f_v = 1 - 0.819 = 0.181 = 18.1\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Vapor Mass:**

$$
m_v = f_v \cdot M = 0.181 \cdot 6000 = 1086 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Conclusion:**

About **1086 kg of ammonia instantly vaporizes** upon vessel rupture, forming a dense (heavier-than-air) toxic cloud. The remaining **4914 kg** forms a cold pool that evaporates slowly, contributing to prolonged toxic exposure downwind. Emergency response must plan for both **flash vapor cloud** and **pool evaporation** scenarios.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Students often mix up kJ/kg and kcal/kg or fail to convert tons to kg. Always carry **SI units (kg, m, K)** throughout.
> 2. **Yield Factor Confusion:** Do not assume $\eta = 1$ (full efficiency). Typical $\eta$ ranges from **1% to 10%** for VCE. Default to **$\eta = 0.04$** if not specified.
> 3. **Threshold Sum Rule:** Even if *no individual* substance exceeds its TQ, the **sum** of fractions $\ge 1$ still triggers regulation. Many students miss this.
> 4. **BLEVE vs VCE:** Do not confuse a *confined vapor cloud explosion* (VCE) with a *boiling liquid expanding vapor explosion* (BLEVE) — the energy models differ.
> 5. **Sign Convention in Flash Fraction:** $f_v$ must be reported as a *decimal*, not a percentage. Convert at the end if needed.
> 6. **NFPA Quadrant Direction:** Blue = Left, Red = Top, Yellow = Right, White = Bottom. Many students reverse these under exam pressure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inventory Analysis** is the **first step** of consequence analysis in industrial safety; without accurate inventory data, no downstream hazard modeling is valid.
- The **total inventory** is the *sum of all individual material masses* within the plant battery limits.
- **Threshold Quantity (TQ)** comparisons determine **PSM/RMP applicability** under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 and EPA 40 CFR Part 68.
- The **synergistic threshold rule** — $\sum \dfrac{q_i}{TQ_i} \ge 1$ — applies even when no individual substance exceeds TQ.
- The **TNT equivalent** formula $W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{\text{TNT}}}$ (with $H_{\text{TNT}} = 4686$ kJ/kg) is the *standard* for explosion energy scaling.
- **Fireball radius** uses $R_{\text{fb}} = 0.39 \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}$ and **duration** uses $t_{\text{fb}} = 0.49 \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/6}$.
- The **flash fraction** for sudden depressurization is $f_v = 1 - \exp\left[-\dfrac{C_p (T_s - T_b)}{H_v}\right]$.
- **NFPA 704** rating system: Blue = Health, Red = Flammability, Yellow = Reactivity, White = Special Hazards.
- High-hazard materials have **NFPA rating $\ge 3$** in any category.
- Inventory analysis feeds into **fire modeling, explosion modeling, and dispersion modeling** — these form Modules 2–3 of the KTU syllabus.
- For **regulatory compliance in India**, refer to the **MSIHC Rules (Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989)** under the Environment Protection Act, 1986.
- Always quote **CAS Numbers** in professional inventory registers; they remove ambiguity from chemical naming.
- **Maximum Credible Release (MCR)** is the worst-case release *assumed for design purposes* and is derived directly from inventory analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET637 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hazard Analysis - Preliminary Hazard Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Hazard Analysis?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hazard Analysis** is a systematic, engineering-based evaluation technique used to **identify, classify, and quantify potential hazards** associated with a system, process, facility, or operation, and to assess the consequences of those hazards on personnel, equipment, environment, and mission continuity.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, *Hazard Analysis* forms the **first technical layer of the broader Process Safety Management (PSM)** and **Loss Prevention** philosophy. It is the foundation upon which subsequent detailed analyses (e.g., HAZOP, FMEA, FTA) are built.

### 1.1.1 Hierarchy of Hazard Analysis Techniques

Hazard analysis is not a single tool but a **family of techniques**, arranged from qualitative to quantitative:

| Level | Technique | Nature |
|------|--------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** | Qualitative, Early |
| 2 | Checklist Analysis | Qualitative |
| 3 | What-If Analysis | Semi-quantitative |
| 4 | HAZOP | Qualitative-Detailed |
| 5 | FMEA / FMECA | Semi-quantitative |
| 6 | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) | Quantitative |
| 7 | Event Tree Analysis (ETA) | Quantitative |

> [!NOTE]
> Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) sits at the **topmost rung** of the analytical ladder — applied when design information is scarce.

---

## 1.2 Definition of Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** is a qualitative risk assessment methodology performed during the **early conceptual or design phase** of a system, facility, or operation to identify potential hazards, assess their severity and probability, and prioritize those needing further detailed study or immediate corrective action.

It originated from **U.S. Department of Defense, U.S. Army Materiel Command (1972)** under Military Standard **MIL-STD-882**, originally called *Hazard Analysis Program*. The technique was later adopted by **NASA**, the **chemical process industry**, **OSHA**, and **nuclear safety regulators** worldwide.

### 1.2.1 Salient Features of PHA

- Performed **before** detailed design drawings are complete.
- Relies on **engineering judgment, historical accident data, and checklists**.
- Produces a **Hazard Log / PHA Register** — a tabular document listing hazards, causes, effects, risk, and recommendations.
- Acts as a **screening tool** to decide which subsystems need **HAZOP, FMEA, or FTA**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pre-Trip Inspection" of a Bus

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Real-World Analogy**

Imagine a long-distance bus trip from **Trivandrum to Delhi**. Before the journey, the driver does a **pre-trip inspection**:

- ✅ Tires inflated? (Mechanical hazard)
- ✅ Brakes working? (Loss-of-control hazard)
- ✅ Fuel sufficient? (Fire/explosion hazard)
- ✅ Lights functional? (Visibility hazard)
- ✅ First-aid kit present? (Medical emergency)

He does **not** dismantle the engine or run finite-element analysis on the chassis. He just lists the *obvious* dangers and the *basic* precautions.

**This is exactly what PHA does for an industrial plant or process.**

It is **NOT** a detailed engineering study. It is the **"pre-trip inspection"** of an industrial facility — performed early, using experience and checklists, to flag the obvious killers before they cause accidents.

> A bus driver who skips the pre-trip inspection may still reach Delhi — but the chance of a blowout on the highway is dramatically higher. Likewise, a process designer who skips PHA may still commission the plant — but with **unidentified latent hazards** that surface as catastrophic incidents later.

---

## 1.4 Primary Objectives of PHA

1. **Identify** all credible hazards associated with the system.
2. **Evaluate** each hazard in terms of severity of consequence and likelihood of occurrence.
3. **Assign** an initial risk level (typically a Risk Code or Risk Rating).
4. **Recommend** preliminary countermeasures, design changes, or further studies.
5. **Document** findings in a Hazard Log for traceability and audit.
6. **Prioritize** hazards for detailed follow-up analyses.

---

## 1.5 Standard Hazard Categories Used in PHA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hazard Categories** classify the *origin* of the hazardous energy or agent. The following eight families are commonly adopted (per MIL-STD-882 & KTU PSM framework):

1. **Mechanical** — Pressure, vibration, moving parts, projectiles.
2. **Electrical** — Shock, arc flash, short-circuit, static discharge.
3. **Thermal** — High temperature, cryogenic burns, steam burns.
4. **Chemical** — Toxic, corrosive, flammable, reactive substances.
5. **Radiation** — Ionizing (X-ray, gamma) and non-ionizing (UV, laser, RF).
6. **Biological** — Bacteria, viruses, biological agents.
7. **Ergonomic / Human Factors** — Repetitive strain, fatigue, slip-trip-fall.
8. **Environmental / External** — Flood, lightning, seismic, dust, wind.

---

## 1.6 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Alignment

| Course Outcome (CO) | Bloom's Level | Mapped Concept |
|----------------------|---------------|-----------------------------------|
| **CO2** | Understand | Explain the purpose and scope of PHA. |
| **CO3** | Apply | Apply PHA to a small process example. |
| **CO4** | Analyze | Compute Risk Rating from Severity × Probability. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 When to Conduct a PHA?

PHA is performed at the following project milestones:

1. **Conceptual Design Stage** — When the basic process flow and equipment list are being defined.
2. **After a Process Change** — When a modification (MOC — Management of Change) is proposed.
3. **Pre-Commissioning Review** — Before final detailed safety studies.
4. **Decommissioning Planning** — Before plant shutdown or demolition.
5. **New Product / Chemical Introduction** — When novel materials are handled.

> [!NOTE]
> PHA should be **revised** whenever there is a design change, accident, or new regulatory requirement. It is a *living document*, not a one-time artifact.

---

## 2.2 PHA Team Composition

| Role | Responsibility |
|----------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| Team Leader | Facilitates analysis, ensures method is followed. |
| Process Engineer | Provides process flow & operating conditions. |
| Safety Engineer | Identifies hazard mechanisms. |
| Maintenance Engineer | Highlights equipment failure modes. |
| Operations Supervisor | Provides field-experience insights. |
| Subject Matter Expert | Brings specialized knowledge (instrumentation, etc.). |

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Procedure of PHA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Seven-Step PHA Procedure (Standard per MIL-STD-882 & KTU framework):**

1. **Define the System / Scope of Study**
   - Draw a **System Block Diagram**.
   - List operating modes: *Startup, Normal Operation, Shutdown, Emergency, Maintenance*.

2. **Review Past Experience**
   - Industry accident databases (e.g., **eMARS, CSB, OSHA IMIS**).
   - Internal near-miss reports.
   - Vendor / supplier failure data.

3. **Identify Potential Hazards**
   - Brainstorming, checklist review, hazard categories (Section 1.5).
   - For each subsystem: ask *"What can go wrong?"*

4. **Identify Causal Scenarios (Initiating Events)**
   - Mechanical failure, human error, external event, process deviation.

5. **Evaluate Effects / Consequences**
   - What happens to personnel, equipment, environment, production?

6. **Determine Severity and Probability Levels**
   - Assign severity category (I–IV) and probability category (A–E).
   - Compute **Risk Level (RL)**.

7. **Recommend Corrective Actions**
   - Engineering controls (preferred — hierarchy of controls).
   - Administrative controls.
   - PPE (last resort).
   - Flag for further analysis if RL is unacceptable.

---

## 2.4 Severity Classification (Standard PHA Scale)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Severity Levels — Category I to IV** (per MIL-STD-882 System Safety Program):

| Level | Category | Consequence Description |
|-------|----------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| **I** | **Catastrophic** | Death, permanent disability, or loss of entire system. > **$1M loss.** |
| **II** | **Critical** | Severe injury, occupational illness, major system damage. |
| **III** | **Marginal** | Minor injury, reversible illness, minor system damage. |
| **IV** | **Negligible** | First-aid-only injury, no system damage. |

---

## 2.5 Probability Classification (Standard PHA Scale)

| Level | Category | Description | Quantitative Range |
|-------|---------------|-------------------------------------------|------------------|
| **A** | **Frequent** | Likely to occur often. | ≥ 1×10⁻¹ per year |
| **B** | **Probable** | Will occur several times. | 1×10⁻² to 1×10⁻¹ |
| **C** | **Occasional** | Likely to occur sometime. | 1×10⁻³ to 1×10⁻² |
| **D** | **Remote** | Unlikely but possible. | 1×10⁻⁶ to 1×10⁻³ |
| **E** | **Improbable** | So unlikely, assumed impossible. | < 1×10⁻⁶ |

---

## 2.6 The Risk Assessment Matrix (RAM)

The **Risk Matrix** cross-tabulates Severity (I–IV) and Probability (A–E) to yield a **Risk Level Code (RL)**. It is the single most examined concept in KTU papers on PHA.

$$
\text{Risk Level (RL)} = f(\text{Severity}, \text{Probability})
$$

> Where $f$ is a lookup mapping defined by the standard RAM table.

### 2.6.1 Standard 5×4 Risk Matrix

| Severity ↓ \\ Probability → | **A** Frequent | **B** Probable | **C** Occasional | **D** Remote | **E** Improbable |
|-----------------------------|----------------|----------------|------------------|--------------|------------------|
| **I – Catastrophic** | **1A** | **1B** | **2** | **3** | **4** |
| **II – Critical** | **1B** | **2** | **3** | **4** | **5** |
| **III – Marginal** | **2** | **3** | **4** | **5** | **5** |
| **IV – Negligible** | **3** | **4** | **5** | **5** | **5** |

### 2.6.2 Risk Level Interpretation

| Risk Code | Action Required |
|----------|----------------------------------------------------------------|
| **1A / 1B** | **Unacceptable** — Halt operation, mandatory immediate corrective action. |
| **2** | **Undesirable** — Senior management decision required; mitigation mandatory. |
| **3** | **Acceptable with controls** — Implement corrective action plan with timeline. |
| **4** | **Acceptable** — Monitor, document, periodic review. |
| **5** | **Acceptable** — No action required, log for traceability. |

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for PHA — KTU Board Examination:**

| S.No | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes |
|------|--------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | Risk Rating (Basic) | $RR = S \times P$ | Dimensionless index code |
| 2 | Weighted Risk Score | $RR_w = w_S \cdot S + w_P \cdot P$ | Where $w_S, w_P$ are weights |
| 3 | Occurrence Frequency | $f = \dfrac{N}{T}$ | Failures per unit time (year⁻¹) |
| 4 | Failure Rate | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | hour⁻¹ or year⁻¹ |
| 5 | Probability of Event | $P = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | For non-repairable over time $t$ |
| 6 | Hazard Rate Index | $H = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i \cdot P_i$ | Total system risk |
| 7 | Acceptable Risk Threshold | $RR \leq RR_{threshold}$ | Per company policy |

> Where:
> - $S$ = Severity category (1–4)
> - $P$ = Probability category (1–5)
> - $\lambda$ = Failure rate
> - MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures
> - $t$ = Exposure / mission time

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

PHA is the **mandatory first layer** in:

- 🛢️ **Oil & Gas Industry** — Pre-FEED (Front-End Engineering Design) safety screening.
- ⚗️ **Chemical Plants** — OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 PSM compliance.
- ✈️ **Aerospace** — NASA NPR 8705.2C Human-Rating Certification.
- 🚢 **Marine / Naval** — SOLAS Convention preliminary safety review.
- 🏗️ **Construction** — CDM Regulations 2015 (UK), MoEFCC EIA (India).

In **KTU Industrial Safety Engineering**, PHA is the gateway technique — students are expected to be able to **fill a PHA worksheet** for a simple process like a *steam boiler*, *chlorine storage*, or *ammonia refrigeration unit*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Risk Rating Formula

The classical **Risk Rating (RR)** in PHA is defined as the **product of severity and probability categorical indices**.

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Define Severity Index (S)

Severity is mapped from a categorical description (I–IV) to a numerical index:

$$
S = \begin{cases} 4 & \text{if Severity = I (Catastrophic)} \\ 3 & \text{if Severity = II (Critical)} \\ 2 & \text{if Severity = III (Marginal)} \\ 1 & \text{if Severity = IV (Negligible)} \end{cases}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Define Probability Index (P)

Probability is mapped from a categorical description (A–E) to a numerical index:

$$
P = \begin{cases} 5 & \text{if Probability = A (Frequent)} \\ 4 & \text{if Probability = B (Probable)} \\ 3 & \text{if Probability = C (Occasional)} \\ 2 & \text{if Probability = D (Remote)} \\ 1 & \text{if Probability = E (Improbable)} \end{cases}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Compute Risk Rating

$$
\boxed{\; RR = S \times P \;}
$$

Where $RR$ ranges from **1 (lowest risk)** to **20 (highest risk)**.

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Map RR to Decision Category

| RR Range | Decision |
|-----------|-------------------------|
| 15 – 20 | **Unacceptable** — Stop. |
| 10 – 14 | **Undesirable** — Mitigate. |
| 5 – 9 | **Acceptable with controls.** |
| 1 – 4 | **Acceptable.** |

> [!NOTE]
> This numeric formula is **an alternative** to the qualitative 5×4 RAM table. Some industries prefer the matrix; others prefer the multiplicative index. Both are accepted in KTU answers.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — LPG Storage Tank PHA

**Scenario**: A small-scale LPG bottling plant stores 2000 kg of LPG in a horizontal bullet tank at 8 bar(g) pressure. Apply PHA to identify and rank three credible hazards.

### 3.2.1 Hazard 1 — Large Leak from Flange Gasket Failure

| Step | Analysis |
|------|---------|
| **Cause** | Gasket degradation, over-torque, vibration fatigue. |
| **Consequence** | Flammable gas cloud, possible jet fire / VCE. |
| **Severity** | I — Catastrophic (potential fatality, plant damage). |
| **Probability** | C — Occasional (gaskets fail every 3–5 years if not maintained). |
| **Risk Code** | I-C → **Risk Level 2** (from RAM) |
| **Numeric RR** | $RR = 4 \times 3 = 12$ → Undesirable |
| **Recommendation** | Install **flame arrestors**, **gas detectors**, **ESD valves**, **double-block-and-bleed** isolation. Periodic gasket replacement as per **MOC** protocol. |

### 3.2.2 Hazard 2 — Operator Exposure to LPG During Hose Decoupling

| Step | Analysis |
|------|---------|
| **Cause** | Manual decoupling without proper bleed-down. |
| **Consequence** | Cold burn (LPG auto-refrigeration), frostbite. |
| **Severity** | II — Critical (severe injury, hospitalization). |
| **Probability** | B — Probable (occurs annually in poorly trained plants). |
| **Risk Code** | II-B → **Risk Level 2** |
| **Numeric RR** | $RR = 3 \times 4 = 12$ |
| **Recommendation** | Provide **PPE (cryogenic gloves)**, **training**, **automatic dry-disconnect couplings**, **bleed-down valves**. |

### 3.2.3 Hazard 3 — Static Electricity Build-up During Filling

| Step | Analysis |
|------|---------|
| **Cause** | Inadequate grounding of the tanker truck. |
| **Consequence** | Static discharge → ignition of LPG vapour. |
| **Severity** | I — Catastrophic. |
| **Probability** | D — Remote (rare if grounding is verified). |
| **Risk Code** | I-D → **Risk Level 3** |
| **Numeric RR** | $RR = 4 \times 2 = 8$ → Acceptable with controls |
| **Recommendation** | Mandate **bonding & grounding cables**, **earth continuity monitoring**, **filling-rate control (≤ 1 m/s)** to limit static generation. |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Risk Threshold Calculation

Suppose a company policy mandates that **acceptable RR must be ≤ 6**. Given a hazard with severity index $S = 3$ and probability index $P = 4$:

**Step 1** — Compute current Risk Rating:

$$
RR_{current} = S \times P = 3 \times 4 = 12
$$

**Step 2** — Compare with threshold:

$$
RR_{current} = 12 \; > \; RR_{threshold} = 6
$$

**Step 3** — Decision: The hazard is **unacceptable**. Mitigation is mandatory.

**Step 4** — After mitigation, suppose the probability is reduced from $P=4$ (Probable) to $P=2$ (Remote):

$$
RR_{mitigated} = S \times P_{new} = 3 \times 2 = 6
$$

**Step 5** — Re-check:

$$
RR_{mitigated} = 6 \;\leq\; RR_{threshold} = 6 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 6** — The hazard is now **acceptable with controls**, provided the mitigation measures (e.g., redundant safety interlocks) are operational and audited.

---

## 3.4 Compound Hazard Index for a Multi-Hazard System

For a system with $n$ independent hazards, the **total system hazard index** $H_{sys}$ is:

$$
H_{sys} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} RR_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i \cdot P_i
$$

**Example** — A chemical reactor with three identified hazards:

| Hazard i | $S_i$ | $P_i$ | $RR_i = S_i \cdot P_i$ |
|----------|------|------|------------------------|
| Runaway reaction | 4 | 3 | 12 |
| Overpressure | 3 | 4 | 12 |
| Corrosion | 2 | 2 | 4 |
| **Total** | — | — | **$H_{sys}$ = 28** |

$$
H_{sys} = 12 + 12 + 4 = 28
$$

This single number can be benchmarked against industry-acceptable thresholds to determine whether the **entire reactor system** is within tolerable risk limits.

---

## 3.5 Probability Calculation Using Failure Rate (Quantitative Enhancement)

When historical data is available, the probability index can be **refined** from a quantitative failure rate.

**Given**: A pressure relief valve has a failure rate $\lambda = 0.005$ per year. The plant mission time is $t = 5$ years.

**Step 1** — Probability of failure within mission time (assuming exponential distribution for non-repairable components):

$$
P(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 2** — Substitute values:

$$
P(5) = 1 - e^{-(0.005)(5)} = 1 - e^{-0.025}
$$

**Step 3** — Evaluate exponential:

$$
e^{-0.025} \approx 0.9753
$$

**Step 4** — Final probability:

$$
P(5) = 1 - 0.9753 = 0.0247 \;\; \approx \; 2.47\%
$$

**Step 5** — Classification:

Since $0.0247$ lies in the range $1 \times 10^{-3}$ to $1 \times 10^{-2}$, this maps to **Probability Level C — Occasional**.

This bridges the gap between **purely qualitative PHA** and **fully quantitative FTA**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
    P9 -.feedback.-> P2
```
